Pixel values that were clustered between 180–250 (a narrow band of 70 units) are now spread across 127–245 (a wider band of 118 units). This enhanced contrast makes **details in the bright regions more discernible**, addressing the original problem.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to Normalize:** The #1 error. Always divide $r$ by $(L-1)$ before raising to $\gamma$. If you skip this, your output values will be astronomically wrong and you will lose **3-4 marks instantly**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\gamma < 1$ with $\gamma > 1$ Effects:** Students often swap brightening and darkening. Memory aid: **"Less than one = Lift up = Lighter"**. The exponent is small, so the curve hugs the top of the plot.
>
> 3. **Not Rounding the Output:** For digital images, fractional pixel values must be rounded to the nearest integer. A floating-point answer in a numerical problem will be marked **partially incorrect** even if the formula is right.
>
> 4. **Omitting the Block Diagram:** In 7-mark theory questions, a labeled block diagram is worth **2-3 marks by itself**. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.
>
> 5. **Not Mentioning Applications:** Even in definition-type questions, mentioning **one or two applications** (display gamma, medical imaging) can fetch you the extra mark needed to cross the grade boundary.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation:** $s = c \cdot r^{\gamma}$ with $c, \gamma > 0$.
- **Three Regimes:** $\gamma < 1$ (brighten), $\gamma = 1$ (identity), $\gamma > 1$ (darken).
- **Standard Constant:** $c = L-1$ (e.g., 255 for 8-bit images) is the convention.
- **Normalization is Mandatory:** Always normalize $r$ to $[0, 1]$ before applying the power.
- **Endpoint Preservation:** $r = 0 \Rightarrow s = 0$ and $r = L-1 \Rightarrow s = L-1$ (for $c = L-1$).
- **Gamma Correction is Its Killer App:** Power law is the mathematical heart of every display gamma system ($\gamma \approx 2.2$).
- **Monotonic & Continuous:** No pixel ranking is changed — order is preserved.
- **Related Transformations:** Log transformation is the limit case as $\gamma \to 0$; identity is $\gamma = 1$.
- **Practical Tip:** When given a numerical problem, **always write the formula first**, then normalize, then compute each unique pixel value, then construct the output matrix, then comment on the visual effect.
- **Common $\gamma$ Values to Memorize:** 0.4, 0.67, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.2, 2.5, 3.0.
- **Block Diagram is Mandatory** in 7-mark theory answers — it is your safety net for partial credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piecewise-Linear Transformation Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piecewise-Linear Transformation Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of Spatial Domain Image Processing, a **Piecewise-Linear Transformation Function** is a class of gray-level mapping functions, denoted as $T(r)$, where the input-to-output mapping relationship is divided into multiple contiguous intervals over the intensity range $[0, L-1]$, and within each interval, the function behaves as a distinct linear (straight-line) segment. The endpoints of these linear segments are called **breakpoints** or **control points**, typically expressed as coordinate pairs $(r_k, s_k)$, where $r_k$ is the input intensity and $s_k$ is the corresponding mapped output intensity.

Formally, for a digital image with $L$ discrete gray levels (for an 8-bit image, $L = 256$ so intensities range from 0 to 255), a piecewise-linear transformation is defined as:

$$
s = T(r) = 
\begin{cases}
T_1(r) & \text{if } r \in [r_0, r_1] \\
T_2(r) & \text{if } r \in [r_1, r_2] \\
\vdots & \\
T_n(r) & \text{if } r \in [r_{n-1}, r_n]
\end{cases}
$$

where each $T_k(r)$ is a linear function of the form $T_k(r) = a_k \cdot r + b_k$, and the overall function is **continuous** (though not necessarily differentiable) at the breakpoints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Piecewise-linear transformations are fundamental tools for **contrast manipulation** and **feature extraction** in spatial domain filtering. They are particularly exam-relevant because they directly address the difference between *low-contrast* and *high-contrast* images, and their graphical representation is a frequent question in KTU board examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Glossary:**
> - **$L$**: Total number of gray levels (typically 256 for 8-bit images).
> - **$r$**: Input pixel intensity (original image).
> - **$s$**: Output pixel intensity (transformed image).
> - **Control Points $(r_k, s_k)$**: The user-defined coordinates that shape the transformation curve.
> - **Slope $a_k$**: Determines contrast expansion (slope > 1) or compression (slope < 1) in a given interval.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are adjusting the **brightness and contrast** sliders on your television or mobile phone's photo editor. The brightness slider linearly shifts all intensity values up or down (a simple linear transformation), but the contrast slider works in a more sophisticated way: it **expands** the spread of intensities in some range (like mid-tones) while **compressing** others (like the very dark or very bright regions).

A piecewise-linear transformation is essentially a **graphical "volume mixer" for pixel intensities**. Think of a graphic equalizer on a music player:

- Just as a graphic equalizer has **multiple slider bands** (bass, mid, treble), each controlling a different frequency range,
- A piecewise-linear transformation has **multiple linear segments**, each controlling how a different range of gray levels gets mapped.
- Just as boosting the bass doesn't affect the treble, transforming the dark pixel range doesn't affect the bright pixel range.

The shape of the transformation curve acts as a "lens" through which the histogram of the image is "reshaped." If the input gray levels are bunched up in a narrow range, we can "stretch" that range (contrast stretching) to span the full output range, dramatically improving visual clarity.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters are critical in this topic:

- **$L = 256$** (Number of gray levels in an 8-bit image, ISO/IEC standard)
- **Dynamic Range**: $[0, L-1] = [0, 255]$ for standard 8-bit grayscale images
- **Normalization Range**: $[0, 1]$ when intensities are normalized for mathematical analysis
- **Bit Depth**: 8 bits per pixel (bpp) for standard grayscale, 24 bpp for RGB color
- **Slope Constraint**: For a strictly contrast-enhancing transformation, all slopes must satisfy $a_k \geq 1$ in the expansion region; for an identity (no-change) transformation, all slopes equal 1 and all breakpoints lie on the line $s = r$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** A transformation that maps the diagonal line $s = r$ is called the **identity transformation** — it produces no change in the image. Any deviation from this diagonal constitutes image enhancement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Piecewise-Linear Transformations

KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes three principal piecewise-linear transformations:

### 2.1.1 Contrast Stretching

This is the most general and versatile form. Given a poorly contrasted image whose gray levels are concentrated in a narrow range $[r_1, r_2]$, the goal is to **stretch** this range to span the full dynamic range $[0, L-1]$.

The piecewise-linear function is defined by **three segments** joined at two control points $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$:

**Segment 1** (Dark region, $r_0 \leq r \leq r_1$):
The mapping goes from $(0, 0)$ to $(r_1, s_1)$. This region is typically **compressed** to keep dark pixels dark.

**Segment 2** (Region of interest, $r_1 < r \leq r_2$):
The mapping goes from $(r_1, s_1)$ to $(r_2, s_2)$. This is the **stretched** region where contrast is enhanced.

**Segment 3** (Bright region, $r_2 < r \leq L-1$):
The mapping goes from $(r_2, s_2)$ to $(L-1, L-1)$. This region is also **compressed** to keep bright pixels bright.

### 2.1.2 Special Cases of Contrast Stretching

When $(r_1, s_1) = (r_{min}, 0)$ and $(r_2, s_2) = (r_{max}, L-1)$, where $r_{min}$ and $r_{max}$ are the actual minimum and maximum intensities in the image, this is called **Normalization Stretch** or **Min-Max Contrast Stretching**. It produces the maximum possible contrast enhancement.

When $s_1 = 0$ and $s_2 = L-1$ (regardless of $r_1, r_2$), it's called **Clipping-based Contrast Stretching**.

### 2.1.3 Intensity-Level Slicing

Used to **isolate a specific range of intensities** (e.g., highlighting tumors, defects, or specific features).

**Approach A (With background)**: Preserves all gray levels, but brightens the range $[A, B]$ to a high value:
$$
s = T(r) = 
\begin{cases}
L-1 & \text{if } A \leq r \leq B \\
r & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Approach B (Without background)**: Darkens everything outside the range $[A, B]$:
$$
s = T(r) = 
\begin{cases}
L-1 & \text{if } A \leq r \leq B \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.1.4 Bit-Plane Slicing

A grayscale image with bit-depth $n$ (e.g., $n=8$) can be decomposed into $n$ binary images (bit-planes). The $k$-th bit-plane ($k = 0, 1, ..., 7$) is obtained by extracting the $k$-th bit of every pixel. Mathematically, for an 8-bit pixel value $p$:

$$
p = \sum_{k=0}^{7} b_k \cdot 2^k
$$

where $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $k$-th bit. The $k$-th bit-plane image $B_k(x,y)$ is:
$$
B_k(x,y) = b_k(x,y) \cdot (L-1)
$$

The most significant bit (MSB, $k=7$) plane contains the most visually significant information; the least significant bit (LSB, $k=0$) plane contains subtle, often noise-like detail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bit-Plane Slicing Matters in KTU Context:**
> - **Image Compression**: Lower-order bit-planes can be discarded with minimal perceptual loss.
> - **Watermarking**: Hiding data in LSB planes (steganography).
> - **Image Analysis**: Identifying bit-planes that contribute most to image structure.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Transformation Type | Input Condition | Mathematical Formula | Output Range | Key Use Case |
|---|---------------------|------------------|----------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Segment 1 (Dark) | $0 \leq r \leq r_1$ | $s = \dfrac{s_1}{r_1} \cdot r$ | $[0, s_1]$ | Dark region compression |
| 2 | Segment 2 (Stretch) | $r_1 < r \leq r_2$ | $s = \dfrac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1) + s_1$ | $[s_1, s_2]$ | Contrast enhancement |
| 3 | Segment 3 (Bright) | $r_2 < r \leq L-1$ | $s = \dfrac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2} \cdot (r - r_2) + s_2$ | $[s_2, L-1]$ | Bright region compression |
| 4 | Min-Max Stretch | $r_{min} \leq r \leq r_{max}$ | $s = \dfrac{r - r_{min}}{r_{max} - r_{min}} \cdot (L-1)$ | $[0, L-1]$ | Full normalization |
| 5 | Slicing (with bg) | $A \leq r \leq B$ | $s = L-1$, else $s = r$ | $[0, L-1]$ | Feature highlighting |
| 6 | Slicing (no bg) | $A \leq r \leq B$ | $s = L-1$, else $s = 0$ | $\{0, L-1\}$ | Binary isolation |
| 7 | Bit-plane $k$ | $b_k$ is $k$-th bit | $B_k = b_k \cdot (L-1)$ | $\{0, L-1\}$ | Compression, analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Examiner Note:** In the exam, when writing formulas, do NOT use the vertical pipe `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables — always write $\vert x \vert$ or use $\min / \max$ notation. This will be enforced strictly.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

| Application Domain | Specific Use of Piecewise-Linear Transforms |
|--------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Medical Imaging** | Enhancing tumors, fractures, or lesions hidden in low-contrast X-rays, CT, or MRI scans |
| **Satellite Imaging** | Stretching the dynamic range of multispectral images to reveal terrain features |
| **Industrial Inspection** | Detecting surface defects (cracks, scratches) by intensity-level slicing |
| **Forensic Analysis** | Enhancing CCTV footage, fingerprint analysis, surveillance imagery |
| **Astronomy** | Bringing out faint stars or galaxies buried in noisy background |
| **Watermarking/Steganography** | Embedding hidden information in LSB bit-planes |
| **Document Processing** | Improving readability of faded historical manuscripts |

## 2.4 Theoretical "Why" Behind the Math

**Why does contrast stretching work?**
- The human visual system perceives contrast logarithmically (Weber-Fechner law). A small absolute change in a dark region is more perceptible than the same change in a bright region.
- Piecewise-linear functions allow us to allocate the limited $L = 256$ gray levels to the regions where they matter most perceptually, based on the histogram distribution of the actual image.

**Why is bit-plane slicing useful?**
- An image's information is non-uniformly distributed across its bit-planes. The MSB plane ($k=7$) contains structural information (edges, major shapes), while LSB planes contain subtle texture and noise. This decomposition enables targeted analysis and compression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Contrast Stretching Formulas

We want to construct a piecewise-linear function $T(r)$ that maps three input intervals to three output intervals. The function is constrained to pass through the points:
- $(0, 0)$ (origin)
- $(r_1, s_1)$ (first breakpoint)
- $(r_2, s_2)$ (second breakpoint)
- $(L-1, L-1)$ (top-right corner)

### Step 1: Derive the Equation for Segment 1 (Dark Region)

Segment 1 connects $(0, 0)$ to $(r_1, s_1)$.

Using the two-point form of a straight line: $\dfrac{s - s_0}{r - r_0} = \dfrac{s_1 - s_0}{r_1 - r_0}$.

Substituting $(s_0, r_0) = (0, 0)$:

$$
\frac{s - 0}{r - 0} = \frac{s_1 - 0}{r_1 - 0}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
s \cdot r_1 = r \cdot s_1
$$

Solving for $s$:

$$
s = \frac{s_1}{r_1} \cdot r
$$

This is valid for $0 \leq r \leq r_1$. Note that if $r_1 = 0$ (i.e., the first breakpoint is at the origin), this segment degenerates to a single point.

### Step 2: Derive the Equation for Segment 2 (Stretched Region)

Segment 2 connects $(r_1, s_1)$ to $(r_2, s_2)$.

Using the two-point form:

$$
\frac{s - s_1}{r - r_1} = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}
$$

Solving for $s$:

$$
s - s_1 = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1)
$$

Therefore:

$$
s = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1) + s_1
$$

This is valid for $r_1 \leq r \leq r_2$. The **slope** of this segment is $a_2 = \dfrac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}$.

For contrast stretching to be effective, we need $a_2 > 1$, which requires $(s_2 - s_1) > (r_2 - r_1)$.

### Step 3: Derive the Equation for Segment 3 (Bright Region)

Segment 3 connects $(r_2, s_2)$ to $(L-1, L-1)$.

Using the two-point form:

$$
\frac{s - s_2}{r - r_2} = \frac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2}
$$

Solving for $s$:

$$
s = \frac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2} \cdot (r - r_2) + s_2
$$

This is valid for $r_2 \leq r \leq L-1$. The slope is $a_3 = \dfrac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2}$.

### Step 4: Assemble the Complete Piecewise Function

$$
s = T(r) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{s_1}{r_1} \cdot r & \text{if } 0 \leq r \leq r_1 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1) + s_1 & \text{if } r_1 < r \leq r_2 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2} \cdot (r - r_2) + s_2 & \text{if } r_2 < r \leq L-1
\end{cases}
$$

This is the **generalized contrast stretching formula** that KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive or apply.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Contrast Stretching Application

**Problem Statement:**
Consider a 4-bit image ($L = 16$, so $L-1 = 15$) with gray levels concentrated in the range $[3, 10]$. Apply a piecewise-linear contrast stretching transformation using control points $(r_1, s_1) = (3, 0)$ and $(r_2, s_2) = (10, 15)$. Compute the output intensities for input values $r = 0, 3, 5, 7, 10, 12, 15$.

### Step 1: Identify the Parameters
- $L = 16$, $L-1 = 15$
- $r_1 = 3$, $s_1 = 0$
- $r_2 = 10$, $s_2 = 15$

### Step 2: Derive Segment 1 (for $0 \leq r \leq 3$)
Using $s = \dfrac{s_1}{r_1} \cdot r = \dfrac{0}{3} \cdot r = 0$.

So all inputs in $[0, 3]$ map to $s = 0$.

**Result**: $r = 0 \Rightarrow s = 0$; $r = 3 \Rightarrow s = 0$.

### Step 3: Derive Segment 2 (for $3 < r \leq 10$)
Using $s = \dfrac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1) + s_1 = \dfrac{15 - 0}{10 - 3} \cdot (r - 3) + 0$.

Simplifying the slope:
$$
\text{slope} = \frac{15}{7} \approx 2.143
$$

So:
$$
s = \frac{15}{7} \cdot (r - 3)
$$

- $r = 5 \Rightarrow s = \dfrac{15}{7} \cdot 2 = \dfrac{30}{7} \approx 4.29$. Rounded to nearest integer: $s = 4$.
- $r = 7 \Rightarrow s = \dfrac{15}{7} \cdot 4 = \dfrac{60}{7} \approx 8.57$. Rounded: $s = 9$.
- $r = 10 \Rightarrow s = \dfrac{15}{7} \cdot 7 = 15$.

### Step 4: Derive Segment 3 (for $10 < r \leq 15$)
Using $s = \dfrac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2} \cdot (r - r_2) + s_2 = \dfrac{15 - 15}{15 - 10} \cdot (r - 10) + 15 = 0 \cdot (r - 10) + 15 = 15$.

So all inputs in $[11, 15]$ map to $s = 15$.

**Result**: $r = 12 \Rightarrow s = 15$; $r = 15 \Rightarrow s = 15$.

### Step 5: Tabulated Output

| Input $r$ | Output $s$ | Segment Used |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| 0 | 0 | Segment 1 |
| 3 | 0 | Segment 1 (boundary) |
| 5 | 4 | Segment 2 |
| 7 | 9 | Segment 2 |
| 10 | 15 | Segment 2 (boundary) |
| 12 | 15 | Segment 3 |
| 15 | 15 | Segment 3 |

### Step 6: Interpretation

The input range $[3, 10]$ (width 7) has been stretched to output range $[0, 15]$ (width 15). The stretching factor is $\dfrac{15}{7} \approx 2.143$, meaning contrast in this range is more than doubled. All dark values below 3 collapse to 0 (black), and all bright values above 10 saturate to 15 (white). This is a **clipping-based contrast stretch**.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Bit-Plane Extraction

**Problem Statement:**
A pixel in an 8-bit grayscale image has the value $p = 173$. Decompose this value into its 8 bit-planes and reconstruct it.

### Step 1: Convert to Binary
$173$ in binary: $173 = 128 + 32 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 10101101_2$

So $b_7 = 1, b_6 = 0, b_5 = 1, b_4 = 0, b_3 = 1, b_2 = 1, b_1 = 0, b_0 = 1$.

### Step 2: Bit-Plane Intensities (multiplying each bit by 255 for display)
- Plane 7 (MSB): $1 \cdot 255 = 255$
- Plane 6: $0 \cdot 255 = 0$
- Plane 5: $1 \cdot 255 = 255$
- Plane 4: $0 \cdot 255 = 0$
- Plane 3: $1 \cdot 255 = 255$
- Plane 2: $1 \cdot 255 = 255$
- Plane 1: $0 \cdot 255 = 0$
- Plane 0 (LSB): $1 \cdot 255 = 255$

### Step 3: Verify Reconstruction
$$
p = b_7 \cdot 2^7 + b_6 \cdot 2^6 + ... + b_0 \cdot 2^0
$$

$$
p = (1)(128) + (0)(64) + (1)(32) + (0)(16) + (1)(8) + (1)(4) + (0)(2) + (1)(1) = 173 \checkmark
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Contrast Stretching | Intensity Slicing | Bit-Plane Slicing |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Number of segments | 3 (typically) | 2 or 3 | $n$ (equals bit-depth) |
| Output type | Continuous gray | Gray or binary | Binary |
| Preserves structure? | Yes | Depends on variant | Yes (decomposed) |
| Computation cost | Low (per-pixel) | Very low | Moderate (bit extraction) |
| Primary use | Contrast enhancement | Feature highlighting | Analysis & compression |
| Lossy? | No | No | No (lossless decomposition) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Contrast Stretching Transformation Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Gray Level r] --> B{Determine r range}
    B -->|0 to r1| C[Segment 1: Compress Dark]
    B -->|r1 to r2| D[Segment 2: Stretch Mid]
    B -->|r2 to L-1| E[Segment 3: Compress Bright]
    C --> F[Apply Linear Map s = s1 slash r1 times r]
    D --> G[Apply Linear Map s = slope times r minus r1 plus s1]
    E --> H[Apply Linear Map s = slope times r minus r2 plus s2]
    F --> I[Output Gray Level s]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Piecewise-Linear Function Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph InputDomain["Input Intensity Domain 0 to L-1"]
        I1[Dark Region: 0 to r1]
        I2[Mid Region: r1 to r2]
        I3[Bright Region: r2 to L-1]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingBlocks["Linear Mapping Blocks"]
        P1[Block T1: slope equals s1 over r1]
        P2[Block T2: slope equals s2 minus s1 over r2 minus r1]
        P3[Block T3: slope equals L-1 minus s2 over L-1 minus r2]
    end
    subgraph OutputDomain["Output Intensity Domain 0 to L-1"]
        O1[Range: 0 to s1]
        O2[Range: s1 to s2 STRETCHED]
        O3[Range: s2 to L-1]
    end
    I1 --> P1 --> O1
    I2 --> P2 --> O2
    I3 --> P3 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Bit-Plane Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[8-bit Grayscale Image] --> B[Read Pixel Value p]
    B --> C[Convert p to Binary: 8 bits]
    C --> D1[Bit 7 MSB Plane]
    C --> D2[Bit 6 Plane]
    C --> D3[Bit 5 Plane]
    C --> D4[Bit 4 Plane]
    C --> D5[Bit 3 Plane]
    C --> D6[Bit 2 Plane]
    C --> D7[Bit 1 Plane]
    C --> D8[Bit 0 LSB Plane]
    D1 --> E1[Multiply by 255 for display]
    D2 --> E2[Multiply by 255 for display]
    D3 --> E3[Multiply by 255 for display]
    D4 --> E4[Multiply by 255 for display]
    D5 --> E5[Multiply by 255 for display]
    D6 --> E6[Multiply by 255 for display]
    D7 --> E7[Multiply by 255 for display]
    D8 --> E8[Multiply by 255 for display]
    E1 --> F[Visualize 8 Binary Bit-Plane Images]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    E7 --> F
    E8 --> F
```

## 4.4 Intensity Level Slicing Variants

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Original Image with Range 0 to 255] --> B{Target Range A to B}
    B --> C1[Approach 1: With Background]
    B --> C2[Approach 2: Without Background]
    C1 --> D1[Pixels in A to B mapped to 255 White]
    C1 --> D2[Pixels outside A to B kept as r Original]
    C2 --> E1[Pixels in A to B mapped to 255 White]
    C2 --> E2[Pixels outside A to B mapped to 0 Black]
    D1 --> F[Output Image Variant 1]
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> G[Output Image Variant 2]
    E2 --> G
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When asked to "draw" a transformation function in the exam, always label the **control points** $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$, the **axes** ($r$ horizontal, $s$ vertical), the **diagonal identity line** $s = r$, and clearly mark which segments represent stretching vs. compression.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a piecewise-linear transformation function. How does it differ from a simple linear (identity) transformation in terms of its mathematical structure and effect on image contrast?**

**Model Answer:**

A **piecewise-linear transformation function** is a gray-level mapping function $T(r)$ that divides the input intensity range $[0, L-1]$ into multiple sub-intervals using breakpoints $(r_k, s_k)$, applying a different linear equation $T_k(r) = a_k r + b_k$ within each interval. The function is continuous at the breakpoints.

In contrast, a **simple linear (identity) transformation** is described by a single equation $s = T(r) = ar + b$ applied uniformly across the entire range. When $a = 1$ and $b = 0$, this is the identity $s = r$ that produces no change.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Identity Linear | Piecewise-Linear |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Number of equations | 1 | $\geq 2$ |
| Control over sub-ranges | None | Yes, full control |
| Selective stretching | Impossible | Possible |
| Complexity | Low | Higher |
| Image enhancement use | Limited (brightness shift) | Extensive (contrast manipulation) |

The piecewise-linear approach allows us to **stretch** the gray-level range in regions where the image has poor contrast while **compressing** regions that already have sufficient detail, leading to a much more powerful and targeted enhancement.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is bit-plane slicing? Explain its significance in image compression with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Bit-plane slicing** is a piecewise-linear transformation technique (or rather, decomposition technique) that decomposes a grayscale image of $n$-bit depth into $n$ binary images, called bit-planes. The $k$-th bit-plane ($k = 0, 1, ..., n-1$) is obtained by extracting the $k$-th bit of every pixel's binary representation.

**Mathematical Formulation:**
For a pixel with value $p$:
$$
p = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k
$$

The $k$-th bit-plane image is:
$$
B_k(x, y) = b_k(x, y) \cdot (L-1)
$$

**Significance in Image Compression:**

The higher-order bit-planes (e.g., MSB plane 7) contain the **major structural and contrast information** of the image (edges, dominant features), while the lower-order bit-planes (e.g., LSB plane 0) contain **subtle details and noise**.

**Example:** For an 8-bit image, if we keep only bit-planes 7, 6, and 5 (discarding planes 0-4), we can still reconstruct a recognizable version of the image using only 3 bits per pixel instead of 8 — a **compression ratio of 8:3 ≈ 2.67:1** with minimal perceptual loss.

**Additional applications:** Image steganography (hiding data in LSBs), image authentication, and progressive image transmission.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the mathematical equations for a piecewise-linear contrast stretching transformation function defined by the control points $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$ over the intensity range $[0, L-1]$. Show that the slope of the middle segment is greater than 1 when contrast stretching is being performed.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Setup**
The piecewise-linear contrast stretching function $T(r)$ is defined to pass through four points:
- $(r_0, s_0) = (0, 0)$
- $(r_1, s_1)$
- $(r_2, s_2)$
- $(r_3, s_3) = (L-1, L-1)$

The function consists of three linear segments connecting these points consecutively.

**Step 2: Derive Segment 1**
Segment 1 passes through $(0, 0)$ and $(r_1, s_1)$. Using the two-point form of a line:

$$
\frac{s - 0}{r - 0} = \frac{s_1 - 0}{r_1 - 0}
$$

Solving:
$$
s = \frac{s_1}{r_1} \cdot r, \quad \text{for } 0 \leq r \leq r_1
$$

**Step 3: Derive Segment 2**
Segment 2 passes through $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$:

$$
\frac{s - s_1}{r - r_1} = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}
$$

Solving:
$$
s = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1) + s_1, \quad \text{for } r_1 \leq r \leq r_2
$$

**Step 4: Derive Segment 3**
Segment 3 passes through $(r_2, s_2)$ and $(L-1, L-1)$:

$$
\frac{s - s_2}{r - r_2} = \frac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2}
$$

Solving:
$$
s = \frac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2} \cdot (r - r_2) + s_2, \quad \text{for } r_2 \leq r \leq L-1
$$

**Step 5: Complete Function**
$$
T(r) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{s_1}{r_1} \cdot r & \text{if } 0 \leq r \leq r_1 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1) + s_1 & \text{if } r_1 < r \leq r_2 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2} \cdot (r - r_2) + s_2 & \text{if } r_2 < r \leq L-1
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 6: Show Slope > 1 for Contrast Stretching**
The slope of the middle segment is:
$$
a_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}
$$

For contrast stretching, the input range $[r_1, r_2]$ is expanded to a wider output range $[s_1, s_2]$. This means:
$$
(s_2 - s_1) > (r_2 - r_1)
$$

Therefore:
$$
a_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} > 1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

This slope greater than 1 means that small differences in the input are magnified in the output, producing a **higher contrast** in this region. Conversely, segments 1 and 3 typically have slopes less than 1 (compression), so subtle differences there are reduced.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a 4-bit ($L = 16$) digital image with the following histogram data: most pixels have intensities in the range $[4, 9]$. A piecewise-linear contrast stretch is applied with control points $(r_1, s_1) = (4, 0)$ and $(r_2, s_2) = (9, 15)$. Compute the transformed output for input gray levels $r = 2, 4, 6, 7, 9, 11, 14$. Present your answer in a tabular form with proper reasoning.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 16$, so $L-1 = 15$
- $r_1 = 4$, $s_1 = 0$
- $r_2 = 9$, $s_2 = 15$

**Step 1: Derive the Three Segments**

**Segment 1** (for $0 \leq r \leq 4$):
$$
s = \frac{s_1}{r_1} \cdot r = \frac{0}{4} \cdot r = 0
$$

**Segment 2** (for $4 < r \leq 9$):
$$
s = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} \cdot (r - r_1) + s_1 = \frac{15 - 0}{9 - 4} \cdot (r - 4) + 0 = \frac{15}{5}(r - 4) = 3(r - 4)
$$

**Segment 3** (for $9 < r \leq 15$):
$$
s = \frac{(L-1) - s_2}{(L-1) - r_2} \cdot (r - r_2) + s_2 = \frac{15 - 15}{15 - 9} \cdot (r - 9) + 15 = 0 + 15 = 15
$$

**Step 2: Compute Output for Each Input**

- **$r = 2$**: $2 \in [0, 4]$ → Segment 1: $s = 0$
- **$r = 4$**: $4 \in [0, 4]$ → Segment 1: $s = 0$
- **$r = 6$**: $6 \in [4, 9]$ → Segment 2: $s = 3(6 - 4) = 6$
- **$r = 7$**: $7 \in [4, 9]$ → Segment 2: $s = 3(7 - 4) = 9$
- **$r = 9$**: $9 \in [4, 9]$ → Segment 2: $s = 3(9 - 4) = 15$
- **$r = 11$**: $11 \in [9, 15]$ → Segment 3: $s = 15$
- **$r = 14$**: $14 \in [9, 15]$ → Segment 3: $s = 15$

**Step 3: Tabulated Result**

| Input $r$ | Output $s$ | Segment Applied | Slope Used |
|-----------|------------|------------------|------------|
| 2 | 0 | Segment 1 | 0 (compressed to black) |
| 4 | 0 | Segment 1 | 0 |
| 6 | 6 | Segment 2 | 3 (stretched) |
| 7 | 9 | Segment 2 | 3 |
| 9 | 15 | Segment 2 | 3 |
| 11 | 15 | Segment 3 | 0 (saturated to white) |
| 14 | 15 | Segment 3 | 0 |

**Step 4: Interpretation**
- The narrow input range $[4, 9]$ (width = 5) is stretched to the full output range $[0, 15]$ (width = 15).
- The stretching factor in Segment 2 is $\dfrac{15}{5} = 3$, which is **3 times the original contrast**.
- All pixels darker than 4 are clipped to black (loss of dark detail).
- All pixels brighter than 9 are saturated to white (loss of bright detail).
- This is a **clipping-based min-max stretch** with potential information loss outside the $[4, 9]$ range.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Alternate Module Question]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **intensity-level slicing** as a piecewise-linear transformation. Compare the "with background" and "without background" approaches with suitable mathematical formulations. Give one real-world example where each is applied.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Intensity-level slicing is a piecewise-linear transformation technique used to **highlight or isolate a specific range of gray levels** in an image. It maps all input intensities within a defined range $[A, B]$ to a single high value (typically $L-1$, i.e., white), while handling the pixels outside this range in one of two ways.

**Mathematical Formulations:**

**Approach 1: With Background Preservation**
$$
s = T(r) =
\begin{cases}
L - 1 & \text{if } A \leq r \leq B \\
r & \text{if } r < A \text{ or } r > B
\end{cases}
$$

In this variant, the original gray levels outside $[A, B]$ are preserved, allowing the rest of the image to remain visible. The target range is highlighted in white.

**Approach 2: Without Background (Binary Isolation)**
$$
s = T(r) =
\begin{cases}
L - 1 & \text{if } A \leq r \leq B \\
0 & \text{if } r < A \text{ or } r > B
\end{cases}
$$

Here, the entire image becomes **binary** (only 0 or $L-1$), with the target range shown in white and everything else turned black. This produces a stark, isolated view of the feature.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | With Background | Without Background |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Output values | Full range $[0, L-1]$ | Only two values $\{0, L-1\}$ |
| Background visible? | Yes | No (turned black) |
| Information preserved | Maximum | Reduced (binary) |
| Visual effect | Highlighted feature with context | Stark feature isolation |
| Best used when | Context is important | Feature isolation is priority |

**Real-World Examples:**

1. **With Background**: In **medical imaging** (e.g., X-rays), highlighting a specific bone density range $[A, B]$ to enhance visualization of fractures, while still being able to see surrounding tissue. The doctor can identify the fracture AND understand its anatomical context.

2. **Without Background**: In **industrial defect detection**, isolating cracks or surface defects on manufactured parts. The defect appears white on a black background, making automated inspection algorithms (e.g., thresholding, edge detection) much more reliable.

**Graphical Interpretation:**
The transformation curve has a flat horizontal segment at $s = L-1$ over the range $r \in [A, B]$ (a "shelf"), and either follows the identity line (Approach 1) or drops to 0 (Approach 2) outside this range. This "shelf" shape is a piecewise-linear function with up to 3 segments (or 5 if both end behaviors are treated as separate flat segments).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-bit image ($L = 64$) has a pixel with value $p = 47$. Perform bit-plane slicing and show how this pixel is distributed across all 6 bit-planes. If the lower 3 bit-planes (planes 0, 1, 2) are discarded for compression, what is the reconstructed pixel value and the percentage compression achieved?

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 64$ (6-bit image, $L-1 = 63$)
- $p = 47$

**Step 1: Convert 47 to 6-bit Binary**

$47 = 32 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 2^5 + 2^3 + 2^2 + 2^1 + 2^0$

In binary: $47 = 101111_2$

So the bits are: $b_5 = 1, b_4 = 0, b_3 = 1, b_2 = 1, b_1 = 1, b_0 = 1$

**Step 2: Compute Bit-Plane Intensities**
For display, each bit is multiplied by $(L-1) = 63$.

| Bit Plane $k$ | Bit Value $b_k$ | Plane Intensity $B_k = b_k \cdot 63$ |
|---------------|------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 5 (MSB) | 1 | 63 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 63 |
| 2 | 1 | 63 |
| 1 | 1 | 63 |
| 0 (LSB) | 1 | 63 |

**Step 3: Verify Original Value**
$$
p = (1)(32) + (0)(16) + (1)(8) + (1)(4) + (1)(2) + (1)(1) = 32 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 47 \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Reconstruct After Discarding Planes 0, 1, 2**
We retain only $b_5, b_4, b_3$. The new reconstructed pixel $p'$ is:
$$
p' = b_5 \cdot 2^5 + b_4 \cdot 2^4 + b_3 \cdot 2^3
$$

$$
p' = (1)(32) + (0)(16) + (1)(8) = 40
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Compression**

- **Original bits per pixel**: 6
- **Retained bits per pixel**: 3
- **Compression ratio**: $\dfrac{6}{3} = 2:1$
- **Percentage compression**: $\dfrac{(6-3)}{6} \times 100\% = 50\%$

**Step 6: Quantify Information Loss**

The original value was 47; the reconstructed value is 40. The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** for a single pixel is $(47 - 40)^2 = 49$. The **percentage error** is $\dfrac{47 - 40}{47} \times 100\% = \dfrac{7}{47} \times 100\% \approx 14.89\%$.

This example shows the classic compression-accuracy tradeoff: **50% storage savings at the cost of ~15% error** for this particular pixel. Across a natural image, the perceptual error is usually much lower because lower bit-planes contain fine details and noise rather than critical structural information.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Examination Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the $(L-1)$ endpoint in Segment 3 derivation** (3 marks lost). Always state the range as $r_2 < r \leq L-1$, not $r_2 < r < \infty$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up input/output notation.** The horizontal axis is $r$ (input) and the vertical axis is $s$ (output). Using them interchangeably is a common error that loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 3. **Not specifying $L$ value in numerical problems.** Always explicitly state $L = 256$ (or whatever value is given) at the start of your solution. Examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
>
> 4. **Failing to draw the diagonal identity line $s = r$** when sketching the transformation function. This line is the reference for showing what constitutes "enhancement" vs. "no change."
>
> 5. **Confusing "with background" and "without background" intensity slicing variants.** The examiner often tests this distinction; use the correct mathematical formulation for each.
>
> 6. **In bit-plane problems, forgetting to multiply by $(L-1)$ for visualization.** A bit value of 1 must be displayed as 255 (or $L-1$) to be visible as a white pixel, not as 1.
>
> 7. **Not justifying why slope > 1 means stretching.** Always explain: "Slope > 1 implies that the output range is wider than the input range, which expands the difference between adjacent gray levels, producing higher contrast."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Piecewise-linear transformation** = a function $T(r)$ composed of multiple linear segments joined at breakpoints $(r_k, s_k)$.
- **Three principal applications**: Contrast Stretching, Intensity-Level Slicing, Bit-Plane Slicing.
- **Contrast Stretching Formula** (memorize this): Three segments, slopes $a_1 = s_1/r_1$, $a_2 = (s_2 - s_1)/(r_2 - r_1)$, $a_3 = ((L-1) - s_2)/((L-1) - r_2)$.
- **Slope Interpretation**: Slope > 1 ⇒ contrast expansion; Slope < 1 ⇒ contrast compression; Slope = 1 ⇒ no change.
- **Identity line** $s = r$: Represents the no-change transformation; any deviation from it constitutes enhancement.
- **Intensity Slicing**: Two variants — with background (preserves outside range) and without background (binary output).
- **Bit-Plane Decomposition**: For $n$-bit image, $n$ binary bit-planes; MSB carries most information, LSB carries noise.
- **Reconstruction**: $p = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k$.
- **Bit-plane display**: $B_k = b_k \cdot (L-1)$ for visualization.
- **Compression via bit-plane discard**: Saves storage but introduces reconstruction error; tradeoff governed by perceptual quality requirements.
- **Key parameter**: $L = 256$ for standard 8-bit grayscale images.
- **Continuity**: Piecewise-linear functions must be **continuous at breakpoints** (typically), though this is not strictly required by all formulations.
- **Perceptual basis**: Operations exploit the Weber-Fechner law of human visual perception.
- **Practical applications**: Medical imaging, satellite imaging, industrial inspection, steganography, document processing.
- **Exam-relevant edge cases**: When $r_1 = 0$, Segment 1 degenerates; when $r_2 = L-1$, Segment 3 degenerates. Mention these in your answer for full marks.
- **Min-Max Stretch**: Special case where $(r_1, s_1) = (r_{min}, 0)$ and $(r_2, s_2) = (r_{max}, L-1)$; maximum possible contrast enhancement without clipping.
- **Always include units and ranges** in your formulas; never leave $L$ unspecified.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contrast Stretching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contrast Stretching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contrast Stretching** is a **point processing spatial domain image enhancement technique** in which the dynamic range (or spread) of the input image's intensity values is **linearly remapped** onto a wider (or desired) output intensity range. Mathematically, it applies a piecewise linear transformation function to each pixel independently, such that a narrow input intensity interval $\left[r_{\min}, r_{\max}\right]$ is stretched to a broader (or full-scale) output interval $\left[s_{\min}, s_{\max}\right]$.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, contrast stretching falls under **Intensity Transformations** in Module 2 (Image Enhancement in the Spatial Domain). It is the simplest and most direct method to improve the visual appearance of images suffering from **low contrast** caused by poor illumination, inadequate sensor dynamic range, or wrong aperture setting of the imaging device.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Whisper to a Shout" Effect**
>
> Imagine you are speaking at a very low volume in a noisy room — your voice ranges between a soft whisper and a slightly louder whisper (say, a narrow range of 2 dB to 4 dB). People cannot hear you clearly. If you have a **microphone amplifier** that takes your quiet 2–4 dB voice and amplifies it linearly to a 60–80 dB output, suddenly your voice is loud, clear, and has a wide dynamic range.
>
> Contrast stretching does **exactly this to pixel intensities**:
> - The **input image** has pixel values clustered in a narrow band (e.g., 80–120 on a 0–255 scale) — it looks washed out and gray.
> - The **transformation function** linearly "amplifies" this narrow band into the **full** 0–255 range.
> - The **output image** now displays rich blacks, bright whites, and distinct gray levels — high contrast.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $r$ | Input pixel intensity (from the source image $f(x, y)$) |
| $s$ | Output pixel intensity (in the processed image $g(x, y)$) |
| $r_{\min}$ | Minimum intensity present in the input image |
| $r_{\max}$ | Maximum intensity present in the input image |
| $s_{\min}$ | Minimum intensity desired in the output image (usually **0**) |
| $s_{\max}$ | Maximum intensity desired in the output image (usually **$L-1$**, where $L$ = number of gray levels, typically **256**) |
| $L$ | Total number of gray levels (**256** for an 8-bit image) |
| $(r_1, s_1), (r_2, s_2)$ | Break-points in a piecewise linear stretch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Point Processing Property:** The operation $s = T(r)$ acts on **each pixel independently**. No neighborhood information is required. Therefore, the output at pixel $(x, y)$ depends **only** on the input intensity at the same $(x, y)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear (Min-Max) Contrast Stretching — The Core Idea

When the input image's intensities are confined to the narrow range $\left[r_{\min}, r_{\max}\right]$, we apply a linear transformation of the form:

$$s = T(r) = \left(\frac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right)(r - r_{\min}) + s_{\min}$$

This single straight line, when plotted with $r$ on the x-axis and $s$ on the y-axis, has:

- **Slope** $m = \dfrac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}$
- **Y-intercept** $c = s_{\min} - m \cdot r_{\min}$

If we choose $s_{\min} = 0$ and $s_{\max} = L - 1$, we obtain the **full-scale min-max normalization** (sometimes called *normalization* or *autocontrast* in software like MATLAB and OpenCV).

### 2.2 Piecewise Linear Contrast Stretching

Often, we do not want a single global stretch because it may over-saturate noise in flat regions. The standard KTU approach uses **piecewise linear** stretching with break-points $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$:

$$s = T(r) = \begin{cases} \alpha \, r & 0 \le r \le r_1 \\ \beta \, (r - r_1) + s_1 & r_1 < r \le r_2 \\ \gamma \, (r - r_2) + s_2 & r_2 < r \le L-1 \end{cases}$$

where the slope constants $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are chosen such that the curve is **continuous** at the break-points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special cases** (highly examinable in KTU):
> 1. If $r_1 = r_2 = r_{\text{thresh}}$ and $s_1 = 0$, $s_2 = L-1$, the piecewise function degenerates into **Image Thresholding** (binary conversion).
> 2. If all three segments have the same slope (i.e., the identity mapping), no stretching occurs.
> 3. If $s_1 < r_1$ and $s_2 > r_2$, we obtain **contrast stretching**.
> 4. If $s_1 > r_1$ and $s_2 < r_2$, we obtain **contrast compression** (the opposite operation).

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why normalize pixel values?** Because imaging sensors (CCD/CMOS) and quantization hardware may not utilize the full dynamic range of the display device. The histogram shows a narrow peak in the middle, indicating under-utilization.
- **How is the slope chosen?** The slope must be greater than 1 to *expand* the dynamic range. For a 2x expansion, the slope is exactly 2. For full-scale min-max stretching, the slope is $(L-1)/(r_{\max}-r_{\min})$ which is always $\ge 1$ for a real image.
- **Why piecewise?** Different intensity regions may have different semantic content (e.g., dark region = background, bright region = object). A piecewise function lets us treat each region independently — stretching the region of interest and compressing the irrelevant parts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Linear Contrast Stretching** | $s = \dfrac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}(r - r_{\min}) + s_{\min}$ | Direct remap of intensity range |
| 2 | **Full-scale (Min-Max) Stretch** | $s = \dfrac{(L-1)(r - r_{\min})}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}$ | Used when $s_{\min}=0$, $s_{\max}=L-1$ |
| 3 | **Slope of transformation** | $m = \dfrac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}$ | Determines strength of stretching |
| 4 | **Piecewise Linear** | 3-segment linear with break-points $(r_1,s_1),(r_2,s_2)$ | Region-specific enhancement |
| 5 | **Thresholding (degenerate case)** | $s = 0$ if $r < T$, else $s = L-1$ | Binarization for segmentation |
| 6 | **Identity (no change)** | $s = r$ | Pass-through, slope = 1 |
| 7 | **Negative Image** | $s = (L-1) - r$ | Inversion (e.g., medical X-rays) |
| 8 | **Continuous Piecewise Slope (middle segment)** | $\beta = \dfrac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}$ | Continuity constraint at $(r_1,s_1)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units / Variables Note:** All $r$ and $s$ values are **dimensionless** (normalized) integer intensity levels in the range $\left[0, L-1\right]$. For an 8-bit image, $L = \mathbf{256}$ and intensities lie in $\left[0, 255\right]$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Medical Imaging:** Enhancing MRI / CT / X-ray scans where the captured dynamic range is too narrow.
- **Satellite Remote Sensing:** Landsat / Sentinel imagery where atmospheric haze compresses the histogram.
- **Robotic Vision (relevant to OERAT832):** Pre-processing step for object detection pipelines — high contrast improves edge detection and feature extraction downstream.
- **Forensic Imaging:** Recovering detail from underexposed CCTV footage.
- **Industrial Inspection:** Detecting micro-defects on PCBs / wafers where contrast between defect and background is naturally low.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Contrast Stretching Formula

**Goal:** Derive a linear function $s = m \cdot r + c$ that maps the input intensity interval $\left[r_{\min}, r_{\max}\right]$ onto the desired output interval $\left[s_{\min}, s_{\max}\right]$.

**Step 1 — State the general form of a straight line:**

$$s = m \cdot r + c$$

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition at the lower end.** When the input is $r = r_{\min}$, the output must be $s = s_{\min}$:

$$s_{\min} = m \cdot r_{\min} + c \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = s_{\min} - m \cdot r_{\min}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition at the upper end.** When the input is $r = r_{\max}$, the output must be $s = s_{\max}$:

$$s_{\max} = m \cdot r_{\max} + c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $c$ from Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$s_{\max} = m \cdot r_{\max} + (s_{\min} - m \cdot r_{\min})$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by collecting the $m$ terms:**

$$s_{\max} - s_{\min} = m \cdot r_{\max} - m \cdot r_{\min} = m(r_{\max} - r_{\min})$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the slope $m$:**

$$m = \frac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $m$ back into the general form to obtain the canonical KTU formula:**

$$s = \left(\frac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}\right)(r - r_{\min}) + s_{\min}$$

This is the **standard contrast-stretching equation** as it appears in KTU textbooks.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full-Scale Min-Max Stretch)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A 4-bit image ($L = 16$, intensities $0$ to $15$) has a histogram confined to the range $\left[r_{\min}, r_{\max}\right] = \left[4, 12\right]$. Apply full-scale contrast stretching to remap this range to the full output range $\left[0, 15\right]$. Find the new intensity of an input pixel $r = 7$.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

$$L = 16, \quad s_{\min} = 0, \quad s_{\max} = L - 1 = 15, \quad r_{\min} = 4, \quad r_{\max} = 12, \quad r = 7$$

**Step 2 — Compute the slope $m$:**

$$m = \frac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}} = \frac{15 - 0}{12 - 4} = \frac{15}{8} = 1.875$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the transformation formula:**

$$s = m(r - r_{\min}) + s_{\min} = 1.875 \times (7 - 4) + 0$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$s = 1.875 \times 3 = 5.625$$

**Step 5 — Round to the nearest valid intensity level (since we are working with integers):**

$$s \approx 6$$

**Step 6 — Verify boundary correctness:**

- For $r = 4$: $s = 1.875 \times 0 = 0$ ✓
- For $r = 12$: $s = 1.875 \times 8 = 15$ ✓
- For $r = 7$: $s = 1.875 \times 3 = 5.625 \approx 6$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The input pixel value $7$ is mapped to approximately $6$ in the stretched image. The overall narrow band $\left[4, 12\right]$ is now expanded to span the full $\left[0, 15\right]$ range, increasing the image's contrast.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Stretching Variants

| Transformation Type | $(r_1, s_1)$ | $(r_2, s_2)$ | Slope of middle segment | Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **No change (identity)** | $(r_1, r_1)$ | $(r_2, r_2)$ | $1$ | Output = Input |
| **Contrast Stretching** | $(r_1, 0)$ | $(r_2, L-1)$ | $\dfrac{L-1}{r_2 - r_1} > 1$ | Enhanced contrast |
| **Contrast Compression** | $(r_1, r_1/2)$ | $(r_2, (L-1+r_2)/2)$ | $< 1$ | Reduced contrast |
| **Thresholding** | $(T, 0)$ | $(T, L-1)$ | Undefined (vertical jump) | Binary image |
| **Negative / Inversion** | $(0, L-1)$ | $(L-1, 0)$ | $-1$ | Photographic negative |
| **Gray-level slicing (no bg)** | $(r_1, L-1)$ | $(r_2, L-1)$ | $0$ | Highlight band, darken rest |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Piecewise Stretch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** For an 8-bit image ($L = 256$), design a piecewise linear stretch that:
> - Leaves $\left[0, 100\right]$ as background (identity),
> - Stretches $\left[100, 150\right]$ to $\left[50, 200\right]$ (highlight region of interest),
> - Saturates $\left[150, 255\right]$ to $255$ (full white for bright objects).
>
> Compute the output for $r = 125$.

**Step 1 — Identify segments:**

$$\text{Segment A: } r \in [0, 100], \quad s = r$$

$$\text{Segment B: } r \in [100, 150], \quad \text{break-points } (r_1=100, s_1=50), (r_2=150, s_2=200)$$

$$\text{Segment C: } r \in [150, 255], \quad s = 255$$

**Step 2 — Slope of Segment B (continuity check):**

$$\beta = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} = \frac{200 - 50}{150 - 100} = \frac{150}{50} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula for $r = 125$:**

$$s = 3 \times (125 - 100) + 50 = 3 \times 25 + 50 = 75 + 50 = 125$$

**Step 4 — Verify continuity at $r = 100$:**

- From Segment A: $s = 100$
- From Segment B at $r = 100$: $s = 3(0) + 50 = 50$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discontinuity Alert!** The two segments do **not** match at $r = 100$ (50 vs 100). To fix this, either change $s_1$ to $100$ (then the slopes must be re-evaluated) or accept a small jump. A KTU-correct design would set $s_1 = 100$ and recompute $\beta = \dfrac{200 - 100}{150 - 100} = 2$. Students often **lose marks** for not checking continuity at the break-points.

**Step 5 — Re-evaluated continuous design:**

$$s = 2(r - 100) + 100 \quad \text{for } r \in [100, 150]$$

$$s(125) = 2(25) + 100 = 150$$

**Conclusion:** With continuity enforced, an input of $r = 125$ is mapped to $s = 150$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Contrast Stretching Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputImg[Input Image f x y] --> histAnalysis[Histogram Analysis]
    histAnalysis --> findMinMax[Find r_min and r_max]
    findMinMax --> slopeCalc[Compute slope m]
    slopeCalc --> lutGen[Generate Look Up Table LUT]
    lutGen --> pixelMap[Map each pixel using s = m r minus r_min plus s_min]
    pixelMap --> clipClip[Optional Clipping at 0 and L minus 1]
    clipClip --> outputImg[Output Image g x y Enhanced Contrast]
    slopeCalc --> feedback[Slope value used for LUT indexing]
    feedback --> lutGen
    findMinMax --> feedback
```

### 4.2 Transformation Function Plot (Mimicking a Graph)

The following Mermaid `xychart-beta` block visualizes a typical **piecewise linear** contrast stretching transformation function. The x-axis represents the **input intensity $r$** and the y-axis represents the **output intensity $s$**. Notice how the middle segment has a slope greater than 1, indicating *expansion* of the contrast in the region of interest.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Piecewise Linear Contrast Stretching Transformation T(r)"
    x-axis "Input intensity r" [0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 225, 255]
    y-axis "Output intensity s" 0 --> 255
    line [0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 225, 255]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The plot above is a *schematic representation*. In a true contrast-stretching function, the *middle* segment (between the two break-points) would have a slope **steeper** than 1, while the *outer* segments would have a slope *less than* 1. The xy-chart above is shown as identity for clarity of the coordinate system. Below is a textual representation of a stretched mapping.

**Textual representation of a piecewise stretched curve:**

```
s
255 |                            _____________________
    |                       ____/
    |                  ____/
200 |              ___/        <-- Steep middle (stretched) segment
    |          ___/
    |      ___/
150 |  ___/
    | /
100 |/                                  <-- (r1=100, s1=100)
 50 |                          (r2=150, s2=200)
  0 |________________________________________
    0    50   100  150   200   255            r
        ^   ^       ^
       Flat Steep   Flat
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — End-to-End Image Enhancement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scene Captured by Camera] --> B[Sensor Readout Raw Image]
    B --> C[Noise Filtering Optional]
    C --> D[Histogram Computation]
    D --> E[Determine r_min and r_max]
    E --> F[Apply Contrast Stretching T r]
    F --> G[Display or Downstream Task]
    F --> H[Edge Detection]
    F --> I[Segmentation]
    F --> J[Object Recognition]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Selecting the Appropriate Stretch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start with Image Histogram] --> q1{Is histogram narrow and centered?}
    q1 -- Yes --> linear[Use Linear Min Max Stretch]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is there a specific intensity band of interest?}
    q2 -- Yes --> piecewise[Use Piecewise Linear Stretch]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Do you need a binary image?}
    q3 -- Yes --> thresh[Use Thresholding Special Case of Piecewise]
    q3 -- No --> identity[Use Identity Pass Through]
    linear --> end1[Output Enhanced Image]
    piecewise --> end1
    thresh --> end1
    identity --> end1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1.** Define **Contrast Stretching** in spatial domain image processing. Mention the role of $(r_{\min}, r_{\max})$ and $(s_{\min}, s_{\max})$ in the transformation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Contrast stretching is a point processing technique that linearly remaps a narrow range of input intensity values $\left[r_{\min}, r_{\max}\right]$ to a wider output range $\left[s_{\min}, s_{\max}\right]$ to enhance visual contrast. **$(r_{\min}, r_{\max})$** represent the minimum and maximum intensity values actually present in the input image, while **$(s_{\min}, s_{\max})$** represent the desired lower and upper bounds of the output intensity scale (commonly $0$ and $L-1$). The transformation expands the dynamic range of the image, making dark regions darker and bright regions brighter. (1 mark definition + 1 mark role of input range + 1 mark role of output range)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
>
> **Q2.** How does **piecewise linear contrast stretching** differ from simple linear contrast stretching? When is the piecewise form preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Simple linear contrast stretching applies a **single linear transformation** to the entire intensity range, whereas piecewise linear contrast stretching uses **multiple linear segments** separated by user-defined break-points $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$. The piecewise form is preferred when: (i) only a specific intensity band contains the region of interest and we wish to **expand** that band while **compressing** irrelevant regions, (ii) the image has a bimodal histogram requiring independent treatment of two modes, or (iii) we need to perform **gray-level slicing** to highlight a particular structure. (1 mark distinction + 2 marks preference with reasons)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question Paper]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for **linear contrast stretching**. Clearly state the assumptions and the role of each term. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 3-bit image ($L = 8$) has pixel values in the range $\left[2, 6\right]$. Apply **full-scale min-max contrast stretching** to remap these values to the full range $\left[0, 7\right]$. Construct a complete input-output mapping table and verify the endpoints. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

1. State the goal: map $\left[r_{\min}, r_{\max}\right]$ linearly to $\left[s_{\min}, s_{\max}\right]$. **(1 mark)**
2. Write the general linear form $s = m r + c$. **(1 mark)**
3. Apply the two boundary conditions: $(r_{\min}, s_{\min})$ and $(r_{\max}, s_{\max})$. **(2 marks)**
4. Solve the simultaneous equations to obtain $m$ and $c$. **(2 marks)**
5. Write the final canonical formula. **(1 mark)**

Final derived equation:

$$s = \frac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}(r - r_{\min}) + s_{\min}$$

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $L = 8$, $r_{\min} = 2$, $r_{\max} = 6$, $s_{\min} = 0$, $s_{\max} = 7$.

**Step 1 — Compute the slope:** **(2 marks)**

$$m = \frac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}} = \frac{7 - 0}{6 - 2} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75$$

**Step 2 — Compute transformation formula:** **(1 mark)**

$$s = 1.75 \times (r - 2)$$

**Step 3 — Build the mapping table:** **(3 marks)**

| Input $r$ | Computation | Output $s$ | Rounded $s$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | $1.75 \times 0$ | $0.00$ | **0** |
| 3 | $1.75 \times 1$ | $1.75$ | **2** |
| 4 | $1.75 \times 2$ | $3.50$ | **4** |
| 5 | $1.75 \times 3$ | $5.25$ | **5** |
| 6 | $1.75 \times 4$ | $7.00$ | **7** |

**Step 4 — Verify endpoints:** **(1 mark)**

- $r = 2 \rightarrow s = 0$ ✓
- $r = 6 \rightarrow s = 7$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The input range $\left[2, 6\right]$ has been successfully stretched to the full output range $\left[0, 7\right]$, maximizing the dynamic range of the 3-bit image.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question Paper]**
>
> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain **piecewise linear contrast stretching** for an 8-bit image. Discuss the effect of varying the slopes of the three segments. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For an 8-bit image, design a piecewise linear contrast stretching function with the following specifications:
> - Input range $\left[0, 80\right]$ should be compressed to output range $\left[0, 40\right]$ (slope = 0.5).
> - Input range $\left[80, 160\right]$ should be stretched to output range $\left[40, 200\right]$.
> - Input range $\left[160, 255\right]$ should be saturated to $255$.
>
> Verify the continuity at the break-points and compute the new value for $r = 200$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

1. Define piecewise linear transformation with three segments and break-points $(r_1, s_1)$, $(r_2, s_2)$. **(2 marks)**
2. Provide the mathematical form:

$$s = \begin{cases} \alpha r & 0 \le r \le r_1 \\ \beta(r - r_1) + s_1 & r_1 < r \le r_2 \\ \gamma(r - r_2) + s_2 & r_2 < r \le L-1 \end{cases}$$

**(2 marks)**

3. Sketch the transformation function (sketch description: identity line, steep middle, flat tail). **(1 mark)**
4. Discuss slope effects:
   - $\alpha < 1$: dark region compressed (background suppression).
   - $\beta > 1$: middle region stretched (contrast enhanced in region of interest).
   - $\gamma < 1$ or $\gamma = 0$: bright region compressed or saturated (highlight clipping). **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the three segments and their slopes:** **(2 marks)**

- Segment 1: $r \in [0, 80]$, slope $\alpha = 0.5$, so $s_1 = 0.5 \times 80 = 40$. Break-point $(r_1, s_1) = (80, 40)$.
- Segment 2: $r \in [80, 160]$, target range $[40, 200]$.

**Step 2 — Verify continuity at $r_1 = 80$:** **(1 mark)**
- From Segment 1: $s(80) = 0.5 \times 80 = 40$ ✓
- Break-point is continuous. ✓

**Step 3 — Compute slope of Segment 2:** **(1 mark)**

$$\beta = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1} = \frac{200 - 40}{160 - 80} = \frac{160}{80} = 2$$

**Step 4 — Verify continuity at $r_2 = 160$:** **(1 mark)**

$$s(160) = 2 \times (160 - 80) + 40 = 2 \times 80 + 40 = 200 \text{ ✓}$$

**Step 5 — Compute for $r = 200$:** **(1 mark)**
$r = 200$ falls in Segment 3, which is saturated to $255$. So $s(200) = 255$.

**Step 6 — Final mapping:** **(1 mark)**

$$s = \begin{cases} 0.5 \, r & 0 \le r \le 80 \\ 2(r - 80) + 40 & 80 < r \le 160 \\ 255 & 160 < r \le 255 \end{cases}$$

**Conclusion:** The designed piecewise function is continuous at both break-points, enhances contrast in the mid-tone region (slope 2), compresses dark regions, and saturates bright regions. Input $r = 200$ maps to $s = 255$.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $+ s_{\min}$ term** in the final formula. Many students write only the slope and the $(r - r_{\min})$ product, omitting the offset. This loses **1–2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing $(r_1, s_1)$ with $(s_1, r_1)$.** The first coordinate is **input** intensity, the second is **output** intensity. Mixing them gives a wrong answer.
> 3. **Not verifying continuity at break-points** in piecewise stretching. KTU examiners specifically check this. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Rounding errors** when applying the formula. Always round to the **nearest integer** (or floor, as specified by the question).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention $L = 256$** for an 8-bit image. A formula like $s_{\max} = L - 1 = 255$ must explicitly show this.
> 6. **Stating "contrast stretching increases contrast" without explaining why** (it expands the dynamic range). Marks are awarded for the *reason*, not just the conclusion.
> 7. **Confusing point processing with neighborhood processing.** Contrast stretching is a *point* operation. Histogram equalization is also a point operation in the *spatial* domain but uses probability. Spatial *filtering* (e.g., averaging) is *not* point processing.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Contrast Stretching:**
>
> - [ ] **Definition:** Linear remapping of input intensity interval $\left[r_{\min}, r_{\max}\right]$ to output interval $\left[s_{\min}, s_{\max}\right]$.
> - [ ] **Canonical Formula:** $s = \dfrac{s_{\max} - s_{\min}}{r_{\max} - r_{\min}}(r - r_{\min}) + s_{\min}$
> - [ ] **Full-Scale Special Case:** When $s_{\min} = 0$ and $s_{\max} = L - 1$.
> - [ ] **Slope $m$:** Must be $> 1$ for contrast *expansion*; $< 1$ for contrast *compression*.
> - [ ] **Piecewise Variant:** Three segments separated by break-points $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$.
> - [ ] **Continuity Constraint:** $\beta = \dfrac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}$ and the segments must connect smoothly.
> - [ ] **Thresholding:** A degenerate piecewise case with a single break-point and a vertical jump — produces a binary image.
> - [ ] **Identity Function:** $s = r$ corresponds to slope = 1 and zero offset.
> - [ ] **Negative Image:** $s = (L-1) - r$ — a special linear case with slope $-1$.
> - [ ] **Point Operation:** Each pixel is processed independently; no neighborhood is involved.
> - [ ] **Histogram Effect:** Narrow histogram becomes wide; visual contrast increases.
> - [ ] **Order of Operations:** (1) Compute histogram → (2) Find $r_{\min}, r_{\max}$ → (3) Compute slope → (4) Apply transformation → (5) Round/clip → (6) Output image.
> - [ ] **Noisy Image Warning:** Contrast stretching *amplifies* noise along with signal. For noisy images, apply a denoising filter **before** stretching.
> - [ ] **KTU Marks Distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Derivation (3 marks) + Numerical application (4 marks) + Continuity/verification (1 mark) in a typical 10-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intensity Level Slicing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intensity Level Slicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Intensity Level Slicing** is a fundamental *point processing technique* in the spatial domain of digital image processing used to *highlight* or *isolate* a specific band (slice) of gray-level intensities in an image while *suppressing* all other intensity values to either a uniform background or a constant level. It is a contrast-stretching variant whose output is piecewise constant rather than continuously stretched.

Mathematically, the transformation $T(r)$ is defined for a chosen intensity range $[L_1, L_2]$ as:

For **Slicing without background preservation (Binary/Threshold slicing):**
$$
T(r) = \begin{cases} L_{\max} & \text{if } L_1 \leq r \leq L_2 \\ L_{\min} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For **Slicing with background preservation (Gray-level slicing):**
$$
T(r) = \begin{cases} L_{\max} & \text{if } L_1 \leq r \leq L_2 \\ r & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $r$ = input pixel intensity in range $[0, L-1]$ (typically $[0, 255]$ for 8-bit images)
- $L_{\min}$ = minimum displayable intensity (**0** for standard 8-bit grayscale)
- $L_{\max}$ = maximum displayable intensity (**255** for standard 8-bit grayscale)
- $L_1, L_2$ = lower and upper bounds of the slice band

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Intensity Level Slicing is categorized under *Intensity (Gray-Level) Transformations* in Module 2. It is a *point operation* — the output depends ONLY on the input pixel value, NOT on spatial neighborhood.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Metal Detector at the Beach" Analogy:**
Imagine sweeping a metal detector along a sandy beach. The detector (your image processing function) *only beeps* (highlights) when it encounters objects made of a specific metal composition — say, gold rings of a particular purity (the slice band $[L_1, L_2]$). All other materials (iron, sand, plastic) are ignored or silenced (mapped to background).

- **Binary Slicing** = A detector that just beeps (255) for gold and stays silent (0) for everything else. You *lose the context* of the surrounding beach.
- **Gray-Level Slicing** = A detector that beeps for gold but also displays a faint map of the rest of the beach in normal tones. You *preserve the context* of where the gold was found.

This is *exactly* what intensity level slicing does — it isolates pixels whose intensity lies within a band of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallels:** Radiologists use intensity slicing on X-rays and CT scans to highlight tissue densities corresponding to tumors or bone fractures. Satellite imaging uses it to highlight vegetation indices (NDVI), water bodies, or burn scars.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Slicing Methods

| Method | Output Behavior | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Slicing** | Pixels in band → White, Others → Black | Segmentation, mask creation |
| **Gray-Level Slicing** | Pixels in band → White, Others → Preserved | Highlighting features without losing context |
| **Bit-Plane Slicing** | Isolates individual binary planes of pixel representation | Compression, watermarking, steganography |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The intensity level slicing transformation operates on **every pixel independently** following this logic chain:

1. **Pixel Ingestion:** Read the input gray-level value $r$ at spatial coordinate $(x, y)$.
2. **Boundary Comparison:** Compare $r$ against the predefined lower bound $L_1$ and upper bound $L_2$.
3. **Decision Routing:** If $r \in [L_1, L_2]$, route to the *highlight* output value; otherwise, route to the *background* path.
4. **Output Assignment:**
   - In *Binary Slicing*: assign $L_{\max}$ (e.g., 255) if in band, else $L_{\min}$ (e.g., 0).
   - In *Gray-Level Slicing*: assign $L_{\max}$ (e.g., 255) if in band, else preserve $r$ unchanged.
5. **Pixel Emission:** Write the transformed value back to the output image at the same coordinate.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1-2:** Slicing is a *point operation* — no filtering kernel, no neighbor lookups. This makes it computationally trivial (O(N) for an image of N pixels) and trivially parallelizable.
- **Step 3:** The piecewise nature is the entire point. Unlike linear contrast stretching (which is monotonic and continuous), slicing creates a *discrete binary decision* about membership in the band of interest.
- **Step 4:** The choice between binary and gray-level slicing is dictated by whether the downstream task needs *spatial context* (gray-level) or a *clean segmentation mask* (binary).

## 2.3 Mathematical Representations in Detail

### 2.3.1 Binary Intensity Slicing (Without Background)

$$
s = T(r) = \begin{cases} 255, & L_1 \leq r \leq L_2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The transfer function plot is a **rectangular pulse** of amplitude 255 within $[L_1, L_2]$ and zero elsewhere.

### 2.3.2 Gray-Level Intensity Slicing (With Background)

$$
s = T(r) = \begin{cases} 255, & L_1 \leq r \leq L_2 \\ r, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The transfer function plot is the **identity line $s = r$** interrupted by a **rectangular pulse** jumping to 255 within $[L_1, L_2]$.

### 2.3.3 Bit-Plane Slicing (Auxiliary Variant)

For an 8-bit pixel $r$ expressed in binary as $b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$, the $k$-th bit-plane is:

$$
B_k(x,y) = \text{bit}_k(r) \times 255
$$

This decomposes the image into 8 binary slices, each isolating the contribution of one bit position.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s = T(r)$ | Point transformation function | Pixel intensity |
| 2 | $L_1 \leq r \leq L_2$ | Slice band condition | $0 \leq L_1 < L_2 \leq 255$ |
| 3 | Binary: $s \in \{0, 255\}$ | Two-level output | Pixels |
| 4 | Gray-level: $s = 255$ if in band, $s = r$ otherwise | Mixed output | Pixels |
| 5 | Bit-plane: $B_k = b_k \cdot 255$ | Bit isolation | Pixels, $k \in [0,7]$ |
| 6 | Histogram of slice: $H_{slice} = \sum_{r=L_1}^{L_2} h(r)$ | Pixel count in band | Pixels |
| 7 | Slice area ratio: $R = \dfrac{H_{slice}}{M \times N}$ | Fraction of highlighted region | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Transform magnitude: $\Delta s = 255 - r_{bg}$ | Contrast jump at boundaries | Intensity levels |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When plotting the transfer function, ALWAYS clearly mark the values of $L_1$, $L_2$, and the output level (typically 255). Examiners award marks for correct axis labeling with `s` (output) on Y-axis and `r` (input) on X-axis.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Highlighting Hounsfield unit ranges corresponding to specific tissue types in CT scans.
- **Remote Sensing:** Isolating vegetation cover, water bodies, urban heat islands.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Detecting surface defects, weld flaws, or material inclusions.
- **Astronomy:** Extracting emission lines from nebula spectra or star fields.
- **Forensics:** Revealing latent fingerprints, bloodstains, or document alterations using specific reflective bands.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Binary Slicing Transformation

**Problem Statement:** An 8-bit grayscale image of size $4 \times 4$ is given. Apply **binary intensity slicing** to highlight pixels with intensities in the range $[L_1, L_2] = [120, 180]$. All pixels in this range should be mapped to 255 (white), and all others to 0 (black). Construct the output image and the transfer function.

### Input Image Matrix (4×4):

$$
I_{in} = \begin{bmatrix} 90 & 130 & 200 & 115 \\ 175 & 80 & 145 & 50 \\ 210 & 125 & 95 & 180 \\ 60 & 155 & 100 & 165 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1: Define the Transformation Function

For binary intensity slicing with slice band $[120, 180]$:

$$
T(r) = \begin{cases} 255, & 120 \leq r \leq 180 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2: Element-wise Application

We scan each pixel and apply the rule:

**Row 1:**
- $r = 90$: $90 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$
- $r = 130$: $130 \in [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 255$
- $r = 200$: $200 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$
- $r = 115$: $115 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$

**Row 2:**
- $r = 175$: $175 \in [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 255$
- $r = 80$: $80 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$
- $r = 145$: $145 \in [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 255$
- $r = 50$: $50 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$

**Row 3:**
- $r = 210$: $210 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$
- $r = 125$: $125 \in [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 255$
- $r = 95$: $95 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$
- $r = 180$: $180 \in [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 255$

**Row 4:**
- $r = 60$: $60 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$
- $r = 155$: $155 \in [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 255$
- $r = 100$: $100 \notin [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 0$
- $r = 165$: $165 \in [120, 180] \Rightarrow s = 255$

### Step 3: Construct Output Image

$$
I_{out} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 255 & 0 & 0 \\ 255 & 0 & 255 & 0 \\ 0 & 255 & 0 & 255 \\ 0 & 255 & 0 & 255 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Slice Area Ratio

Total pixels in band:

$$
H_{slice} = \sum_{r=120}^{180} h(r) = 7 \text{ pixels}
$$

Total image pixels: $M \times N = 4 \times 4 = 16$

$$
R = \frac{H_{slice}}{M \times N} = \frac{7}{16} = 0.4375 = 43.75\%
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Gray-Level Slicing with Background Preservation

**Problem Statement:** Apply *gray-level intensity slicing* to the SAME input image with the same band $[120, 180]$, preserving the original intensity for pixels outside the band.

### Step 1: Define Transformation

$$
T(r) = \begin{cases} 255, & 120 \leq r \leq 180 \\ r, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2: Apply to Each Pixel

For pixels in the band, we set 255. For pixels outside, we copy the original value.

### Step 3: Output Image

$$
I_{out} = \begin{bmatrix} 90 & 255 & 200 & 115 \\ 255 & 80 & 255 & 50 \\ 210 & 255 & 95 & 255 \\ 60 & 255 & 100 & 255 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4: Compare the Two Methods

Notice that in **binary slicing** we *destroyed* all information outside the band (turned 90, 200, 115, etc., into 0). In **gray-level slicing**, we *preserved* the original context (90 stays 90, 200 stays 200, etc.). This is the critical practical difference.

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation of the Transfer Function Plot

The binary slicing transfer function is mathematically a *rectangular window function*:

$$
T(r) = 255 \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{r - \mu}{W}\right)
$$

where:
- $\mu = \dfrac{L_1 + L_2}{2}$ is the **center** of the slice band
- $W = L_2 - L_1$ is the **width** of the slice band
- $\text{rect}(x) = 1$ if $\vert x \vert \leq 0.5$, else $0$

**Verification:** If $r \in [L_1, L_2]$, then $\vert r - \mu \vert \leq W/2$, so $\text{rect}(\cdot) = 1$ and $T(r) = 255$. If $r \notin [L_1, L_2]$, then $\text{rect}(\cdot) = 0$ and $T(r) = 0$. ✓

## 3.4 Derivative Analysis: Why Slicing Creates "Hard Edges"

The first derivative of the binary slicing function contains **two Dirac delta impulses**:

$$
\frac{dT(r)}{dr} = 255 \cdot \left[\delta(r - L_1) - \delta(r - L_2)\right]
$$

This mathematical fact has a practical consequence: **small changes in pixel intensity near the boundaries $L_1$ or $L_2$ can cause dramatic changes in output** (jumping from 0 to 255 or vice versa). This is why slicing is sensitive to noise — a single noisy pixel crossing the threshold flips its classification entirely.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
