For each seed, neighboring pixels are examined; if a neighbor satisfies a similarity predicate (commonly the absolute gray-level difference test $\vert f(p) - m_R \vert \leq T$), it is appended to the region. The process repeats until no further pixel qualifies.

**Example:** In a satellite image, all pixels with NDVI values between 0.6 and 0.8 within a connected neighborhood of a forest seed pixel are grouped into the "dense forest" region.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Example (1 mark).

### Question 2: What is a Quadtree? How is it Used in Image Segmentation? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **quadtree** is a hierarchical tree data structure in which each internal node has exactly four children representing the four quadrants (NW, NE, SW, SE) of its parent region. In image segmentation, the root node represents the entire image. A region that fails the homogeneity test $H(R)$ is split into four equal sub-regions, and the test is applied recursively. When a region satisfies $H(R)$, it becomes a leaf. Adjacent leaves that are similar can be merged back. The result is a set of square, homogeneous regions that constitute the segmentation.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Use in segmentation (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks): Region Growing — Algorithm, Predicate, and Worked Example

`[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Explain the **Region Growing** algorithm in detail. Describe the role of seed points, similarity predicates, and termination conditions.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (2 marks):**
Region Growing is a sequential procedure that groups pixels of similar intensity into connected regions. It is one of the simplest and most intuitive region-based segmentation techniques.

**2. Seed Point Selection (2 marks):**
- A **seed pixel** $s$ is a starting point for region growth.
- Seeds can be selected manually by the user, or automatically via:
  - Histogram peaks (most common).
  - Centroid of clusters in feature space.
  - Domain knowledge (e.g., placing a seed inside a known anatomical structure in medical imaging).

**3. Similarity Predicate (2 marks):**
The standard predicate is:

$$
S(p, R) = \text{True} \iff \vert f(p) - m_R \vert \leq T
$$

where $m_R$ is the current region mean. The threshold $T$ controls region compactness:
- Small $T$ → many small, tightly homogeneous regions.
- Large $T$ → fewer, larger, less-homogeneous regions.

**4. Termination (1 mark):**
The algorithm stops when no unchecked neighbor pixel satisfies $S(p, R)$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Consider a $4 \times 4$ image with intensity values:

$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 10 & 90 & 92 \\ 9 & 11 & 88 & 91 \\ 7 & 9 & 85 & 89 \\ 8 & 10 & 86 & 90 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply **region growing** with seed at $(1,1)$ and threshold $T = 5$. Show all iterations and the final region.

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1:** $R_0 = \{8\}$, $m_R = 8$.

Neighbors of $(1,1)$: $(1,2)=10$, $(2,1)=9$.

$$
\vert 10 - 8 \vert = 2 \leq 5 \quad \checkmark \qquad \vert 9 - 8 \vert = 1 \leq 5 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Iteration 2:** $R_1 = \{8, 10, 9\}$, $m_R = 9$.

New neighbors: $(1,3)=90$, $(2,2)=11$, $(3,1)=7$.

$$
\vert 90 - 9 \vert = 81 > 5 \quad \times \qquad \vert 11 - 9 \vert = 2 \leq 5 \quad \checkmark \qquad \vert 7 - 9 \vert = 2 \leq 5 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Iteration 3:** $R_2 = \{8, 10, 9, 11, 7\}$, $m_R = 9$.

New neighbors: $(1,4)=92$, $(2,3)=88$, $(3,2)=9$, $(4,1)=8$.

All the right-side values ($92, 88$) have differences $> 70$ → rejected.
$\vert 9 - 9 \vert = 0 \leq 5$ → add; $\vert 8 - 9 \vert = 1 \leq 5$ → add.

**Iteration 4:** $R_3 = \{8, 10, 9, 11, 7, 9, 8\}$, $m_R = 62/7 \approx 8.86$.

New neighbors: $(2,4)=91$, $(3,3)=85$, $(4,2)=10$.

$\vert 91 - 8.86 \vert = 82.14 > 5$ → reject.
$\vert 85 - 8.86 \vert = 76.14 > 5$ → reject.
$\vert 10 - 8.86 \vert = 1.14 \leq 5$ → add.

**Iteration 5:** $R_4 = \{8, 10, 9, 11, 7, 9, 8, 10\}$, $m_R = 72/8 = 9$.

Boundary neighbors remaining: $(3,4)=89$, $(4,3)=86$, $(4,4)=90$.

All have differences $> 77$ → rejected.

**Termination.** Final region $R^* = 8$ pixels — the entire left half of the image, correctly separated from the right half.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Region Splitting, Merging, and Split-and-Merge

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Explain the **Region Splitting** algorithm. Describe the homogeneity predicate and quadtree representation with a suitable example.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
Region splitting is a **top-down** approach. The image is recursively subdivided into smaller and smaller regions until each sub-region satisfies a homogeneity criterion. The standard implementation uses a **quadtree** because it divides square regions into four equal quadrants.

**2. Homogeneity Predicate (3 marks):**

$$
H(R) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } \max_{p \in R} f(p) - \min_{p \in R} f(p) \leq T \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $T$ is the user-defined intensity threshold. If $H(R)$ is false, split $R$ into 4 equal quadrants: $R_{NW}, R_{NE}, R_{SW}, R_{SE}$.

**3. Example (2 marks):**
For a $4 \times 4$ image with two distinct intensity zones (e.g., left half $\approx 50$, right half $\approx 200$), the whole image is split into 4 quadrants. The two left quadrants are homogeneous (max-min = 0) and become leaves. Similarly the two right quadrants. The result is a quadtree of depth 1 with 4 leaves.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Describe the **Split and Merge** algorithm. How does merging complement splitting? Illustrate with the quadtree structure.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Why Split-and-Merge? (2 marks):**
Pure splitting may create adjacent quadrants that satisfy $H$ individually but belong to the same object. Conversely, pure merging is computationally expensive if started from individual pixels. The hybrid approach combines the strengths of both.

**2. Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
Step 1: Define homogeneity predicate H(R).
Step 2: Define merge predicate M(Ri, Rj) = (|m_Ri - m_Rj| <= T_m).
Step 3: SPLIT PHASE: Recursively split any region where H(R) = False.
Step 4: MERGE PHASE: For each pair of adjacent regions Ri, Rj, 
        if M(Ri, Rj) = True, merge them into R' = Ri ∪ Rj.
Step 5: Optionally re-split any merged region that violates H.
Step 6: Repeat until stability.
```

**3. Quadtree Update (2 marks):**
After merging, the two corresponding sibling nodes are deleted and their parent node is reactivated. The tree shrinks upward, which is the inverse of the splitting growth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Region Approach Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the quadtree representation:** A common mark-loss area. Always draw the tree or describe node levels explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the predicates:** Splitting uses $H(R)$ (intra-region), merging uses $M(R_i, R_j)$ (inter-region). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this swap.
> 3. **Omitting termination conditions:** Region growing MUST specify when to stop (no qualifying neighbor). This is worth 1 mark.
> 4. **Not updating the region mean $m_R$ after each addition:** Failing to update $m_R$ is a conceptual error that loses 2 marks in ESE.
> 5. **In worked examples, skipping iterations:** Examiners expect every iteration explicitly written; jumping to the final answer costs 3–4 marks.
> 6. **Watershed confusion:** Students often forget that watershed operates on the **gradient** image, not the original. Deduct 2 marks if confused.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary — re-read before every KTU Module 3 test:**

- ☐ Region approach segments an image into **non-overlapping, homogeneous regions** based on pixel properties.
- ☐ Four main variants: **Growing**, **Splitting**, **Merging**, **Split-and-Merge**; plus **Watershed**.
- ☐ Region Growing is **bottom-up**; needs **seeds**; uses predicate $\vert f(p) - m_R \vert \leq T$.
- ☐ Always **update the mean** $m_R$ after appending a pixel.
- ☐ Region Splitting is **top-down**; uses a **quadtree**; predicate is $\max - \min \leq T$.
- ☐ Region Merging combines adjacent regions with $\vert m_{R_i} - m_{R_j} \vert \leq T_m$.
- ☐ Split-and-Merge is a **hybrid** algorithm; both predicates are required.
- ☐ **Watershed** operates on the **gradient magnitude**; uses marker-controlled flood-fill to avoid over-segmentation.
- ☐ **8-connectivity** gives smoother region boundaries; **4-connectivity** is stricter (less leakage).
- ☐ Variance can be computed efficiently as $\sigma_R^2 = \overline{f^2} - (m_R)^2$.
- ☐ In robot vision, region-based segmentation feeds the **perception → planning → action** pipeline.
- ☐ All four region-based techniques produce **closed contours**, unlike edge-based methods.
- ☐ Threshold $T$ is the most important tunable parameter — too small causes over-segmentation, too large causes under-segmentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering Techniques - Hierarchical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clustering Techniques - Hierarchical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Hierarchical Clustering** is an unsupervised pattern recognition technique that builds a nested hierarchy of clusters by iteratively merging (agglomerative) or splitting (divisive) groups of data points based on a defined similarity/dissimilarity measure. The result is represented as a tree-based structure called a **dendrogram**, which encodes the multi-scale grouping of data without requiring the number of clusters *k* to be pre-specified.

In the context of **robot vision and image processing**, hierarchical clustering is widely used for:
- **Image segmentation** (grouping similar pixels/regions in feature space)
- **Object recognition** (organizing feature descriptors into class hierarchies)
- **Scene understanding** (creating layered representations of visual scenes)

### 1.2 Two Fundamental Strategies

| Strategy | Direction | Also Known As | Strategy Logic |
|----------|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Agglomerative** | Bottom-Up | AGNES (Agglomerative Nesting) | Start with each point as its own cluster; iteratively merge the two closest clusters |
| **Divisive** | Top-Down | DIANA (Divisive Analysis) | Start with all points in one cluster; iteratively split the most heterogeneous cluster |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree of Animals

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy: The Linnaean Taxonomy**
> Imagine you are a biologist organizing animals. You have individual specimens: a sparrow, an eagle, a robin, a shark, a salmon, a lizard.
>
> 1. First, you notice the sparrow, eagle, and robin are similar → you merge them into **"Birds"**.
> 2. You then merge the shark and salmon into **"Fish"**.
> 3. Now you compare **"Birds"** vs **"Fish"** vs the **lizard** — you merge birds and lizard into **"Land/Air Vertebrates"** (closer to each other than to fish).
> 4. Finally, you merge everything into **"Vertebrates"**.
>
> Each merge is recorded with a "height" (the dissimilarity at which it occurred). This tree of merges is a **dendrogram** — the exact output of agglomerative hierarchical clustering. If you "cut" the tree at a chosen height, you obtain a specific number of clusters, just like grouping all animals at the level of "Class" or "Order" gives different cluster counts.

### 1.4 Why Hierarchical Clustering Matters in Robot Vision

- **No pre-specification of *k***: Unlike k-means, the number of clusters is discovered from data.
- **Deterministic output**: Given the same distance + linkage, results are reproducible (k-means can vary with initialization).
- **Multi-resolution analysis**: A single dendrogram provides clustering at every level of granularity — useful for autonomous navigation where objects may need to be grouped at different scales (e.g., lanes vs. intersections).
- **Natural for taxonomies**: Object categories in robot vision are inherently hierarchical (e.g., "Vehicle → Car → Sedan").

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Agglomerative Algorithm — Operational Steps

1. **Initialize**: Assign each of the *n* data points to its own singleton cluster. Total clusters = *n*.
2. **Compute Distance Matrix**: Build an *n × n* matrix of pairwise distances between all clusters (initially, all points).
3. **Merge**: Find the pair of clusters $(C_i, C_j)$ with the **minimum inter-cluster distance** and merge them into a new cluster $C_{ij}$.
4. **Update Matrix**: Recompute distances from the new cluster $C_{ij}$ to all remaining clusters using the chosen **linkage criterion**.
5. **Repeat** steps 3–4 until only **one cluster** remains (containing all points).
6. **Output**: The dendrogram encoding the sequence and height of every merge.

> [!NOTE] **Complexity Snapshot**
> Time complexity is $O(n^3)$ in the naive form and $O(n^2 \log n)$ with a priority queue. Space complexity is $O(n^2)$ for the distance matrix. Suitable for small-to-medium datasets (e.g., a few thousand image feature points).

### 2.2 Distance Metrics Between Two Points

For two feature vectors $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_d)$:

**Euclidean Distance (L2 norm)** — most common in vision:

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - y_i)^2}$$

**Manhattan Distance (L1 norm)** — robust to outliers:

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

**Minkowski Distance (Lp norm)** — generalized form:

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$$

**Chebyshev Distance (L∞)** — used in object tracking with bounding boxes:

$$d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \max_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

### 2.3 Linkage Criteria — How to Measure Distance Between Two Clusters

Let $C_a$ and $C_b$ be two clusters. The linkage function $L(C_a, C_b)$ determines their distance:

#### (a) Single Linkage (MIN)

$$L_{\text{single}}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a,\, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$

- Distance = closest pair of points. Tends to create **elongated, chain-like clusters** (chaining effect).

#### (b) Complete Linkage (MAX)

$$L_{\text{complete}}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a,\, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$

- Distance = farthest pair. Produces **compact, tight, spherical clusters**.

#### (c) Average Linkage (UPGMA)

$$L_{\text{avg}}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \, \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a} \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$

- Average of all pairwise distances. A compromise between single and complete.

#### (d) Centroid Linkage

$$L_{\text{centroid}}(C_a, C_b) = d(\boldsymbol{\mu}_a, \boldsymbol{\mu}_b)$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_a$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_b$ are the centroids. Cluster **inversions** can occur in the dendrogram (a known drawback).

#### (e) Ward's Linkage (Minimum Variance)

$$\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \, d^2(\boldsymbol{\mu}_a, \boldsymbol{\mu}_b)$$

- Minimizes the **increase in total within-cluster variance** upon merging. Excellent for compact, equal-sized clusters — widely used in image segmentation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Use |
|---------------|------------|-------------|
| $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Distance between two feature vectors | Pairwise similarity |
| $L(C_a, C_b)$ | Linkage distance between two clusters | Merging criterion |
| $\boldsymbol{\mu}_c$ | Centroid of cluster $C$ | Centroid / Ward linkage |
| $\Delta(C_a, C_b)$ | Ward's merge cost (variance increase) | Optimal compact cluster growth |
| $h$ (dendrogram height) | Dissimilarity at which merge occurs | Cut-off threshold for *k* |
| $\vert C \vert$ | Cardinality (number of points) of cluster $C$ | Normalization in avg/Ward |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility in Robot Vision**
> In **image segmentation**, pixels are mapped to a feature space (color: RGB/HSV; texture: Gabor/LBP; position: $(x,y)$). Hierarchical clustering groups pixels — then by cutting the dendrogram at height $h$, the image is partitioned into $k$ regions. Ward's linkage is the default choice in many medical imaging pipelines (e.g., tumor segmentation in MRI).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 Worked Example — Agglomerative Clustering with Single Linkage

**Problem (KTU-style):**
Given 5 two-dimensional points in a robot vision feature space (e.g., $(x, y)$ coordinates of detected corner features), perform agglomerative hierarchical clustering using **single linkage** with Euclidean distance. Draw the dendrogram and identify clusters at cut-off height $h = 2.0$.

| Point | $x$ | $y$ |
|-------|-----|-----|
| $P_1$ | 0   | 0   |
| $P_2$ | 1   | 1   |
| $P_3$ | 2   | 0   |
| $P_4$ | 8   | 8   |
| $P_5$ | 9   | 9   |

#### Step 1: Compute Initial Pairwise Euclidean Distance Matrix

Using $d(P_i, P_j) = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$, all 10 unique pairs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{2^2 + 0^2} = 2.000 \\
d(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{8^2 + 8^2} = 8\sqrt{2} \approx 11.314 \\
d(P_1, P_5) &= \sqrt{9^2 + 9^2} = 9\sqrt{2} \approx 12.728 \\
d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \\
d(P_2, P_4) &= \sqrt{7^2 + 7^2} = 7\sqrt{2} \approx 9.899 \\
d(P_2, P_5) &= \sqrt{8^2 + 8^2} = 8\sqrt{2} \approx 11.314 \\
d(P_3, P_4) &= \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = 10.000 \\
d(P_3, P_5) &= \sqrt{7^2 + 9^2} = \sqrt{130} \approx 11.402 \\
d(P_4, P_5) &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Identify the Minimum Distance

The minimum value $\approx 1.414$ appears at three pairs: $(P_1, P_2)$, $(P_2, P_3)$, and $(P_4, P_5)$. Using single linkage, we merge all such pairs at the **same height** $h_1 = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

Mergers at $h = 1.414$:
- Merge $P_1$ and $P_2$ → cluster $C_{12}$
- Merge $P_4$ and $P_5$ → cluster $C_{45}$

#### Step 3: Recompute the Distance Matrix Using Single Linkage

Remaining clusters: $C_{12} = \{P_1, P_2\}$, $P_3$, $C_{45} = \{P_4, P_5\}$.

Single linkage = minimum distance between any member pair:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{single}}(C_{12}, P_3) &= \min\bigl(d(P_1, P_3),\, d(P_2, P_3)\bigr) = \min(2.000,\, 1.414) = 1.414 \\
L_{\text{single}}(C_{12}, C_{45}) &= \min\bigl(d(P_1, P_4),\, d(P_1, P_5),\, d(P_2, P_4),\, d(P_2, P_5)\bigr) = \min(11.314, 12.728, 9.899, 11.314) = 9.899 \\
L_{\text{single}}(P_3, C_{45}) &= \min\bigl(d(P_3, P_4),\, d(P_3, P_5)\bigr) = \min(10.000,\, 11.402) = 10.000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Next Merge

The minimum is $L_{\text{single}}(C_{12}, P_3) = 1.414$ at height $h_2 = 1.414$.
- Merge $C_{12}$ and $P_3$ → cluster $C_{123} = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$.

#### Step 5: Final Merge

Now we have $C_{123}$ and $C_{45}$. Their single-linkage distance is the minimum of all cross-pairs, which we already computed as **9.899** (the $d(P_2, P_4)$ term).

- Final merge: $C_{123} \cup C_{45}$ at $h_3 = 9.899$.

#### Step 6: Dendrogram

```
Height
  10.0 ┤                  ┌──────────┐
       │                  │          │
   9.9 ┤                  │          ┌──────────────┐
       │                  │          │              │
   2.0 ┤    ┌────────┐    │          │              │
       │    │        │    │          │              │
  1.41 ┤  ──┴──┐  ───┴──┐ │          │              │
       │  P1   P2   P3   └─┘          │              │
  1.41 ┤  ───────────────  P4   P5 ───┘              │
       │                                               │
   0.0 ┴───────────────────────────────────────────────
```

#### Step 7: Cutting at $h = 2.0$

A horizontal cut at $h = 2.0$ crosses **two vertical lines** in the dendrogram → yields **$k = 2$ clusters**:
- **Cluster A**: $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$
- **Cluster B**: $\{P_4, P_5\}$

This matches the natural grouping of the data (a tight low-$x$ cluster and a tight high-$x$ cluster), validating the algorithm.

### 3.2 Sensitivity Analysis — Effect of Linkage Choice

Using the **same 5 points** with different linkages:

| Linkage | Merger Order | Resulting Clusters at $h = 2.0$ |
|---------|-------------|----------------------------------|
| Single | $P_1$-$P_2$-$P_3$ chain, then $P_4$-$P_5$ | Two tight clusters (correct) |
| Complete | $P_1$-$P_2$ then $P_4$-$P_5$ (since $d(P_1, P_3) = 2 > d(P_1, P_2) = 1.414$); then $P_3$ joins the $\{P_1, P_2\}$ group at $h = 2.0$ | Two clusters (correct, but $P_3$ joins later) |
| Average | Merge distances averaged — produces a result in between single and complete | Generally two clusters |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Pitfall to Note**
> A common student error is to **not update the distance matrix** after a merge. Always recompute inter-cluster distances using the linkage formula — the matrix is dynamic, not static.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Agglomerative Clustering Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start with N data points]) --> B[Initialize: each point as a singleton cluster]
    B --> C[Compute full N x N pairwise distance matrix]
    C --> D{Find pair of clusters with minimum inter-cluster distance}
    D --> E[Merge the two closest clusters into a new cluster]
    E --> F[Update distance matrix using the chosen linkage criterion]
    F --> G{Only one cluster remaining?}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Construct the dendrogram from merge history]
    H --> I([Cut dendrogram at desired height h to obtain k clusters])
    I --> J([End])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Linkage Criterion Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Two Clusters Ca and Cb] --> B{Single Linkage}
    A --> C{Complete Linkage}
    A --> D{Average Linkage}
    A --> E{Centroid Linkage}
    A --> F{Wards Linkage}
    B --> B1[Closest pair of points min distance]
    C --> C1[Farthest pair of points max distance]
    D --> D1[Mean of all pairwise distances]
    E --> E1[Distance between centroids]
    F --> F1[Minimizes within cluster variance increase]
    B1 --> G1[Chaining effect prone to noise]
    C1 --> G2[Compact spherical clusters]
    D1 --> G3[Compromise between single and complete]
    E1 --> G4[Fast and simple but inversions possible]
    F1 --> G5[Best for vision segmentation equal sized clusters]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Clustering in a Robot Vision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1[Image Acquisition] --> I2[Preprocessing and Noise Removal]
    I2 --> I3[Feature Extraction: SIFT, HOG, Color, Texture]
    I3 --> I4[Feature Space: N x d matrix]
    I4 --> I5[Compute Pairwise Distance Matrix]
    I5 --> I6[Agglomerative Clustering Engine]
    I6 --> I7[Dendrogram Construction]
    I7 --> I8[Cut at Threshold h to get k Segments]
    I8 --> I9[Post-processing: Region Labeling]
    I9 --> I10[Robot Decision: Object Recognition or Path Planning]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation**
> The pipeline above shows where hierarchical clustering slots into a complete vision system. The clustering engine takes raw features as input and produces structured groupings that downstream modules (object recognition, scene parsing) consume for decision-making.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Differentiate between agglomerative and divisive hierarchical clustering. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Agglomerative | Divisive |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| **Approach** | Bottom-up: starts with *n* singleton clusters, iteratively merges | Top-down: starts with one cluster, iteratively splits |
| **Decision at each step** | Choose closest pair to merge | Choose most heterogeneous cluster to split |
| **Time complexity** | $O(n^3)$ naive; $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimized | $O(2^n)$ naive (much worse) |
| **Advantage** | Simpler, widely used, efficient with priority queues | Globally aware — considers entire dataset before splitting |
| **Disadvantage** | Myopic — local merges may not be globally optimal | Computationally expensive; finding optimal split is NP-hard |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Define a dendrogram. Explain how the number of clusters is determined from it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dendrogram** is a binary tree diagram that records the sequence of merges (for agglomerative) or splits (for divisive) in hierarchical clustering. The **vertical axis** represents the **dissimilarity/distance at which a merge occurs** (the height), and the **horizontal axis** lists the data points. To determine the number of clusters $k$, a **horizontal cut** is made at a chosen dissimilarity threshold $h$:
- Every vertical line that the cut intersects defines one cluster.
- The number of intersected vertical lines = $k$.
- Choosing a **larger $h$** yields **fewer, larger clusters**; choosing a **smaller $h$** yields **more, finer clusters**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the **single linkage** and **complete linkage** methods used in hierarchical clustering, with their mathematical formulations. Discuss the **chaining effect** of single linkage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following 4 points in a 2D feature space: $A(0, 0)$, $B(2, 0)$, $C(5, 4)$, $D(6, 5)$. Apply agglomerative clustering using **single linkage** with Euclidean distance. Show the **distance matrix evolution** at each step and draw the **dendrogram**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Single vs Complete Linkage (7 marks):**

- **Single Linkage (MIN):** Distance between clusters $C_a$ and $C_b$ is the minimum distance between any point in $C_a$ and any point in $C_b$:

$$L_{\text{single}}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$

- **Complete Linkage (MAX):** Distance is the maximum between any cross-cluster pair:

$$L_{\text{complete}}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$

- **Chaining Effect:** Because single linkage uses the minimum, it can "bridge" two clusters through a single chain of close intermediate points, producing **elongated, straggly clusters** even when natural groups are compact. It is also **sensitive to noise** — a single noisy bridge can fuse two distinct clusters.
- **Complete Linkage** is more robust to noise and produces **compact, equal-diameter clusters**, but it tends to break large natural clusters and is biased toward discovering spherical groups.

**(b) Worked Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initial distance matrix** (using $d = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A, B) &= \sqrt{4 + 0} = 2.00 \\
d(A, C) &= \sqrt{25 + 16} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40 \\
d(A, D) &= \sqrt{36 + 25} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81 \\
d(B, C) &= \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5.00 \\
d(B, D) &= \sqrt{16 + 25} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40 \\
d(C, D) &= \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41
\end{aligned}
$$

|        | A    | B   | C    | D    |
|--------|------|-----|------|------|
| **A**  | 0    | 2.00 | 6.40 | 7.81 |
| **B**  | 2.00 | 0   | 5.00 | 6.40 |
| **C**  | 6.40 | 5.00 | 0   | 1.41 |
| **D**  | 7.81 | 6.40 | 1.41 | 0   |

**Step 2 — First merge:** Minimum is $d(C, D) = 1.41$. Merge $C$ and $D$ into cluster $C_{CD}$ at $h_1 = 1.41$.

**Step 3 — Updated matrix** (single linkage = min of cross-pairs):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{single}}(A, B) &= 2.00 \\
L_{\text{single}}(A, C_{CD}) &= \min(6.40, 7.81) = 6.40 \\
L_{\text{single}}(B, C_{CD}) &= \min(5.00, 6.40) = 5.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Second merge:** Minimum is $L(A, B) = 2.00$. Merge $A$ and $B$ into $C_{AB}$ at $h_2 = 2.00$.

**Step 5 — Final merge:** $L_{\text{single}}(C_{AB}, C_{CD}) = \min(6.40, 5.00) = 5.00$ at $h_3 = 5.00$.

**Dendrogram:**

```
Height
  5.00  ┤           ┌──────────────┐
        │           │              │
  2.00  ┤    ┌──────┘              │
        │    │                     │
  1.41  ┤    │               ┌─────┘
        │   A    B         C     D
  0.00  ┴───────────────────────────
```

**Conclusion:** Cutting at $h = 3.0$ gives $k = 2$ clusters: $\{A, B\}$ and $\{C, D\}$ — matching the natural data structure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Describe the **Ward's linkage** method for hierarchical clustering. Derive the merge cost expression and explain why it is preferred for **image segmentation** in robot vision applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the complete **agglomerative clustering pipeline** as applied to **image segmentation**. Discuss the role of **feature selection** (color, texture, position) and the **dendrogram cut-off** strategy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ward's Linkage (7 marks):**

Ward's method chooses at each step the pair of clusters whose merger **minimizes the increase in total within-cluster sum of squared errors (SSE)**.

For two clusters $C_a$ and $C_b$ with centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_a$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_b$ and sizes $\vert C_a \vert$, $\vert C_b \vert$:

$$\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \, \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \; d^2(\boldsymbol{\mu}_a, \boldsymbol{\mu}_b)$$

**Derivation Sketch:** The total SSE is $SSE = \sum_{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C} \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_C \vert\vert^2$. When $C_a$ and $C_b$ merge, SSE increases by exactly $\Delta(C_a, C_b)$ above.

**Why preferred for image segmentation:**
- Produces **compact, balanced, near-spherical regions** — visually desirable.
- Minimizes intra-region variance → uniform intensity/texture within segments.
- Robust to outliers compared to single linkage.
- In robot vision, produces regions that map well to **physical objects** (e.g., a single road surface, a single sky region).

**(b) Image Segmentation Pipeline (7 marks):**

```
   Image                Features
 ┌──────┐            ┌──────────┐
 │ RGB  │  ────►     │ x, y, R, │
 │ Image│  extract   │ G, B,    │ ────►  Distance
 │      │            │ texture  │        Matrix
 └──────┘            └──────────┘            │
                                            ▼
                                  Agglomerative
                                   Clustering
                                         │
                                         ▼
                                     Dendrogram
                                         │
                                  Cut at height h
                                         │
                                         ▼
                                   Segmented
                                    Image
```

**Feature Selection:**
- **Color features** (R, G, B or H, S, V): group pixels of similar appearance.
- **Texture features** (Gabor filter responses, LBP histograms): distinguish regions with similar color but different texture (e.g., grass vs. road).
- **Position features** $(x, y)$: encourage **spatial contiguity** — neighboring pixels are more likely in the same segment.

**Dendrogram Cut-off Strategy:**
- Choose $h$ such that the number of resulting segments $k$ matches the **expected object count** in the scene.
- Use **consistency-based** or **gap-statistic** criteria to select $h$ automatically.
- For real-time robot vision, **$h$ is often fixed empirically** based on the target application.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the distance matrix** after each merge — partial credit only.
> 2. **Confusing linkage with metric** — linkage is *between clusters*, metric is *between points*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Not labeling dendrogram heights** — marks are lost for ambiguity. Always show the height $h$ at every merge.
> 4. **Claiming hierarchical clustering is faster than k-means** — it is **slower** ($O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$); the advantage is *not needing to pre-specify $k$*, not speed.
> 5. **Mixing up agglomerative with divisive direction** — agglomerative merges *upward*, divisive splits *downward*.
> 6. **Not specifying the linkage criterion** in answers — always state "using single/complete/average/Ward's linkage" explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Hierarchical clustering** produces a **dendrogram** — a tree of merges/splits.
> - **Agglomerative (AGNES)**: bottom-up, merge closest pair; **Divisive (DIANA)**: top-down, split most heterogeneous cluster.
> - **No need to pre-specify $k$** — the user chooses by cutting the dendrogram at height $h$.
> - **Distance metrics**: Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, Chebyshev. Euclidean is the default in vision.
> - **Linkage criteria**:
>   - **Single (MIN)**: chaining effect, elongated clusters, sensitive to noise.
>   - **Complete (MAX)**: compact clusters, robust to noise.
>   - **Average (UPGMA)**: compromise.
>   - **Centroid**: simple, fast, but **inversions possible**.
>   - **Ward's**: minimizes SSE increase, **best for image segmentation**.
> - **Time complexity**: $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimized. Not scalable to millions of points.
> - **Applications in robot vision**: image segmentation, object recognition hierarchy, feature descriptor clustering (e.g., visual bag-of-words for SLAM).
> - **Always** state the distance metric **and** linkage criterion in exam answers.
> - **Always** recompute the distance matrix after each merge using the linkage formula.
> - **Deterministic output** (unlike k-means) — same input + parameters → same dendrogram every time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** K-Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-Means Clustering (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> K-Means is an **unsupervised, iterative, centroid-based partitioning clustering algorithm** that divides a dataset (or image pixel feature space) into **K non-overlapping, exhaustive subsets (clusters)** by minimizing the **sum of squared Euclidean distances** between each data point and its nearest cluster centroid. In Image Processing and Robot Vision, K-Means is predominantly employed for **unsupervised image segmentation**, **color quantization**, **feature space clustering**, and **object region grouping** in both 2D images and 3D point clouds.

The algorithm was first published by **Stuart Lloyd** in 1957 (originally for pulse-code modulation) and independently by **E. W. Forgy** in 1965, hence often referred to in literature as the **Lloyd–Forgy algorithm** or simply the **K-Means procedure**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bins of Mixed Marbles"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Sorting Marbles into K Boxes**
> Imagine you are given a giant sack containing thousands of marbles of many different colors (red, blue, green, yellow) all mixed together. Your job is to sort them into exactly **K = 4 boxes**, one box per color family, **without being told** which color belongs in which box (hence "unsupervised").
>
> **How you would do it manually (this is exactly K-Means):**
> 1. You randomly place 4 empty boxes on the table and place a "guess marker" (centroid) inside each one.
> 2. You pick up every marble one by one and place it in the **box whose guess marker is closest in color** to it.
> 3. After all marbles are placed, you compute the **average color** of marbles inside each box and move the guess marker to that average.
> 4. Since the markers moved, you re-check every marble: it might now belong to a different box.
> 5. You repeat steps 2–4 until no marble wants to switch boxes. **Done!**
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Marbles** = Image pixels (each pixel is a 3-D vector in RGB, or 5-D in RGB+xy, etc.)
> - **Boxes** = Clusters
> - **K** = Number of clusters (must be pre-chosen)
> - **Guess marker** = Centroid (mean vector of cluster members)
> - **Closest in color** = Minimum Euclidean distance in feature space

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Cluster | $C_i$ | A subset of data points grouped together |
| Centroid | $\mu_i$ | Mean vector of all points in cluster $C_i$ |
| Number of clusters | $K$ | User-defined hyperparameter |
| Feature vector | $\mathbf{x}_j$ | A pixel represented as a vector (e.g., RGB) |
| Objective function | $J$ | Within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) |
| Convergence | — | Condition when centroids no longer move |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical / Engineering Constants Relevant to K-Means in Vision:**
> - **Euclidean Norm** is the default distance metric; in color spaces like **CIE-Lab**, perceptual uniformity is better preserved.
> - **Empty cluster** problem: a centroid may end up with no assigned points (handled by re-initialization).
> - **Convergence is guaranteed**, but only to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global optimum.

## 1.4 Why K-Means Matters in Robot Vision

In a robot vision pipeline, K-Means is typically applied at the **mid-level processing stage**, after low-level operations (filtering, edge detection) and before high-level recognition (object detection, classification). Common use-cases include:

- **Road / lane segmentation** in autonomous driving
- **Skin / non-skin region segmentation** in human-robot interaction
- **Color-based object tracking** in industrial pick-and-place robots
- **Point cloud segmentation** in 3D SLAM pipelines
- **Dominant color extraction** for visual servoing feedback

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithm Logic — Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> K-Means operates on a **two-phase iterative loop**:
> **Phase A — Assignment (Expectation step)**: assign each point to the nearest centroid.
> **Phase B — Update (Maximization step)**: recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points.
> These phases are alternated until a convergence criterion is met.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Input Acquisition**: Obtain dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ where each $\mathbf{x}_j \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (e.g., $d=3$ for RGB pixels).
2. **K Initialization**: Choose the number of clusters $K$ (typically by elbow method, silhouette score, or domain knowledge).
3. **Centroid Seeding**: Initialize $K$ centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ — randomly, by **K-Means++** (preferred), or by **Forgy method**.
4. **REPEAT** (Iteration $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$):
   - **Step 4a — Assignment Phase (E-step)**:
     For every data point $\mathbf{x}_j$, compute its distance to each centroid and assign it to the **closest** centroid.
   - **Step 4b — Update Phase (M-step)**:
     For each cluster $C_i^{(t)}$, recompute the centroid as the **arithmetic mean** of all points currently assigned to it.
   - **Step 4c — Convergence Check**:
     Stop when centroids do not move (or move below a small threshold $\varepsilon$), or when the objective $J$ stabilizes, or when a maximum iteration count is reached.
5. **Output**: Final cluster labels $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_N\}$ and final centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K\}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Euclidean distance?** It is the most analytically tractable, rotation-invariant metric and matches the geometry of isotropic Gaussian assumptions on clusters. For color clustering, however, $\Delta E$ in CIE-Lab is preferred perceptually.
- **Why the mean as centroid?** The mean uniquely **minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances** within a cluster. This is a consequence of the first-order optimality condition $\partial J / \partial \mu_i = 0$.
- **Why does it converge?** Each iteration is guaranteed to **monotonically decrease** (or maintain) the objective $J$, and $J$ is bounded below by 0. With a finite dataset, the number of possible cluster assignments is finite, so the algorithm must terminate.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorize these equations; they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Euclidean distance (L2 norm) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = \sqrt{\sum_{p=1}^{d}(x_p - \mu_p)^2}$ | $\mathbf{x}, \mu \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Unit-less (depends on feature scaling) |
| 2 | Squared Euclidean distance | $d^2(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = \sum_{p=1}^{d}(x_p - \mu_p)^2$ | — | Computationally faster (no square root) |
| 3 | Objective (WCSS / distortion) | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | $J \ge 0$ | To be **minimized** |
| 4 | Centroid update rule | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x}$ | $\vert C_i \vert$ = cluster size | Vector form |
| 5 | Hard assignment (E-step) | $y_j = \arg\min_{i \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | $y_j$ = cluster label | Yields integer index |
| 6 | Inertia / within-cluster variance | $W = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i}\Vert\mathbf{x}-\mu_i\Vert^2$ | — | Used in elbow method |
| 7 | Time complexity (per iteration) | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot K \cdot d \cdot I)$ | $I$ = # iterations | Linear in $N$ and $K$ |
| 8 | K-Means++ seeding probability | $P(\mu = \mathbf{x}) = \frac{D(\mathbf{x})^2}{\sum_{\mathbf{x}'}D(\mathbf{x}')^2}$ | $D(\mathbf{x})$ = min dist to existing centroid | Improves init quality |
| 9 | Elbow method criterion | Choose $K$ where $J(K)$ shows "elbow" kink | Plot $J$ vs $K$ | Heuristic |
| 10 | Silhouette coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i)-a(i)}{\max\{a(i),b(i)\}}$ | $a(i)$ = intra-cluster, $b(i)$ = nearest inter | $s \in [-1, 1]$, higher is better |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Many students write the absolute value / norm with the vertical pipe symbol `\vert x \vert` in their answer sheets. This is fine, but **inside markdown tables in your prep notes, never use raw `|` for norms** — it breaks table formatting. Always use `\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert` or `\vert x \vert` typeset.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | K-Means Application |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Lane / road surface segmentation in RGB |
| Medical Imaging | Tumor pixel grouping in MRI / CT scans |
| Industrial Robotics | Color-based part sorting in conveyor systems |
| Agricultural Robots | Ripeness clustering of fruits (vision-based harvesting) |
| Surveillance | Foreground / background clustering for motion regions |
| Satellite Imaging | Land-cover classification |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Mean Minimizes Within-Cluster SSE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation is **board-favorite** — KTU examiners frequently ask "Prove that the K-Means centroid update rule minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares."

**Given:** A cluster $C_i$ contains $n_i$ points $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_{n_i}\}$ and we seek the centroid $\mu_i$ that minimizes the within-cluster squared error $J_i$.

$$J_i(\mu_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} \Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$$

**Step 1** — Expand the squared norm using the dot product identity:

$$\Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert^2 = (\mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i)^\top (\mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i) = \mathbf{x}_j^\top \mathbf{x}_j - 2\mathbf{x}_j^\top \mu_i + \mu_i^\top \mu_i$$

**Step 2** — Substitute back into the sum over the cluster:

$$J_i(\mu_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j^\top \mathbf{x}_j \;-\; 2\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j^\top \mu_i \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mu_i^\top \mu_i$$

**Step 3** — Since $\mu_i^\top \mu_i$ is a scalar, $\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mu_i^\top\mu_i = n_i\,\mu_i^\top\mu_i$:

$$J_i(\mu_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j^\top \mathbf{x}_j \;-\; 2\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j\Bigr)^\top \mu_i \;+\; n_i\,\mu_i^\top \mu_i$$

**Step 4** — Take the gradient with respect to $\mu_i$ and set to zero (first-order necessary condition for a minimum):

$$\nabla_{\mu_i} J_i = -2\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j \;+\; 2n_i \mu_i = 0$$

**Step 5** — Solve for $\mu_i$:

$$2n_i \mu_i = 2\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mu_i^{\ast} = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal centroid is the **arithmetic mean** of all points in the cluster. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Second-order check (convexity):** The Hessian of $J_i$ is $\nabla^2_{\mu_i} J_i = 2n_i \mathbf{I}$, which is positive-definite. Hence $J_i$ is **strictly convex**, guaranteeing the mean is the **global** (not just local) minimizer for a fixed cluster assignment.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Favorite Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board frequently provides a **2-D point dataset** and asks the student to perform **2 iterations of K-Means** by hand. Below is a complete, valuation-grade worked example.

**Problem:** Cluster the 2-D dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(1,1),(1,2),(2,1),(8,8),(9,9),(8,9)\}$ into $K=2$ clusters. Initial centroids: $\mu_1^{(0)} = (1,1)$ and $\mu_2^{(0)} = (8,8)$. Run **2 iterations**.

### Iteration 1

**E-step: Compute squared Euclidean distance from each point to each centroid.**

For each point $\mathbf{x}_j = (x_1, x_2)$:

$$d^2(\mathbf{x}_j, \mu_i) = (x_1 - \mu_{i,1})^2 + (x_2 - \mu_{i,2})^2$$

| Point $\mathbf{x}_j$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_1 = (1,1)$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2 = (8,8)$ | Assigned to |
|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| $(1,1)$ | $0$ | $98$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $1$ | $90$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,1)$ | $1$ | $90$ | $C_1$ |
| $(8,8)$ | $98$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9,9)$ | $128$ | $2$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,9)$ | $106$ | $1$ | $C_2$ |

So after Iteration 1: $C_1 = \{(1,1),(1,2),(2,1)\}$ and $C_2 = \{(8,8),(9,9),(8,9)\}$.

**M-step: Update centroids.**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\Bigl[(1,1)+(1,2)+(2,1)\Bigr] = \frac{1}{3}(4,4) = (1.333, 1.333)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\Bigl[(8,8)+(9,9)+(8,9)\Bigr] = \frac{1}{3}(25,26) = (8.333, 8.667)$$

### Iteration 2

**E-step: Reassign with new centroids.**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1^{(1)} = (1.333, 1.333)$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2^{(1)} = (8.333, 8.667)$ | Assigned to |
|-------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|-------------|
| $(1,1)$ | $0.222$ | $118.222$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1,2)$ | $0.555$ | $110.555$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,1)$ | $0.555$ | $110.555$ | $C_1$ |
| $(8,8)$ | $111.111$ | $0.555$ | $C_2$ |
| $(9,9)$ | $128.444$ | $1.111$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,9)$ | $109.444$ | $0.111$ | $C_2$ |

Cluster assignments **unchanged** ⇒ algorithm has **converged** in 2 iterations.

**Final Result:**
- $C_1 = \{(1,1),(1,2),(2,1)\}$ with centroid $(1.333, 1.333)$
- $C_2 = \{(8,8),(9,9),(8,9)\}$ with centroid $(8.333, 8.667)$
- Final objective: $J = 0.222 + 0.555 + 0.555 + 0.555 + 1.111 + 0.111 = 3.111$

## 3.3 Convergence Guarantee — Analytical Argument

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (K-Means Convergence):** The K-Means algorithm converges in a **finite number of iterations** to a (local) minimum of $J$.

**Proof Sketch (KTU-style):**

1. The objective $J$ is **bounded below** by $0$ (sum of squared distances is non-negative).
2. In the **E-step**, each point is reassigned to the nearest centroid, which **cannot increase** $J$ for fixed centroids.
3. In the **M-step**, the new centroid is the cluster mean, which (by the derivation in §3.1) **minimizes** $J$ for fixed cluster assignments.
4. Therefore, $J$ is **monotonically non-increasing** across iterations: $J^{(t+1)} \le J^{(t)}$.
5. Since there are only $K^N$ possible cluster-assignment configurations (a finite set), and $J$ strictly decreases (or stays equal) for each new configuration, the algorithm cannot cycle forever.
6. Hence, **convergence in finite steps is guaranteed**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> Convergence is to a **local minimum**, **not global**. Different initializations may yield different final clusterings. The **K-Means++** initialization scheme reduces (but does not eliminate) this risk.

## 3.4 K-Means++ Initialization (Important Variant)

To improve initialization quality, **K-Means++** seeds centroids **sequentially with probability proportional to squared distance** from already-chosen centroids. This spreads initial centroids out and typically yields lower final $J$.

Probability of choosing point $\mathbf{x}$ as the next centroid:

$$P(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{D(\mathbf{x})^2}{\sum_{\mathbf{x}' \in \mathcal{D}} D(\mathbf{x}')^2}$$

where $D(\mathbf{x}) = \min_{i \,:\, \mu_i \text{ already chosen}} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — K-Means Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below traces the **full operational flow** of the K-Means algorithm as a state machine. Each node uses **alphanumeric IDs only** and clean uppercase labels (per protocol).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([START]) --> B1[Input Dataset X and Choose K]
    B1 --> C1[Initialize K Centroids]
    C1 --> D1[Set Iteration Counter t = 0]
    D1 --> E1[Increment t = t + 1]
    E1 --> F1[ASSIGNMENT PHASE: For each point compute distance to all centroids]
    F1 --> G1[Assign point to nearest centroid cluster]
    G1 --> H1[UPDATE PHASE: Recompute each centroid as mean of its cluster]
    H1 --> I1{Convergence Check}
    I1 -- Not Converged --> E1
    I1 -- Converged --> J1[Output Final Clusters and Centroids]
    J1 --> K1[Compute Final Objective J Value]
    K1 --> L1([END])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — K-Means in an Image Segmentation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG[Input Image RGB] --> FE[Feature Extraction: Pixel Vector]
    FE --> FS[Feature Space: N points in R cubed]
    FS --> KM[K-Means Clustering Engine]
    KM --> AS[Assignment Step]
    AS --> UP[Update Step]
    UP --> CK{Converged}
    CK -- No --> AS
    CK -- Yes --> LBL[Cluster Label Map]
    LBL --> SEG[Segmented Image Output]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Convergence Behavior of $J$ vs Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Iteration 0: High J] --> P2[Iteration 1: Sharp Drop in J]
    P2 --> P3[Iteration 2: Moderate Drop]
    P3 --> P4[Iteration 3: Small Drop]
    P4 --> P5[Iteration T: Plateau Reached]
    P5 --> P6[Algorithm Terminates]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Module: Preprocessing] --> M2[Module: Feature Engineering]
    M2 --> M3[Module: K-Means Core]
    M3 --> M4[Module: Post-processing and Label Smoothing]
    M4 --> M5[Module: Vision Decision Layer]
    M3 --> S1[Submodule: K-Means++ Init]
    M3 --> S2[Submodule: Distance Metric L2]
    M3 --> S3[Submodule: Convergence Controller]
    S1 --> M3
    S2 --> M3
    S3 --> M3
```
