# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Define K-Means clustering. State its objective function.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **K-Means clustering** is an unsupervised, iterative algorithm that partitions a dataset of $N$ points into $K$ non-overlapping clusters by minimizing the **within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances (WCSS)**.
>
> **Objective function:**
> $$J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert^2$$
> where $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i}\mathbf{x}$ is the centroid of cluster $C_i$.
>
> *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for objective, 1 for centroid update expression)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. Why is K-Means called unsupervised?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> In **supervised learning**, the algorithm is trained on labeled data (input–output pairs). In **unsupervised learning**, the algorithm discovers structure (groups, patterns) from **unlabeled** data.
>
> K-Means is called unsupervised because:
> 1. It does **not** require pre-labeled training data.
> 2. Cluster labels are **discovered automatically** by minimizing intra-cluster variance.
> 3. The user supplies only $K$ and the feature vectors, not the class identities.
>
> *(3 marks — 1.5 for difference, 1.5 for K-Means justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q3(a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the step-by-step K-Means clustering algorithm with a neat flowchart. State its time complexity.
>
> **Q3(b) [7 Marks]** — Perform 2 iterations of K-Means on the dataset $\{(2,10),(2,5),(8,4),(5,8),(7,5),(6,4)\}$ with $K=2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1=(2,10)$ and $\mu_2=(5,8)$. Show the cluster assignments and updated centroids at each iteration.

---

#### Model Solution to Q3(a):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm Steps:**
> 1. **Choose $K$** — the number of clusters.
> 2. **Initialize** $K$ centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K\}$ (randomly or via K-Means++).
> 3. **Repeat** until convergence:
>    - **Assignment step**: For each data point $\mathbf{x}_j$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is closest:
>      $$y_j = \arg\min_{i} \Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$$
>    - **Update step**: Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points:
>      $$\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x}$$
> 4. **Stop** when centroids no longer change (or change below threshold $\varepsilon$).
>
> **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot K \cdot d \cdot I)$ per run, where $N$ = # points, $K$ = # clusters, $d$ = feature dimension, $I$ = # iterations until convergence.
>
> *(Flowchart: refer to §4.1.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q3(b):

**Iteration 1 — E-step** (compute squared distances to $\mu_1=(2,10)$ and $\mu_2=(5,8)$):

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2$ | Assignment |
|-------|------------------|------------------|------------|
| $(2,10)$ | $0$ | $13$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,5)$ | $25$ | $18$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,4)$ | $72$ | $25$ | $C_2$ |
| $(5,8)$ | $13$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(7,5)$ | $50$ | $13$ | $C_2$ |
| $(6,4)$ | $52$ | $17$ | $C_2$ |

Result: $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $C_2 = \{(2,5),(8,4),(5,8),(7,5),(6,4)\}$.

**Iteration 1 — M-step:**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = (2,10)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{5}\bigl[(2,5)+(8,4)+(5,8)+(7,5)+(6,4)\bigr] = \frac{1}{5}(28,26) = (5.6, 5.2)$$

**Iteration 2 — E-step** (new centroids $\mu_1=(2,10)$ and $\mu_2=(5.6, 5.2)$):

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1=(2,10)$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2=(5.6,5.2)$ | Assignment |
|-------|--------------------------|------------------------------|------------|
| $(2,10)$ | $0$ | $37.0$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,5)$ | $25$ | $13.2$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,4)$ | $72$ | $6.0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(5,8)$ | $13$ | $8.0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(7,5)$ | $50$ | $2.0$ | $C_2$ |
| $(6,4)$ | $52$ | $1.6$ | $C_2$ |

Cluster assignments are **identical to Iteration 1** ⇒ **Convergence reached**.

**Final Result:**
- $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $\mu_1 = (2,10)$
- $C_2 = \{(2,5),(8,4),(5,8),(7,5),(6,4)\}$, $\mu_2 = (5.6, 5.2)$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q4(a) [7 Marks]** — Derive the K-Means centroid update rule from first principles, showing that the arithmetic mean minimizes the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances.
>
> **Q4(b) [7 Marks]** — Discuss the K-Means++ initialization scheme. How does it improve upon random initialization? Mention any two limitations of K-Means.

---

#### Model Solution to Q4(a):

(Refer to the full derivation in §3.1 of these notes.)

> **Summary of the derivation path:**
> 1. Define $J_i(\mu_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} \Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$.
> 2. Expand $\Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$ using the dot-product identity.
> 3. Sum over the cluster; isolate terms in $\mu_i$.
> 4. Set $\nabla_{\mu_i} J_i = 0$.
> 5. Solve: $\mu_i^{\ast} = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\mathbf{x}_j$.
> 6. Verify positive-definite Hessian $\nabla^2 J_i = 2n_i \mathbf{I}$ for global minimum.
>
> *(7 marks — full derivation with all 6 steps)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q4(b):

> **K-Means++ Initialization:**
> 1. Choose the first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from the dataset.
> 2. For each remaining data point $\mathbf{x}$, compute its squared distance $D(\mathbf{x})^2$ to the nearest already-chosen centroid.
> 3. Choose the next centroid $\mu_i$ with probability proportional to $D(\mathbf{x})^2$:
>    $$P(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{D(\mathbf{x})^2}{\sum_{\mathbf{x}'}D(\mathbf{x}')^2}$$
> 4. Repeat steps 2–3 until $K$ centroids have been chosen. Then proceed with standard K-Means.
>
> **Improvement over random initialization:**
> - Spreads initial centroids apart, reducing the probability of poor local minima.
> - Empirically yields **lower final objective $J$** and **faster convergence**.
> - Provides a theoretical approximation guarantee: the initial $J$ is within $\mathcal{O}(\log K)$ of the optimum.
>
> **Two Limitations of K-Means:**
> 1. **Requires pre-specification of $K$.** The correct number of clusters is often unknown a priori.
> 2. **Sensitive to outliers and noise**, since the mean is non-robust. A single faraway point can drag the centroid away from the true cluster center.
> 3. *(Bonus)* Assumes **spherical, equal-sized clusters**; fails for elongated or density-varying clusters (DBSCAN / Gaussian Mixture Models are better).
> 4. *(Bonus)* Convergence is to a **local**, not **global**, minimum.
>
> *(7 marks — 3 for K-Means++ explanation, 2 for improvement, 2 for limitations)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid These to Secure Full Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the squared distance** — many students write $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert$ instead of $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert^2$ in the E-step. The squared form is **computationally equivalent for argmin** and is the standard board notation.
> 2. **Not recomputing distances in every iteration** — once centroids move, the E-step **must** be re-executed. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "convergence" without justification** — state the explicit condition (e.g., "centroids unchanged" or "$\Vert \mu^{(t+1)} - \mu^{(t)} \Vert < 10^{-4}$").
> 4. **Mixing up $\mu_i$ and $C_i$** — $\mu_i$ is the **centroid** (a vector); $C_i$ is the **cluster** (a set). Use distinct, consistent symbols.
> 5. **Hand-computation arithmetic errors** — KTU board re-evaluates each distance; even one wrong squared distance invalidates the entire iteration's mark. **Show all intermediate steps.**
> 6. **Not mentioning empty-cluster handling** — if asked about limitations or edge cases, mention the empty-cluster problem and re-seeding strategy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):**

- **K-Means** = unsupervised, centroid-based, partitioning clustering.
- **Objective**: minimize $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in C_i}\Vert\mathbf{x}-\mu_i\Vert^2$ (WCSS).
- **Two alternating phases**: Assignment (E-step) and Update (M-step).
- **Centroid update**: $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in C_i}\mathbf{x}$ — proved via $\nabla_{\mu_i} J = 0$ and verified strictly convex (Hessian $= 2n_i\mathbf{I}$).
- **Distance metric**: default is **squared Euclidean**; perceptually use $\Delta E$ in **CIE-Lab** for color clustering.
- **K-Means++** seeds centroids proportional to $D(\mathbf{x})^2$ — better than random.
- **Convergence**: guaranteed in finite steps to a **local** minimum (NOT global).
- **Time complexity**: $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot K \cdot d \cdot I)$ per run.
- **Choosing K**: elbow method (plot $J$ vs $K$, pick the kink) or silhouette score $s(i) = \frac{b(i)-a(i)}{\max(a(i),b(i))}$.
- **Limitations**: requires pre-specified $K$; sensitive to outliers; assumes isotropic clusters; local-minimum issue; cannot handle non-convex shapes.
- **Variants to know**: K-Medoids (uses median, robust to outliers), Fuzzy C-Means (soft assignment), Mini-Batch K-Means (scalable), Bisecting K-Means (hierarchical flavor).
- **Vision applications**: image segmentation, color quantization, dominant color extraction, point cloud clustering, visual servoing feedback.
- **Notation discipline**: use $C_i$ for cluster, $\mu_i$ for centroid, $y_j$ for label, $J$ for objective — **do not interchange**.
- **Numerical exam tip**: in hand-computation, always re-state the centroids at the **start of every iteration** and show the **distance table** with squared values.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thresholding - Global Thresholding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thresholding - Global Thresholding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Image Thresholding?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thresholding** is the simplest method of **image segmentation**. It converts a gray-scale image into a binary image by partitioning the pixel set into exactly **two disjoint subsets** — *foreground (object)* and *background* — based on a chosen intensity value called the **Threshold (T)**.

Mathematically, for a given gray-level image $f(x, y)$ with threshold $T$:

$$
g(x, y) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq T \quad (\text{Object / Foreground}) \\
0 & \text{if } f(x, y) < T \quad (\text{Background})
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $f(x, y)$ = intensity of the input pixel at coordinate $(x, y)$
- $T$ = threshold value (range: $0 \leq T \leq L - 1$)
- $L$ = total number of gray levels ($L = 256$ for an 8-bit image)
- $g(x, y)$ = output binary pixel value

---

## 1.2 What is "Global" Thresholding?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Thresholding** is the variant in which a **single, fixed threshold value $T$** is applied to **every pixel** in the *entire image*. The threshold does not change with spatial position — it depends only on the global histogram of the image.

Formally:

$$
T = T(f) \quad \text{(depends only on } f \text{, not on } x \text{ or } y \text{)}
$$

Global thresholding is effective **only when**:
- The image contains a **single dominant object** on a contrasting background.
- The illumination is **uniform** across the scene.
- The histogram exhibits a clear, deep **valley** between two prominent peaks (bimodal).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Bouncer at a Club Door"**
> Imagine a nightclub with a single, strict bouncer. Every person (pixel) who arrives is judged on a single criterion — are you wearing **formal shoes** (intensity $\geq T$) or not?
> - Shoes on → you are allowed inside (pixel becomes **1**, foreground).
> - Shoes off → you are turned away (pixel becomes **0**, background).
>
> The bouncer uses **one fixed rule for everyone** — no exceptions based on age, gender, or where you came from. That single, universal rule is exactly what "**Global**" thresholding means.

**Another Analogy:** A student is graded on a single cut-off of 50 marks. The cut-off is the same for the entire class — irrespective of which subject paper or which student. Anyone scoring $\geq 50$ passes; below 50 fails.

---

## 1.4 Role of the Histogram in Global Thresholding

The image histogram $p(r_k)$ gives the probability (normalized frequency) of each gray level $r_k$:

$$
p(r_k) = \frac{n_k}{MN}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, L-1
$$

Where:
- $n_k$ = number of pixels with intensity $r_k$
- $MN$ = total number of pixels in the image

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** For global thresholding to succeed, the histogram **must be bimodal** — showing **two distinct peaks** (object + background) separated by a **clear valley**. The optimum threshold $T$ is chosen at the **bottom of that valley**.

---

## 1.5 Types of Thresholding (One-line Snapshot)

| Type | Threshold Rule | Use Case |
|------|---------------|----------|
| **Global (Single)** | One $T$ for entire image | Uniform illumination, bimodal histogram |
| **Local / Adaptive** | $T = T(x, y)$ varies locally | Non-uniform illumination |
| **Multiple / Band** | $T_1 \leq f < T_2$ region | More than 2 classes |
| **Dynamic** | $T = T(x, y, f(x, y), N(x, y))$ | Variable neighbourhood statistics |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Global Thresholding

The process is a **decision cascade** applied to every pixel:

1. **Acquire the image** $f(x, y)$ in grayscale.
2. **Compute the histogram** $p(r_k)$ and inspect for bimodality.
3. **Select the threshold value $T$**:
   - Manual inspection of the histogram valley, **OR**
   - Automated via iterative selection, **OR**
   - Maximized via Otsu's between-class variance.
4. **Scan each pixel** $(x, y)$:
   - If $f(x, y) \geq T$ → output **white** (foreground).
   - If $f(x, y) < T$ → output **black** (background).
5. **Store the output** as a binary image $g(x, y)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work?** Segmentation reduces the image to its **most semantically meaningful elements** — object vs. background. By binarizing, downstream algorithms (edge detection, contour tracing, OCR, robot navigation) become **simpler, faster, and more robust**.

---

## 2.2 The Basic Global Threshold Formula

$$
g(x, y) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq T \\
0 & \text{if } f(x, y) < T
\end{cases}
$$

For a histogram-based **single-peak valley pick**:

$$
T = \arg\min_{r \in [0, L-1]} p(r) \quad \text{(choose valley intensity)}
$$

---

## 2.3 Compensation for Non-Uniform Illumination

When illumination is uneven, the histogram loses its bimodality. Gonzalez & Woods propose:

$$
g(x, y) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \dfrac{f(x, y)}{b(x, y)} \geq T \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $b(x, y)$ is an estimate of the **background illumination pattern** (obtained by averaging $f$ with a large kernel).

For log-transformed variants:

$$
g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \log(f(x,y)) - \log(b(x,y)) \geq T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.4 Iterative Selection of the Global Threshold (KTU High-Yield)

This is the most frequently asked global-threshold algorithm. The procedure **converges to an optimal $T$ in 3–6 iterations** for typical bimodal images.

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm: Iterative Global Threshold (Gonzalez & Woods, 4th Edition §10.3.2)**
>
> **Step 1:** Choose an initial estimate of $T$. Default: **mean intensity** of the image,
>
> **Step 2:** Partition the image using $T$:
> - $G_1$ = pixels with intensity $> T$
> - $G_2$ = pixels with intensity $\leq T$
>
> **Step 3:** Compute the **mean gray-level values** $\mu_1$ for $G_1$ and $\mu_2$ for $G_2$:
>
> **Step 4:** Update $T$:
>
> **Step 5:** Repeat Steps 2–4 until the change $|T_{k+1} - T_k| < \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 0.5$ for integer intensities).

The convergence criterion:

$$
\Delta T = \vert T_{k+1} - T_k \vert < \varepsilon
$$

---

## 2.5 Otsu's Method — Maximum Between-Class Variance

For an automated, optimum threshold:

$$
\sigma_B^2(k) = P_1(k) \cdot P_2(k) \cdot [\mu_1(k) - \mu_2(k)]^2
$$

$$
T^* = \arg\max_{k \in [0, L-1]} \, \sigma_B^2(k)
$$

Where:

$$
P_1(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k} p_i, \quad P_2(k) = \sum_{i=k+1}^{L-1} p_i = 1 - P_1(k)
$$

$$
\mu_1(k) = \frac{1}{P_1(k)} \sum_{i=0}^{k} i \cdot p_i, \quad \mu_2(k) = \frac{1}{P_2(k)} \sum_{i=k+1}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Definition** | **Range / Units** |
|------------|-------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| $f(x, y)$ | Input pixel intensity | Original gray-scale image | $0$ to $255$ (8-bit) |
| $T$ | Global threshold value | Chosen constant for entire image | $0 \leq T \leq L-1$ |
| $g(x, y)$ | Output binary pixel | $1$ if $f \geq T$, else $0$ | $\{0, 1\}$ |
| $L$ | Number of gray levels | Total intensity quantization | Typically $256$ |
| $p(r_k)$ | Histogram probability | $p(r_k) = n_k / (MN)$ | $0 \leq p \leq 1$ |
| $\mu_1, \mu_2$ | Class means (Iterative) | Mean of pixels in $G_1$ and $G_2$ | Intensity units |
| $T_{new}$ | Updated threshold | $T_{new} = (\mu_1 + \mu_2)/2$ | Intensity units |
| $\sigma_B^2$ | Between-class variance | $P_1 P_2 [\mu_1 - \mu_2]^2$ | Variance units |
| $T^*$ | Otsu optimum threshold | $\arg\max \sigma_B^2$ | Intensity units |
| $b(x, y)$ | Background illumination estimate | Low-pass / average of $f(x, y)$ | Intensity units |
| $\varepsilon$ | Convergence tolerance | Stop when $\Delta T < \varepsilon$ | $\approx 0.5$ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Use of Global Thresholding** |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Robotics** | Detect floor lines, obstacles, fiducial markers on shop floor |
| **Industrial Inspection** | Detect surface cracks, missing components, soldering defects |
| **Medical Imaging** | Segment tumours, blood cells, X-ray bone contours |
| **OCR / Document Processing** | Separate text from paper background |
| **Traffic & Surveillance** | License-plate extraction, motion blob detection |
| **Agriculture** | Distinguish ripe fruit from foliage in grading machines |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Iterative Global Threshold Convergence Equation

We now derive the closed-form update used by the Iterative Global Threshold algorithm.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Partitions

Given a threshold $T_k$ at iteration $k$, partition the image pixels:

$$
G_1^{(k)} = \{ (x, y) \mid f(x, y) > T_k \}
$$

$$
G_2^{(k)} = \{ (x, y) \mid f(x, y) \leq T_k \}
$$

Let $N_1^{(k)}$ and $N_2^{(k)}$ denote the number of pixels in $G_1$ and $G_2$ respectively.

### Step 2 — Compute Class Means

The mean intensity of pixels in $G_1$ is:

$$
\mu_1^{(k)} = \frac{1}{N_1^{(k)}} \sum_{(x,y) \in G_1^{(k)}} f(x, y)
$$

Similarly:

$$
\mu_2^{(k)} = \frac{1}{N_2^{(k)}} \sum_{(x,y) \in G_2^{(k)}} f(x, y)
$$

### Step 3 — Threshold Update Rule

The new threshold is set as the **midpoint of the two class means**:

$$
T_{k+1} = \frac{\mu_1^{(k)} + \mu_2^{(k)}}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition behind midpoint choice:** When the two classes are well separated, the midpoint of their means lies in the **valley between the two histogram peaks**, which is the most natural segmentation boundary.

### Step 4 — Convergence Condition

Stop when:

$$
\vert T_{k+1} - T_k \vert < \varepsilon
$$

Otherwise, set $T_k \leftarrow T_{k+1}$ and return to Step 1.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement

> An 8-bit image has the following histogram counts (out of $MN = 30$ pixels):
>
> $r_k$: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7
>
> $n_k$: 2, 4, 6, 3, 2, 1, 8, 4
>
> Apply the **Iterative Global Threshold Algorithm** to find the optimum threshold (use $\varepsilon = 0.5$).

### Solution — Iteration 0 (Initial Estimate)

Initial threshold = mean intensity:

$$
T_0 = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{7} k \cdot n_k}{MN} = \frac{0(2) + 1(4) + 2(6) + 3(3) + 4(2) + 5(1) + 6(8) + 7(4)}{30}
$$

$$
T_0 = \frac{0 + 4 + 12 + 9 + 8 + 5 + 48 + 28}{30} = \frac{114}{30} = 3.8
$$

### Solution — Iteration 1

**Partition using $T_0 = 3.8$:**

$$
G_1 = \{ r_k \mid k > 3.8 \} = \{ 4, 5, 6, 7 \} \quad \text{with counts } 2, 1, 8, 4 \Rightarrow N_1 = 15
$$

$$
G_2 = \{ r_k \mid k \leq 3.8 \} = \{ 0, 1, 2, 3 \} \quad \text{with counts } 2, 4, 6, 3 \Rightarrow N_2 = 15
$$

**Compute class means:**

$$
\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{4(2) + 5(1) + 6(8) + 7(4)}{15} = \frac{8 + 5 + 48 + 28}{15} = \frac{89}{15} \approx 5.93
$$

$$
\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{0(2) + 1(4) + 2(6) + 3(3)}{15} = \frac{0 + 4 + 12 + 9}{15} = \frac{25}{15} \approx 1.67
$$

**Update threshold:**

$$
T_1 = \frac{5.93 + 1.67}{2} = \frac{7.60}{2} = 3.80
$$

**Check convergence:**

$$
\Delta T = \vert 3.80 - 3.80 \vert = 0.0 < \varepsilon = 0.5
$$

### Solution — Final Answer

$$
\boxed{T^* = 3.80}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice how the algorithm **converged in a single iteration** because the initial mean already happened to lie in the natural valley between the two histogram peaks ($r_k = 3$ and $r_k = 4$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Otsu Between-Class Variance

### Step 1 — Class Probabilities

For a candidate threshold $k$, define the probability of class $C_1$ (pixels $\leq k$):

$$
P_1(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k} p_i
$$

And class $C_2$ (pixels $> k$):

$$
P_2(k) = \sum_{i=k+1}^{L-1} p_i = 1 - P_1(k)
$$

### Step 2 — Class Means

$$
\mu_1(k) = \frac{1}{P_1(k)} \sum_{i=0}^{k} i \, p_i
$$

$$
\mu_2(k) = \frac{1}{P_2(k)} \sum_{i=k+1}^{L-1} i \, p_i
$$

### Step 3 — Total Mean

$$
\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \, p_i = P_1 \mu_1 + P_2 \mu_2
$$

### Step 4 — Between-Class Variance

$$
\sigma_B^2(k) = P_1(k) \, P_2(k) \, [\mu_1(k) - \mu_2(k)]^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** When the two classes are well separated, $\vert \mu_1 - \mu_2 \vert$ is **large**, making $\sigma_B^2$ **maximum**. The threshold that maximizes $\sigma_B^2$ is automatically optimal.

### Step 5 — Optimum Threshold

$$
T^* = \arg\max_{k} \sigma_B^2(k)
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Otsu Example (Compact)

Using the same histogram above ($MN = 30$):

$$
p_k = n_k / 30: \quad 0.067, 0.133, 0.200, 0.100, 0.067, 0.033, 0.267, 0.133
$$

**Test $k = 3$:**

$$
P_1 = 0.067 + 0.133 + 0.200 + 0.100 = 0.500
$$

$$
P_2 = 1 - 0.500 = 0.500
$$

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{0(0.067) + 1(0.133) + 2(0.200) + 3(0.100)}{0.500} = \frac{0.933}{0.500} \approx 1.867
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \frac{4(0.067) + 5(0.033) + 6(0.267) + 7(0.133)}{0.500} = \frac{3.633}{0.500} \approx 7.267
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(3) = (0.5)(0.5)(1.867 - 7.267)^2 = 0.25 \times 29.16 \approx 7.29
$$

Repeating the test for $k = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$ and selecting the maximum yields:

$$
\boxed{T^* = 3 \text{ (verified by Otsu's method)}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of Global Thresholding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> inputA[Input Grayscale Image fx,y]
    inputA --> histA[Compute Histogram pk of fx,y]
    histA --> bimodA{Bimodal?}
    bimodA -- No --> failA[Use Local or Adaptive Threshold]
    bimodA -- Yes --> initA[Choose Initial Threshold T0]
    initA --> segA[Partition Pixels into G1 and G2 using T]
    segA --> meanA[Compute Class Means mu1 and mu2]
    meanA --> updA[Update Tnew equals mu1 plus mu2 divided by 2]
    updA --> convA{Delta T less than epsilon?}
    convA -- No --> segA
    convA -- Yes --> binA[Apply Final Threshold T to fx,y]
    binA --> outA[Output Binary Image gx,y]
    outA --> stopA([End])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:**
> - The outer loop (Steps 4 → 7) executes the **iterative refinement**.
> - The convergence check (`Delta T less than epsilon`) prevents infinite loops.
> - If the histogram is **not bimodal**, the algorithm fails — and the system falls back to local/adaptive thresholding.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Otsu's Global Thresholding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> inputB[Input Image fx,y]
    inputB --> histB[Compute Normalized Histogram pk]
    histB --> initB[Initialize Best Variance sigBest = 0 and Tk = 0]
    initB --> loopB[For each candidate threshold k from 0 to L minus 1]
    loopB --> probsB[Compute P1k and P2k]
    probsB --> meansB[Compute mu1k and mu2k]
    meansB --> varB[Compute Between Class Variance sigmaB squared k]
    varB --> compB{sigmaB squared k greater than sigBest?}
    compB -- Yes --> updB[Set sigBest = sigmaB squared k and Tk = k]
    compB -- No --> loopB
    updB --> loopB
    loopB -- Done --> outB[Output Optimum Threshold Tk]
    outB --> stopB([End])
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Map — Global vs Local Thresholding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    imgA[Input Image fx,y] --> q1{Single Threshold T?}
    q1 -- Yes --> gth[Global Thresholding]
    q1 -- No --> lth[Local Adaptive Thresholding]
    gth --> outA[Fast, Simple, Fails on uneven lighting]
    lth --> outB[Robust, Slower, Needs window size]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define global thresholding. Under what conditions does it produce satisfactory segmentation?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Global thresholding is a pixel-classification technique in which a **single intensity value $T$** is applied to **all pixels** of an image to produce a binary output:
>
> $$
> g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq T \\ 0 & \text{if } f(x, y) < T \end{cases}
> $$
>
> **Conditions for satisfactory performance:**
> 1. The histogram of $f(x, y)$ must be **bimodal** — two well-separated peaks.
> 2. The **illumination** across the scene must be **uniform**.
> 3. The object-background **contrast** must be high.
> 4. There should be a **deep valley** between the two peaks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List any three limitations of global thresholding.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Three limitations:**
> 1. **Fails on non-uniform illumination** — a single $T$ cannot handle brightness gradients across the image.
> 2. **Requires bimodal histograms** — multimodal or unimodal histograms produce poor segmentation.
> 3. **Sensitive to noise** — noise spikes near $T$ get misclassified, breaking object contours.
> 4. **Cannot handle overlapping intensities** — when object and background intensities overlap, a single cut-off inevitably misclassifies boundary pixels.
> 5. **Manual $T$ selection** is subjective unless automated methods (Otsu, iterative) are used.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Format (KTU ESE Module 3):** Answer **any ONE full question** from the pair.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Iterative Global Threshold Selection Algorithm**. Explain how the threshold is initialized, updated, and how convergence is determined. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A $5 \times 5$ image has the following intensity matrix. Apply the Iterative Global Threshold Algorithm (assume $T_0$ = global mean) and determine the final threshold.

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
10 & 12 & 11 & 200 & 205 \\
9 & 13 & 10 & 198 & 202 \\
11 & 14 & 12 & 199 & 204 \\
9 & 10 & 13 & 201 & 203 \\
12 & 11 & 10 & 200 & 206
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Use $\varepsilon = 0.5$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Iterative Global Threshold Algorithm (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Initialization:**
> Set initial threshold $T_0$ to the **global mean intensity** of the image:
>
> $$
> T_0 = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x, y} f(x, y)
> $$
>
> Alternatively, $T_0$ may be chosen as the **midpoint of the gray-level range**, i.e., $T_0 = (L-1)/2$.
>
> **Step 2 — Partitioning:**
> Use $T_k$ to divide pixels into two groups:
>
> $$
> G_1 = \{ (x, y) \mid f(x, y) > T_k \}, \quad G_2 = \{ (x, y) \mid f(x, y) \leq T_k \}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Mean Computation:**
> Calculate the mean of each group:
>
> $$
> \mu_1^{(k)} = \frac{1}{\vert G_1 \vert} \sum_{(x,y) \in G_1} f(x, y), \quad \mu_2^{(k)} = \frac{1}{\vert G_2 \vert} \sum_{(x,y) \in G_2} f(x, y)
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Threshold Update:**
>
> $$
> T_{k+1} = \frac{\mu_1^{(k)} + \mu_2^{(k)}}{2}
> $$
>
> **Step 5 — Convergence Test:**
> Stop when $\vert T_{k+1} - T_k \vert < \varepsilon$, else loop to Step 2.
>
> **Why it converges:** Each iteration pushes $T$ closer to the **valley between histogram peaks**. Within 3–6 iterations, the threshold stabilizes.

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sum of all intensities:**

$$
S = (10+12+11+200+205) + (9+13+10+198+202) + (11+14+12+199+204) + (9+10+13+201+203) + (12+11+10+200+206)
$$

$$
S_{\text{row1}} = 10+12+11+200+205 = 438
$$

$$
S_{\text{row2}} = 9+13+10+198+202 = 432
$$

$$
S_{\text{row3}} = 11+14+12+199+204 = 440
$$

$$
S_{\text{row4}} = 9+10+13+201+203 = 436
$$

$$
S_{\text{row5}} = 12+11+10+200+206 = 439
$$

$$
S_{total} = 438 + 432 + 440 + 436 + 439 = 2185
$$

**Step 2 — Initial threshold:**

$$
T_0 = \frac{2185}{25} = 87.4
$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 1: Partition at $T_0 = 87.4$**

$$
G_1 = \{ \text{pixels} > 87.4 \} = \{ 200, 205, 198, 202, 199, 204, 201, 203, 200, 206 \} \Rightarrow N_1 = 10
$$

$$
G_2 = \{ \text{pixels} \leq 87.4 \} = \{ 10, 12, 11, 9, 13, 10, 11, 14, 12, 9, 10, 13, 12, 11, 10 \} \Rightarrow N_2 = 15
$$

**Step 4 — Class means:**

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{200+205+198+202+199+204+201+203+200+206}{10} = \frac{2018}{10} = 201.8
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \frac{10+12+11+9+13+10+11+14+12+9+10+13+12+11+10}{15} = \frac{167}{15} \approx 11.13
$$

**Step 5 — Update:**

$$
T_1 = \frac{201.8 + 11.13}{2} = \frac{212.93}{2} = 106.47
$$

**Step 6 — Convergence check:**

$$
\Delta T = \vert 106.47 - 87.4 \vert = 19.07 > 0.5 \Rightarrow \text{Not converged}
$$

**Step 7 — Iteration 2: Partition at $T_1 = 106.47$**

- $G_1$ unchanged (object pixels still $> 106.47$) → $\mu_1 = 201.8$
- $G_2$ unchanged (background pixels still $\leq 106.47$) → $\mu_2 = 11.13$

$$
T_2 = \frac{201.8 + 11.13}{2} = 106.47
$$

$$
\Delta T = \vert 106.47 - 106.47 \vert = 0 < \varepsilon \Rightarrow \textbf{Converged}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{T^* = 106.47}
$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain **Otsu's method** of automatic global thresholding. Derive the expression for **between-class variance** $\sigma_B^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the histogram below (image size $MN = 50$), determine the optimum threshold using Otsu's method. **(7 Marks)**

| $r_k$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $n_k$ | 5 | 7 | 8 | 2 | 1 | 4 | 10 | 13 |

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Otsu's Method Theory (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** Otsu's method finds the threshold $k^*$ that **maximizes the between-class variance** $\sigma_B^2(k)$ across all candidate thresholds $k = 0, 1, \dots, L-1$. The optimum threshold separates the histogram into two classes such that they are maximally distinct.
>
> **Derivation:**
>
> Class probabilities:
>
> $$
> P_1(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k} p_i, \quad P_2(k) = \sum_{i=k+1}^{L-1} p_i = 1 - P_1(k)
> $$
>
> Class means:
>
> $$
> \mu_1(k) = \frac{1}{P_1(k)} \sum_{i=0}^{k} i \, p_i, \quad \mu_2(k) = \frac{1}{P_2(k)} \sum_{i=k+1}^{L-1} i \, p_i
> $$
>
> **Total image mean:**
>
> $$
> \mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \, p_i = P_1 \mu_1 + P_2 \mu_2
> $$
>
> **Between-class variance:**
>
> $$
> \sigma_B^2(k) = P_1(k) \, P_2(k) \, [\mu_1(k) - \mu_2(k)]^2
> $$
>
> **Optimum threshold:**
>
> $$
> T^* = \arg\max_{k \in [0, L-1]} \sigma_B^2(k)
> $$
>
> **Justification:** Maximizing $\sigma_B^2$ is equivalent to minimizing within-class variance $\sigma_W^2$, since total variance $\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_W^2 + \sigma_B^2$ is constant.

### Part (b) — Otsu Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Normalized histogram $p_k = n_k / 50$:**

$$
p: \; 0.10, \; 0.14, \; 0.16, \; 0.04, \; 0.02, \; 0.08, \; 0.20, \; 0.26
$$

**Step 2 — Test each $k$ from 0 to 6:**

**Test $k = 2$:**

$$
P_1 = 0.10 + 0.14 + 0.16 = 0.40
$$

$$
P_2 = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60
$$

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{0(0.10) + 1(0.14) + 2(0.16)}{0.40} = \frac{0.46}{0.40} = 1.15
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \frac{3(0.04) + 4(0.02) + 5(0.08) + 6(0.20) + 7(0.26)}{0.60} = \frac{0.12 + 0.08 + 0.40 + 1.20 + 1.82}{0.60} = \frac{3.62}{0.60} \approx 6.03
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(2) = (0.40)(0.60)(1.15 - 6.03)^2 = 0.24 \times 23.81 \approx 5.71
$$

**Test $k = 3$:**

$$
P_1 = 0.44, \quad P_2 = 0.56
$$

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{0.46 + 3(0.04)}{0.44} = \frac{0.58}{0.44} \approx 1.32
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \frac{3.62 - 3(0.04)}{0.56} = \frac{3.50}{0.56} \approx 6.25
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(3) = (0.44)(0.56)(1.32 - 6.25)^2 \approx 0.2464 \times 24.31 \approx 5.99
$$

**Test $k = 4$:**

$$
P_1 = 0.46, \quad P_2 = 0.54
$$

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{0.58 + 4(0.02)}{0.46} = \frac{0.66}{0.46} \approx 1.43
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \frac{3.50 - 4(0.02)}{0.54} = \frac{3.42}{0.54} \approx 6.33
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(4) = (0.46)(0.54)(1.43 - 6.33)^2 \approx 0.2484 \times 24.01 \approx 5.96
$$

**Step 3 — Compile results:**

| $k$ | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $\mu_1$ | $\mu_2$ | $\sigma_B^2(k)$ |
|-----|-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 2   | 0.40  | 0.60  | 1.15    | 6.03    | 5.71            |
| 3   | 0.44  | 0.56  | 1.32    | 6.25    | **5.99 (max)**  |
| 4   | 0.46  | 0.54  | 1.43    | 6.33    | 5.96            |

**Step 4 — Optimum:**

$$
\boxed{T^* = 3}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify $T_0$** — Always state the initialization rule (mean intensity is the safest default).
> 2. **Sign error in class means** — Dividing by **wrong $N$**: use $N_1$ for $\mu_1$ and $N_2$ for $\mu_2$. Never use $MN$ for both.
> 3. **Misnaming $\varepsilon$** — KTU specifically checks **convergence criterion** wording. Say "*change in threshold is less than $\varepsilon$*" — not "*error less than $\varepsilon$*".
> 4. **Skipping the stopping condition** — In iterative algorithms, marks are reserved for the **convergence test** ($|T_{k+1} - T_k| < \varepsilon$). Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Otsu: forgetting to test ALL $k$** — You must show the computation (or at least justify) that $T^*$ is the **maximum** of $\sigma_B^2$, not just the first one you tested.
> 6. **Confusing $p_k$ with $n_k$** — Otsu's formula uses **probabilities $p_k$**, not raw counts $n_k$. The denominator is $MN$.
> 7. **Mentioning "Otsu is unsupervised" but not saying why** — Justify: it requires **no training data** and is derived purely from the image's histogram.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Yield Recap — Global Thresholding**
>
> - **Definition:** A **single** threshold $T$ applied to **all pixels** of an image to produce a binary output $g(x, y) \in \{0, 1\}$.
> - **Core formula:**
>
> $$
> g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq T \\ 0 & \text{if } f(x, y) < T \end{cases}
> $$
>
> - **Pre-requisites for success:** **Bimodal histogram** + **uniform illumination** + **high object-background contrast**.
> - **Iterative threshold update rule:**
>
> $$
> T_{k+1} = \frac{\mu_1^{(k)} + \mu_2^{(k)}}{2}
> $$
>
> - **Convergence criterion:** $\vert T_{k+1} - T_k \vert < \varepsilon$, with $\varepsilon \approx 0.5$.
> - **Otsu's between-class variance:**
>
> $$
> \sigma_B^2(k) = P_1 P_2 [\mu_1 - \mu_2]^2, \quad T^* = \arg\max \sigma_B^2(k)
> $$
>
> - **Illumination compensation:** Use $\dfrac{f(x,y)}{b(x,y)} \geq T$ where $b$ is the background estimate.
> - **Failure mode:** Non-uniform lighting → switch to **local/adaptive thresholding**.
> - **Output:** Binary image (1 = object, 0 = background).
> - **KTU favourite algorithms:** (1) Iterative selection, (2) Otsu's method, (3) Manual valley pick.
> - **Advantage:** Simple, fast, $O(MN)$ time.
> - **Disadvantage:** Single $T$ cannot handle complex or uneven scenes.
> - **Edge case:** If histogram is unimodal → global thresholding **fails** — answer this when asked about "limitations".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Thresholding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adaptive Thresholding** (also known as *local* or *dynamic thresholding*) is an image segmentation technique in which the threshold value is **not constant across the entire image**, but is computed locally for each pixel based on the statistical properties of its surrounding neighborhood. Formally, for an input grayscale image $I(x,y)$, the binarized output $B(x,y)$ is computed as:

$$B(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } I(x,y) > T(x,y) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $T(x,y)$ is a **spatially varying threshold function** derived from a local window $W$ of size $w \times w$ centered at $(x,y)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Adaptive thresholding is critical in real-world robot vision applications where lighting conditions are **non-uniform**, shadows exist, and a single global threshold fails to segment foreground objects reliably.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to read a page of handwritten notes where the **left side is brightly lit by a window** and the **right side is in deep shadow**.

- A **Global Threshold** would act like a single pair of sunglasses — it works perfectly on one side, but either blinds you on the bright side or makes the shadowed text completely invisible.
- An **Adaptive Threshold** acts like a **smart, auto-adjusting eyepiece** — it brightens the dark regions and dims the bright regions so that you can read the text **uniformly across the whole page**.

In robotics, this is exactly what happens when a camera moves across a factory floor: a metallic part under a spotlight appears pure white, while the same part under a dim corner appears nearly black. Adaptive thresholding ensures the robot "sees" both versions as the same object.

## 1.3 Why Adaptive Thresholding? — Motivation

Global thresholding (e.g., Otsu's method) assumes a **bimodal histogram** with consistent foreground/background illumination. This assumption **breaks down** in the following real-world robot vision scenarios:

- **Non-uniform illumination** (shadows, glare, vignetting from camera lens)
- **Variable reflectance** (curved metallic surfaces, textured objects)
- **Low-contrast regions** mixed with high-contrast regions in the same frame
- **Medical imaging** (X-rays, MRI slices with intensity gradients)
- **Document scanning** under uneven ambient light

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Window Size ($w$):** The local neighborhood size $w \times w$ is the most critical hyperparameter. A **larger window** is robust to noise but may blur fine details. A **smaller window** preserves detail but is highly sensitive to noise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Four Step Logic

The adaptive thresholding algorithm executes the following logical sequence for every pixel $(x,y)$ in the image:

1. **Window Extraction:** Define a local neighborhood window $W_{x,y}$ of size $w \times w$ centered around pixel $(x,y)$. Typical values of $w$ in OpenCV are odd numbers (e.g., 3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 21).
2. **Local Statistic Computation:** Compute a statistical function $\mathcal{F}$ over the intensities within the window. Common choices for $\mathcal{F}$ are:
   - **Arithmetic Mean** $\mu_{x,y}$
   - **Gaussian Weighted Mean** $\mu_{G(x,y)}$
   - **Median** $M_{x,y}$
3. **Threshold Offsetting:** Subtract a constant tuning parameter $C$ from the local statistic to form the local threshold $T(x,y)$.
4. **Binarization:** Compare the center pixel intensity $I(x,y)$ against $T(x,y)$ and assign a binary label (0 or 255).

## 2.2 The Core Adaptive Thresholding Equation

The general form for the local threshold is:

$$T(x,y) = \mathcal{F}\bigl(I(u,v) : (u,v) \in W_{x,y}\bigr) - C$$

where $\mathcal{F}$ is the chosen local statistic operator and $C$ is a non-negative integer constant that compensates for variations in surface reflectance.

## 2.3 Method 1 — Adaptive Mean Thresholding

In this method, $\mathcal{F}$ is the **arithmetic mean** of pixel intensities within the local window. The local mean $\mu_{x,y}$ is defined as:

$$\mu_{x,y} = \frac{1}{w^2} \sum_{i=-r}^{r} \sum_{j=-r}^{r} I(x+i, y+j)$$

where $r = \frac{w-1}{2}$ is the window radius. The final decision rule is:

$$B(x,y) = \begin{cases} 255, & \text{if } I(x,y) > \mu_{x,y} - C \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why subtract C?** Subtracting a small constant $C$ (e.g., 2–10) compensates for the fact that the mean tends to overestimate the true background intensity in regions where foreground occupies less than half the window. $C$ acts as a **safety margin**.

## 2.4 Method 2 — Adaptive Gaussian Thresholding

Here, $\mathcal{F}$ is a **Gaussian-weighted mean** that gives more importance to pixels near the center of the window and exponentially less weight to pixels far from the center. A 2D Gaussian kernel $G_{\sigma}$ is convolved with the image patch:

$$\mu_{G(x,y)} = \frac{\sum_{i=-r}^{r}\sum_{j=-r}^{r} w(i,j) \cdot I(x+i, y+j)}{\sum_{i=-r}^{r}\sum_{j=-r}^{r} w(i,j)}$$

where the Gaussian weight is:

$$w(i,j) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{i^2 + j^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

and the standard deviation is typically set as $\sigma = 0.3 \cdot \bigl((w-1) \cdot 0.5 - 1\bigr) + 0.8$ (a heuristic from OpenCV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gaussian adaptive thresholding is superior to mean adaptive thresholding** when the foreground objects are *compact clusters* surrounded by extensive background. The Gaussian falloff ensures peripheral noisy pixels do not contaminate the local estimate.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Typical Value (KTU Context)** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Window Size | $w$ | 3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 21, 31 | Pixels (odd integer only) |
| Window Radius | $r$ | $\frac{w-1}{2}$ | Pixels |
| Constant Offset | $C$ | 2, 3, 5, 8, 10 | Grayscale Intensity Units (0–255) |
| Local Mean | $\mu_{x,y}$ | Computed dynamically | Intensity units |
| Gaussian Std. Dev. | $\sigma$ | $0.3 \cdot \bigl((w-1)\cdot 0.5 - 1\bigr) + 0.8$ | Pixels (empirical) |
| Output Levels | $B(x,y)$ | 0 and 255 | 8-bit binary |
| Complexity | $O(N \cdot w^2)$ | Per pixel | N = total pixels |

> **Critical Note on Punctuation:** In LaTeX, never use the pipe symbol $\vert$ inside markdown table cells to denote absolute value or cardinality, as it breaks the table parser. Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or the `\mid` command instead.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Robot Vision

- **Autonomous Navigation:** Real-time lane detection under dappled sunlight or tunnel shadows.
- **Pick-and-Place Robots:** Segregating metallic parts of varying reflectance on a conveyor belt under uneven LED lighting.
- **Agricultural Robotics:** Detecting ripe fruits on trees where the canopy casts irregular shadows.
- **Biomedical Robotics:** Segmenting cells in microscopy images with vignetting artifacts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Example — Adaptive Mean Thresholding

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for KTU Board Exam:** Consider a $3 \times 3$ sub-image patch centered at pixel $(2,2)$. Compute the adaptive threshold and the binarized output.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

The local $3 \times 3$ grayscale neighborhood is:

$$\text{Patch} = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 60 & 55 \\ 65 & 200 & 70 \\ 58 & 62 & 54 \end{bmatrix}$$

The center pixel intensity is $I(2,2) = 200$, window size is $w = 3$, and the constant $C = 5$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify all 9 pixels in the $3 \times 3$ window.**

The pixel values within the window $W_{2,2}$ are: $\{50, 60, 55, 65, 200, 70, 58, 62, 54\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of all pixel intensities.**

$$\sum I = 50 + 60 + 55 + 65 + 200 + 70 + 58 + 62 + 54$$

Breaking this down:

$$50 + 60 = 110$$
$$110 + 55 = 165$$
$$165 + 65 = 230$$
$$230 + 200 = 430$$
$$430 + 70 = 500$$
$$500 + 58 = 558$$
$$558 + 62 = 620$$
$$620 + 54 = 674$$

**Step 3 — Compute the arithmetic mean $\mu_{2,2}$.**

The window has $w^2 = 9$ pixels. Therefore:

$$\mu_{2,2} = \frac{674}{9} = 74.888...$$

Rounding to two decimal places: $\mu_{2,2} \approx 74.89$.

**Step 4 — Compute the local threshold $T(2,2)$.**

$$T(2,2) = \mu_{2,2} - C = 74.89 - 5 = 69.89$$

**Step 5 — Apply the binarization decision rule.**

Compare the center pixel $I(2,2) = 200$ against $T(2,2) = 69.89$:

$$200 > 69.89 \implies B(2,2) = 255$$

The center pixel is classified as **foreground** (white).

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Example — Adaptive Gaussian Thresholding

### 3.2.1 Given Data

Using the **same** $3 \times 3$ patch with $w = 3$, $r = 1$, and a specified standard deviation $\sigma = 1.0$. The constant $C = 5$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Construct the Gaussian weight matrix $w(i,j)$.**

For a $3 \times 3$ window, the coordinate offsets $(i,j)$ range from $(-1,-1)$ to $(1,1)$:

$$w(i,j) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{i^2 + j^2}{2 \cdot 1.0^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{i^2 + j^2}{2}\right)$$

Computing each weight explicitly:

- $w(-1,-1) = e^{-(1+1)/2} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$
- $w(-1, 0) = e^{-(1+0)/2} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$
- $w(-1, 1) = e^{-(1+1)/2} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$
- $w(0, -1) = e^{-(0+1)/2} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$
- $w(0, 0) = e^{0} = 1.0000$
- $w(0, 1) = e^{-(0+1)/2} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$
- $w(1, -1) = e^{-(1+1)/2} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$
- $w(1, 0) = e^{-(1+0)/2} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$
- $w(1, 1) = e^{-(1+1)/2} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$

**Step 2 — Construct the Gaussian kernel matrix aligned with the patch.**

$$\text{Kernel} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3679 & 0.6065 & 0.3679 \\ 0.6065 & 1.0000 & 0.6065 \\ 0.3679 & 0.6065 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sum of all weights (denominator).**

$$\sum w = 0.3679 + 0.6065 + 0.3679 + 0.6065 + 1.0000 + 0.6065 + 0.3679 + 0.6065 + 0.3679$$

Grouping for clarity:

$$0.3679 \times 4 = 1.4716$$
$$0.6065 \times 4 = 2.4260$$
$$1.0000 \times 1 = 1.0000$$
$$\sum w = 1.4716 + 2.4260 + 1.0000 = 4.8976$$

**Step 4 — Compute the weighted pixel sum (numerator).**

Multiply each pixel value by its corresponding weight:

$$0.3679 \times 50 = 18.395$$
$$0.6065 \times 60 = 36.390$$
$$0.3679 \times 55 = 20.2345$$
$$0.6065 \times 65 = 39.4225$$
$$1.0000 \times 200 = 200.000$$
$$0.6065 \times 70 = 42.455$$
$$0.3679 \times 58 = 21.3382$$
$$0.6065 \times 62 = 37.603$$
$$0.3679 \times 54 = 19.8666$$

Summing all weighted products:

$$18.395 + 36.390 = 54.785$$
$$54.785 + 20.2345 = 75.0195$$
$$75.0195 + 39.4225 = 114.442$$
$$114.442 + 200.000 = 314.442$$
$$314.442 + 42.455 = 356.897$$
$$356.897 + 21.3382 = 378.2352$$
$$378.2352 + 37.603 = 415.8382$$
$$415.8382 + 19.8666 = 435.7048$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Gaussian-weighted local mean $\mu_{G(2,2)}$.**

$$\mu_{G(2,2)} = \frac{435.7048}{4.8976} = 88.962...$$

Rounded: $\mu_{G(2,2)} \approx 88.96$.

**Step 6 — Compute the local threshold and binarize.**

$$T(2,2) = 88.96 - 5 = 83.96$$

Since $I(2,2) = 200 > 83.96$, we have $B(2,2) = 255$ (foreground).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Global Thresholding** | **Adaptive Mean** | **Adaptive Gaussian** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold type | Single value for whole image | Local mean per pixel | Local Gaussian-weighted mean |
| Computational Cost | $O(N)$ | $O(N \cdot w^2)$ | $O(N \cdot w^2)$ (with exp ops) |
| Robust to Illumination | **Poor** | **Good** | **Very Good** |
| Noise Sensitivity | Low | Moderate | Low (weighted averaging) |
| Edge Preservation | Good | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Best Use Case | Uniform lighting | Gradual illumination changes | Sharp illumination gradients |
| KTU Mark Weightage | 2 marks (definition) | 7 marks (full derivation) | 7 marks (full derivation) |

## 3.4 Computational Complexity Derivation

For an image of size $M \times N$ pixels and a local window of size $w \times w$:

- **Per-pixel operations:** $w^2$ multiplications, $w^2 - 1$ additions, and 1 comparison.
- **Total operations:** $M \cdot N \cdot w^2$ multiplications and similar additions.
- **Asymptotic complexity:** $O(M \cdot N \cdot w^2)$.

When $w$ is small (e.g., $w = 3$ or $w = 5$), the practical runtime is close to $O(M \cdot N)$, making it suitable for real-time robot vision applications.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Adaptive Thresholding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Grayscale Image I of size M by N] --> B[Initialize window size w and constant C]
    B --> C{For each pixel x,y in image}
    C --> D[Extract w by w local neighborhood Wx,y]
    D --> E{Choose local statistic method}
    E --> F[Method 1: Compute arithmetic mean mu_xy]
    E --> G[Method 2: Compute Gaussian weighted mean muG_xy]
    F --> H[Subtract C to obtain local threshold Txy]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Compare I x,y with Txy}
    I --> J[I x,y greater than Txy]
    I --> K[I x,y less than or equal to Txy]
    J --> L[Set output pixel to 255 foreground]
    K --> M[Set output pixel to 0 background]
    L --> N[Store Bx,y in output image]
    M --> N
    N --> O{More pixels to process}
    O --> C
    O --> P[Return final binary image B]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Choosing Thresholding Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Robot Vision Input Image]) --> Q1{Is illumination uniform across scene?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Histogram clearly bimodal?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Are foreground objects compact clusters?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use Global Otsu Thresholding]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Use Global Manual Threshold]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Use Adaptive Gaussian Thresholding]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Use Adaptive Mean Thresholding]
    R1 --> Out([Segmented Binary Output])
    R2 --> Out
    R3 --> Out
    R4 --> Out
```

## 4.3 Pixel Neighborhood Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CenterPixel[Processing Center Pixel I x,y]
        CP[200]
    end
    subgraph NWCorner[Top Left Region]
        P1[50]
        P2[60]
        P3[55]
    end
    subgraph NEMid[Top Right Region]
        P4[65]
        P5[70]
    end
    subgraph SWMid[Bottom Left Region]
        P6[58]
        P7[62]
        P8[54]
    end
    NWCorner --> CenterPixel
    NEMid --> CenterPixel
    SWMid --> CenterPixel
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Raw Image Acquisition from Camera Sensor] --> L2[Layer 2: Grayscale Conversion using luminance formula]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Noise Smoothing using Gaussian Blur optional]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Sliding Window Scan over Image]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Local Statistic Computation per window]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Threshold Comparison and Binarization]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Morphological Post Processing to clean noise]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8: Connected Component Analysis for object detection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** Define adaptive thresholding. Why is it preferred over global thresholding in real-world robot vision applications?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Adaptive thresholding is an image segmentation technique in which the threshold value used to convert a grayscale image into a binary image **varies spatially** across the image. For each pixel $(x,y)$, the threshold $T(x,y)$ is computed from the statistical properties (mean, Gaussian mean, or median) of a local $w \times w$ neighborhood centered at that pixel.

It is preferred over global thresholding because **(1 mark)** it handles **non-uniform illumination** which is common in real-world environments (shadows, glare, and uneven lighting), **(1 mark)** it adapts to local image contrast, and **(1 mark)** it preserves object boundaries even when background intensity varies across the scene — making it ideal for robot vision under dynamic lighting.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q:** Differentiate between adaptive mean thresholding and adaptive Gaussian thresholding.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Adaptive Mean** | **Adaptive Gaussian** |
|---|---|---|
| Local Statistic | Arithmetic mean | Gaussian-weighted mean |
| Pixel Weighting | Equal weight to all neighbors | Higher weight to central pixels |
| Noise Robustness | Moderate | **Higher** |
| Computational Cost | Lower | Higher (exponential operations) |
| Best For | Gradual illumination changes | Sharp illumination gradients, compact objects |

**(1 mark)** for each correct distinguishing point.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** *(a)* Explain the concept of adaptive thresholding with a suitable mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of window size $w$ and constant $C$ in the algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* For a $3 \times 3$ local neighborhood with pixel values $\begin{bmatrix} 30 & 40 & 35 \\ 45 & 180 & 50 \\ 38 & 42 & 32 \end{bmatrix}$ and $C = 4$, compute the adaptive mean threshold and the binarized output for the center pixel. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Concept and Formulation (7 Marks):**

Adaptive thresholding is a **local binarization technique** that computes a spatially varying threshold $T(x,y)$ for each pixel based on the statistical properties of its surrounding neighborhood (typically a square window of size $w \times w$). **(1 mark)**

The mathematical formulation is:

$$T(x,y) = \mathcal{F}\bigl(I(u,v) : (u,v) \in W_{x,y}\bigr) - C$$

$$\text{And } B(x,y) = \begin{cases} 255, & \text{if } I(x,y) > T(x,y) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(1 mark)** for the formula. For the arithmetic mean version:

$$T(x,y) = \frac{1}{w^2}\sum_{i=-r}^{r}\sum_{j=-r}^{r} I(x+i, y+j) - C, \quad \text{where } r = \frac{w-1}{2}$$

**(1 mark)** for the expanded form. For the Gaussian version:

$$T(x,y) = \frac{\sum w(i,j) \cdot I(x+i,y+j)}{\sum w(i,j)} - C, \quad w(i,j) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{i^2+j^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**(1 mark)** for the Gaussian form. **Role of window size $w$:** A larger $w$ produces smoother thresholds and better noise suppression, but loses fine detail. A smaller $w$ preserves edges and detail but is more sensitive to noise. **(1 mark)**. **Role of constant $C$:** The constant $C$ is a tunable offset subtracted from the local statistic to prevent foreground pixels from being misclassified as background when the local mean is dominated by bright background pixels. Higher $C$ makes the thresholding more aggressive (more pixels become background). **(1 mark)**. The remaining **1 mark** is for the binary decision rule and a concluding remark on its application to non-uniformly lit robot vision scenes.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Sum all 9 pixel values in the $3 \times 3$ window:** **(1 mark)**

$$\sum I = 30 + 40 + 35 + 45 + 180 + 50 + 38 + 42 + 32$$

Computing step by step:
- $30 + 40 = 70$
- $70 + 35 = 105$
- $105 + 45 = 150$
- $150 + 180 = 330$
- $330 + 50 = 380$
- $380 + 38 = 418$
- $418 + 42 = 460$
- $460 + 32 = 492$

**Step 2 — Compute the arithmetic mean:** **(2 marks)**

$$\mu_{2,2} = \frac{492}{9} = 54.667$$

**Step 3 — Compute the local threshold:** **(1 mark)**

$$T(2,2) = \mu_{2,2} - C = 54.667 - 4 = 50.667$$

**Step 4 — Apply the binarization rule:** **(1 mark)**

Since $I(2,2) = 180 > 50.667$, we have $B(2,2) = 255$.

**Step 5 — State the conclusion clearly:** **(1 mark)**

The center pixel is classified as **foreground (white, intensity 255)** because its intensity 180 is significantly higher than the local adaptive threshold 50.667.

**Step 6 — Discuss the result (bonus insight):** **(1 mark)**

The high-intensity center pixel (180) stands out from its neighbors (which range from 30 to 50), so the algorithm correctly identifies it as a bright object (e.g., a metallic screw head under robot vision) against a darker background.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q:** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an adaptive thresholding system. Compare it with global Otsu thresholding. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A $5 \times 5$ image patch has an average intensity of $\mu = 120$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 15$. The Gaussian adaptive threshold with $\sigma_{G} = 0.8$ and $C = 3$ is applied. Compute the Gaussian-weighted local threshold and comment on the binarization outcome for a center pixel of intensity 130. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Working and Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Working (Block Diagram explanation — 4 marks):**

The adaptive thresholding system operates in the following sequence:
1. **Image Input:** A grayscale image $I(x,y)$ of size $M \times N$ is received from the camera. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Window Initialization:** A window size $w$ (odd integer) and constant $C$ are set as hyperparameters. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Sliding Window:** A $w \times w$ window slides across every pixel position. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Local Statistic:** Inside each window, either the arithmetic mean or Gaussian-weighted mean is computed. **(1 mark)**
5. **Threshold Computation:** The local threshold $T(x,y) = \mathcal{F} - C$ is calculated. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Binarization:** $I(x,y)$ is compared to $T(x,y)$ and assigned 0 or 255. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Output:** A binary image $B$ is returned. **(0.5 mark)**

**Comparison with Otsu (3 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Otsu (Global)** | **Adaptive (Local)** |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold | One value for whole image | Varies per pixel |
| Assumption | Bimodal histogram | No global assumption |
| Illumination | Requires uniform lighting | Handles non-uniform lighting |
| Speed | Faster ($O(N)$) | Slower ($O(N \cdot w^2)$) |
| Use case | Document scanning, simple scenes | Real-world robot vision |

**(1 mark)** per valid row of comparison.

**Part (b) — Gaussian Threshold Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Gaussian-weighted local mean formula:** **(1 mark)**

For a flat local image patch where the center pixel represents the typical intensity, the Gaussian-weighted mean $\mu_{G}$ is approximated using the relationship:

$$\mu_{G} \approx \mu + \sigma_{G} \cdot \sigma_{local}$$

where $\sigma_{local}$ captures the local contrast variation.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:** **(2 marks)**

Given $\mu = 120$, $\sigma = 15$, $\sigma_{G} = 0.8$, and $C = 3$:

$$\mu_{G} = 120 + 0.8 \times 15 = 120 + 12 = 132$$

**Step 3 — Compute the local threshold:** **(1 mark)**

$$T = \mu_{G} - C = 132 - 3 = 129$$

**Step 4 — Compare the center pixel intensity:** **(1 mark)**

Center pixel intensity $= 130$ and $T = 129$. Since $130 > 129$, the pixel is **just above** the threshold.

**Step 5 — Binarization decision:** **(1 mark)**

$B = 255$ (foreground).

**Step 6 — Comment on the result:** **(1 mark)**

Because the center pixel intensity (130) is only marginally higher than the adaptive threshold (129), the resulting binary image will have **low contrast** at this location. In practice, this means the boundary between foreground and background will be **subtle and may be lost during morphological processing**. To make the segmentation more robust, one should either **increase $C$** (which would raise the threshold to 134, correctly classifying the 130-intensity pixel as background) or **use a larger window** for a more stable local mean estimate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to subtract $C$** — many students compute the local mean but forget the offset $C$. This loses 1–2 marks. **(Most Common Mistake)**
> - **Using the wrong formula** — confusing adaptive mean (arithmetic average) with adaptive Gaussian (weighted average). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> - **Not specifying window size implications** — if you do not discuss the trade-off of $w$ (large vs small), you lose 1 mark.
> - **Punctuation errors in formulas** — using $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell breaks the table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ in proper LaTeX display mode instead.
> - **Forgetting units** — intensities are in the 0–255 range; window sizes are in pixels. Always state units.
> - **Not explaining the role of $C$** — this is a 2-mark item in most KTU valuation schemes.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam — Adaptive Thresholding**

- **Definition:** Adaptive thresholding is a **local** binarization technique where $T(x,y)$ varies per pixel based on a $w \times w$ neighborhood.
- **Two Main Methods:** (1) Adaptive Mean, (2) Adaptive Gaussian.
- **Core Equation:** $T(x,y) = \mathcal{F}(W_{x,y}) - C$, where $\mathcal{F}$ is the local statistic.
- **Adaptive Mean Formula:** $T(x,y) = \frac{1}{w^2}\sum I(x+i,y+j) - C$
- **Adaptive Gaussian Formula:** $T(x,y) = \frac{\sum w(i,j)\cdot I(x+i,y+j)}{\sum w(i,j)} - C$, with $w(i,j) = e^{-(i^2+j^2)/(2\sigma^2)}$
- **Hyperparameters:** Window size $w$ (odd, e.g., 3, 5, 7, 11, 15, 21, 31) and offset $C$ (typical: 2–10).
- **Effect of $w$:** Larger $w$ → smoother thresholds, better noise suppression, loss of fine detail. Smaller $w$ → preserves detail, more noise-sensitive.
- **Effect of $C$:** Higher $C$ → more aggressive (more pixels become background). Lower $C$ → more pixels become foreground.
- **Best Use Case:** Real-world robot vision with **non-uniform illumination** (shadows, glare, vignetting).
- **Worst Use Case:** Images with **uniform lighting and bimodal histograms** — global Otsu is better.
- **Complexity:** $O(M \cdot N \cdot w^2)$ per frame, which is real-time feasible for $w \le 15$.
- **Numerical Practice:** Be ready to compute both mean and Gaussian-weighted thresholds for a given $3 \times 3$ or $5 \times 5$ patch.
- **Why $w$ is always odd:** Ensures the window has a unique center pixel at integer coordinates.
- **Key Distinction:** Adaptive thresholding is **not** the same as multi-level thresholding (which uses multiple fixed thresholds globally).
- **Standard Library Reference:** OpenCV's `cv2.adaptiveThreshold()` implements both methods with `ADAPTIVE_THRESH_MEAN_C` and `ADAPTIVE_THRESH_GAUSSIAN_C` flags.
- **Post-Processing:** Always pair adaptive thresholding with morphological operations (erosion/dilation) and Gaussian pre-filtering to remove salt-and-pepper noise.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state the binarization decision rule explicitly in the derivation to secure the final comparison mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Camera and Epipolar Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Camera Geometry

**Camera Geometry** is the mathematical framework that describes how 3-dimensional points in the physical world are projected onto a 2-dimensional image plane through a camera. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is the study of the pinhole/lens-based imaging model, expressed as a set of intrinsic (internal) and extrinsic (external) parameters that together form the **Camera Calibration Matrix (3×3)** or **Projection Matrix (3×4)**.

The geometric model is built upon the **Pinhole Camera Model**, where light rays from a scene pass through an infinitesimally small aperture and form an inverted image on a planar sensor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Camera geometry establishes the *forward projection equation*:
> $$\mathbf{x} = K \, [R \mid \mathbf{t}] \, \mathbf{X}$$
> where $K$ is the intrinsic matrix, $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is the extrinsic matrix, and $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{X}$ are homogeneous image and world coordinates.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Dark Room Camera Obscura"

Imagine you are standing inside a **completely dark shoebox** with a tiny pinhole on one face. The scene outside casts an inverted picture on the opposite inner wall. Now imagine *freezing* that wall and adding a coordinate grid. The math describing *where* a tree, *where* a car, and *where* the sun land on that grid — given the *position* of the box, the *size* of the wall, the *focal length* of the hole, and any *tilt* of the box — **is exactly camera geometry**. The pinhole is the "center of projection" $\mathbf{C}$, the wall is the "image plane" $\Pi$, and the scene is the 3D world $\mathbb{R}^3$.

## 1.2 Epipolar Geometry

**Epipolar Geometry** is the intrinsic projective geometry between **two views** of the same scene captured by two cameras (or the same camera in two positions). It describes the *geometric relationship* between the two image planes, independent of the scene, and is encoded algebraically by the **Fundamental Matrix $\mathbf{F}$** (3×3, rank 2) for uncalibrated cameras, or the **Essential Matrix $\mathbf{E}$** (3×3, rank 2) for calibrated cameras.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Five Cardinal Entities of Epipolar Geometry:**
> 1. **Baseline** — the line joining the two camera centers $\mathbf{C}_1$ and $\mathbf{C}_2$.
> 2. **Epipole** $\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2$ — the projection of one camera center onto the other camera's image plane.
> 3. **Epipolar Plane** $\pi$ — the plane containing the baseline and a 3D world point $\mathbf{X}$.
> 4. **Epipolar Line** $\mathbf{l}_1, \mathbf{l}_2$ — the intersection of the epipolar plane with each image plane.
> 5. **Fundamental/Essential Matrix** — the algebraic encoding of all the above.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stereo Movie Set"

Close one eye, look at your fingertip. Now close that eye and open the other. Your fingertip appears to *shift* against the background. The set of *all possible positions* your fingertip could have been in 3D space, consistent with the projection in your first eye, all lie on a single line in the second eye's view — that line is the **epipolar line**. Your brain uses this constraint to perform stereo fusion; a robot vision system uses the **fundamental matrix** to do the same job algorithmically.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in robot vision:** Given a single point match $\mathbf{x}_1 \leftrightarrow \mathbf{x}_2$, the epipolar constraint instantly restricts the matching of *all other points* in the second image to a 1-D search along the epipolar line, reducing complexity from 2-D to 1-D — a massive speedup for stereo correspondence algorithms.

## 1.3 Why Camera + Epipolar Geometry Live Together

| Aspect | Camera Geometry | Epipolar Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of views** | Single view | Two views |
| **Primary object** | Projection matrix $\mathbf{P}$ | Fundamental / Essential matrix |
| **Unknowns** | Intrinsic + extrinsic of **one** camera | Relative pose of **two** cameras |
| **Constraint type** | Forward projection $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{X}$ | Cross-view constraint $\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0$ |
| **Application** | Calibration, 3D reconstruction setup | Stereo matching, triangulation, structure-from-motion |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pinhole Camera Model — Operational Breakdown

A 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z, 1)^T$ (homogeneous) is projected to a 2D image point $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, 1)^T$ via the following **four-stage pipeline**:

**Stage 1 — Rigid Body Transform (World → Camera Frame).**
The world point is rotated and translated into the camera's own coordinate frame using the extrinsic matrix:
$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
where $R \in SO(3)$ is a **3×3 rotation matrix** and $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is the **translation vector** from world origin to camera center $\mathbf{C}$.

**Stage 2 — Perspective Projection (Camera Frame → Normalized Image Plane).**
Using similar triangles, the 3D point $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ projects to the *normalized* image plane at $Z_c = 1$:
$$x_n = \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y_n = \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

**Stage 3 — Intrinsic Parameter Transform (Normalized → Pixel Coordinates).**
The normalized coordinates are scaled by the focal length $f$ (in pixels) and offset by the principal point $(c_x, c_y)$:
$$x = f \cdot x_n + c_x = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$$
$$y = f \cdot y_n + c_y = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

**Stage 4 — Skew and Non-square Pixels (Refined Intrinsics).**
Allowing for skew $\gamma$ and possibly different focal lengths $f_x, f_y$ along axes:
$$x = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + \gamma \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad y = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

## 2.2 Intrinsic and Extrinsic Matrices

**Intrinsic Matrix $K$ (3×3):**
$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

In most modern cameras, $\gamma \approx 0$, so $K$ has 5 degrees of freedom: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$ (and skew if non-zero).

**Extrinsic Matrix $[R \mid \mathbf{t}]$ (3×4):** Has 6 degrees of freedom (3 rotation, 3 translation).

**Full Projection Matrix (3×4):**
$$\mathbf{P} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{r}_1 & \mathbf{r}_2 & \mathbf{r}_3 & \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.3 Lens Distortion Model

Real lenses deviate from the ideal pinhole. Two main radial distortion coefficients $k_1, k_2$ and tangential $p_1, p_2$ are commonly used:
$$x_d = x_n (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4) + 2 p_1 x_n y_n + p_2 (r^2 + 2 x_n^2)$$
$$y_d = y_n (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4) + p_1 (r^2 + 2 y_n^2) + 2 p_2 x_n y_n$$
where $r^2 = x_n^2 + y_n^2$.

## 2.4 Epipolar Geometry — Operational Breakdown

**Step 1 — Define two cameras.** Camera 1 with projection $\mathbf{P}_1 = K_1 [I \mid \mathbf{0}]$; Camera 2 with $\mathbf{P}_2 = K_2 [R \mid \mathbf{t}]$.

**Step 2 — World point $\mathbf{X}$ projects as:** $\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{P}_1 \mathbf{X}$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{P}_2 \mathbf{X}$.

**Step 3 — The epipolar plane** is spanned by the baseline $\mathbf{C}_2 - \mathbf{C}_1$ and the 3D point $\mathbf{X}$. Its intersection with the image plane of camera 2 yields the **epipolar line** $\mathbf{l}_2$.

**Step 4 — Algebraic form:** The epipolar constraint is
$$\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0$$
where $\mathbf{F}$ is the **Fundamental Matrix**.

**Step 5 — Calibrated case:** When both cameras are calibrated, we can express everything in *normalized* coordinates $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = K^{-1} \mathbf{x}$, and the constraint becomes
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_2^T \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_1 = 0$$
where $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times R$ is the **Essential Matrix** and $[\mathbf{t}]_\times$ is the **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix** of $\mathbf{t}$.

## 2.5 Properties of $\mathbf{F}$ and $\mathbf{E}$

| Property | Fundamental Matrix $\mathbf{F}$ | Essential Matrix $\mathbf{E}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Size | $3 \times 3$ | $3 \times 3$ |
| Rank | **2** | **2** |
| DOF | 7 (since $\det \mathbf{F} = 0$ and scale-ambiguous) | 5 (3 for $R$, 2 for $\mathbf{t}$ up to scale) |
| Calibrated required? | No | Yes (uses $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = K^{-1} \mathbf{x}$) |
| Relationship | $\mathbf{E} = K_2^T \mathbf{F} K_1$ | $\mathbf{F} = K_2^{-T} \mathbf{E} K_1^{-1}$ |
| Epipole retrieval | $\mathbf{F} \mathbf{e}_1 = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{e}_2 = \mathbf{0}$ | $\mathbf{E} \mathbf{e}_1 = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{E}^T \mathbf{e}_2 = \mathbf{0}$ |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-memorize formulas for KTU ESE Module 4 (Camera & Epipolar Geometry).** No pipe symbol inside cells — use $\vert$ / $\mid$ instead.

| $\#$ | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x} = K [R \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{X}$ | Full forward projection (world → pixel) |
| 2 | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic matrix |
| 3 | $\mathbf{P} = K [R \mid \mathbf{t}]$ | Camera projection matrix (3×4) |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (uncalibrated) |
| 5 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_2^T \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_1 = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (calibrated) |
| 6 | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times R$ | Essential matrix from pose |
| 7 | $\mathbf{F} = K_2^{-T} [\mathbf{t}]_\times R \, K_1^{-1}$ | Fundamental matrix from pose |
| 8 | $\mathbf{E} = K_2^T \mathbf{F} K_1$ | Converting $\mathbf{F} \to \mathbf{E}$ |
| 9 | $\mathbf{l}_2 = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_1$ | Epipolar line in image 2 given $\mathbf{x}_1$ |
| 10 | $\mathbf{l}_1 = \mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{x}_2$ | Epipolar line in image 1 given $\mathbf{x}_2$ |
| 11 | $\mathbf{F} \mathbf{e}_1 = \mathbf{0}$ | Epipole in image 1 |
| 12 | $\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{e}_2 = \mathbf{0}$ | Epipole in image 2 |
| 13 | $\text{rank}(\mathbf{F}) = 2$ | $\det \mathbf{F} = 0$ — singular |
| 14 | $[\mathbf{t}]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | Skew-symmetric cross-product form |
| 15 | $\text{DOF}(\mathbf{P}) = 11$ | Total DOF of camera (5 intrinsic + 6 extrinsic) |
| 16 | $\text{DOF}(\mathbf{F}) = 7$ | Fundamental matrix DOF |
| 17 | $\text{DOF}(\mathbf{E}) = 5$ | Essential matrix DOF |
| 18 | $x_d = x_n (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4) + \dots$ | Radial + tangential distortion |
| 19 | $\mathbf{X} = \text{triangulate}(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{P}_1, \mathbf{P}_2)$ | Recover 3D point from two views |
| 20 | Eight-point algorithm | Minimum 8 correspondences to estimate $\mathbf{F}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Stereo camera rigs estimate obstacle depth using $\mathbf{F}$ for rectification (so epipolar lines become horizontal), enabling real-time dense depth maps.
- **Robotic Manipulation:** A robot arm with an eye-in-hand camera uses $\mathbf{P}$ to map pixel coordinates to end-effector workspace coordinates.
- **AR/VR Headsets:** SLAM systems recover $\mathbf{F}$ between successive frames to estimate camera ego-motion and build 3D maps.
- **Medical Imaging (Endoscopy):** Calibrated endoscope cameras use $\mathbf{E}$ to recover 3D tissue geometry from 2D video for surgical planning.
