topics.title: Background Subtraction and Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT832 - IMAGE PROCESSING AND ROBOT VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Background Subtraction and Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Background Subtraction and Modelling** is a computational vision technique used to isolate moving foreground objects (regions of interest) from a relatively static background in a sequence of video frames. It involves constructing and maintaining a statistical or deterministic **Background Model** $B(x, y, t)$ for every pixel location $(x, y)$ at time $t$, and then comparing the incoming frame $I(x, y, t)$ against this model to classify each pixel as either foreground or background.

Mathematically, the foreground mask $F(x, y, t)$ is computed as:

$$
F(x, y, t) = 
\begin{cases} 
1 & \text{if } \vert I(x, y, t) - B(x, y, t) \vert > T \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $T$ is a predefined **threshold** value (typically between **20 and 50** in 8-bit grayscale images), and $F = 1$ denotes a foreground pixel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Background subtraction forms the **first stage** of nearly every video surveillance pipeline. Under the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this topic directly maps to **CO3 (Apply motion detection algorithms for robot vision applications)** and is essential for **Module 4: Motion Detection and Tracking**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a window in a coffee shop, watching people walk by on the street.

- The **street, footpath, lamp post, and parked cars** = **Background** (things that don't change or change very slowly).
- The **pedestrians, cyclists, and moving vehicles** = **Foreground** (things you actually want to detect).
- Your **brain** is essentially performing **Background Subtraction** — it has built an internal *mental model* of the empty street and flags anything that *deviates* from that model as "new and noteworthy."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it hard for a computer?**
> Unlike your brain, a computer must mathematically *learn* the empty scene. The street is never perfectly the same — clouds cast moving shadows, tree leaves sway, headlights flicker, and the sun rises. A robust background model must **adapt** to these slow "noises" while remaining **sensitive** to fast-moving objects.

### 1.3 Core Components of a Background Subtraction System

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Background Initialization** | Bootstraps the model $B$ from the first $N$ frames (typically $N = 50$ to $200$). |
| **Background Update** | Periodically refreshes $B(x, y, t)$ to absorb gradual illumination changes. |
| **Foreground Detection** | Compares incoming pixels to $B$ using distance metrics. |
| **Post-Processing** | Applies morphological filters to remove noise and fill holes. |
| **Shadow Suppression** | Eliminates cast shadows that would otherwise be misclassified as foreground. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The standard background subtraction pipeline executes the following steps for every incoming video frame:

1. **Acquire Frame** $I(x, y, t)$ from the camera sensor.
2. **Convert Color Space** (optional) — often transform from **RGB to HSV or YCrCb** to separate intensity from chromaticity. This improves robustness to shadows.
3. **Compare with Model** — for each pixel, compute the deviation $D(x, y, t) = I(x, y, t) - B(x, y, t)$.
4. **Apply Threshold** — if $\vert D \vert > T$, mark pixel as foreground.
5. **Update Model** — adaptively modify $B(x, y, t)$ using a learning rate $\alpha$.
6. **Morphological Cleanup** — apply erosion, dilation, opening, or closing.
7. **Connected Component Analysis** — group adjacent foreground pixels into blobs.
8. **Shadow Removal** — suppress pixels whose chromaticity is preserved but brightness is reduced.

### 2.2 Background Modelling Approaches

#### 2.2.1 Frame Differencing (Naïve Method)

The simplest possible model: the previous frame **is** the background.

$$
B(x, y, t) = I(x, y, t-1)
$$

> **Pros:** Zero memory overhead, zero training time.
> **Cons:** Fails when foreground objects become stationary; produces "ghost" detections and double-edges.

#### 2.2.2 Running (Moving) Average

The background pixel is a **linear combination** of the previous background and the current frame, controlled by the learning rate $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.

$$
B(x, y, t) = (1 - \alpha) \cdot B(x, y, t-1) + \alpha \cdot I(x, y, t)
$$

- If $\alpha$ is **large** (e.g., 0.1): fast adaptation but foreground bleeds into background.
- If $\alpha$ is **small** (e.g., 0.001): slow, clean background but slow to recover from sudden changes.

#### 2.2.3 Running Gaussian Average (Single Gaussian Model — Wren et al., 1997)

Each pixel is modelled by a **single Gaussian distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

$$
B(x, y, t) = \left\{ \mu(x, y, t), \sigma^2(x, y, t) \right\}
$$

Update rules:

$$
\mu(x, y, t) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \mu(x, y, t-1) + \rho \cdot I(x, y, t)
$$

$$
\sigma^2(x, y, t) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \sigma^2(x, y, t-1) + \rho \cdot \left[ I(x, y, t) - \mu(x, y, t) \right]^2
$$

where $\rho = \frac{\alpha}{\sigma^2(x, y, t-1)}$ is the **adaptive learning rate**.

A pixel is foreground if it lies outside $\pm k\sigma$ (typically $k = 2.5$).

#### 2.2.4 Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM — Stauffer & Grimson, 1999)

Each pixel is modelled as a mixture of $K$ Gaussians (typically $K = 3$ to $5$).

$$
P(X_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i \cdot \eta(X_t, \mu_i, \Sigma_i)
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight, $\mu_i$ the mean, $\Sigma_i$ the covariance, and $\eta$ the Gaussian PDF:

$$
\eta(X_t, \mu, \Sigma) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}(X_t - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (X_t - \mu)}
$$

The Gaussians are ranked by $\frac{w_i}{\sigma_i}$. The **top $B$** Gaussians are declared "background" where:

$$
B = \arg\min_b \left( \sum_{i=1}^{b} w_i > T_{\text{bg}} \right)
$$

with $T_{\text{bg}} \approx 0.7$.

#### 2.2.5 Kernel Density Estimation (KDE — Elgammal et al., 2000)

Non-parametric model that estimates the pixel intensity distribution using the **last $N$ samples** (typically $N = 100$ to $200$).

$$
P(I_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} K(I_t - I_{t-i})
$$

Using a Gaussian kernel with bandwidth $h$:

$$
P(I_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi h^2}} \, e^{-\frac{(I_t - I_{t-i})^2}{2h^2}}
$$

#### 2.2.6 Running Median

The background is the **median** of the last $N$ pixel values. Highly robust to outliers (a foreground object appearing in 1 of 50 frames won't corrupt the median).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Foreground Detection | $\vert I - B \vert > T$ | $I, B$: pixel intensity (0–255); $T$: scalar threshold (typical: **20–50**) | Foundation of every method |
| Running Average | $B_t = (1-\alpha) B_{t-1} + \alpha I_t$ | $\alpha$: learning rate (**0.001 to 0.1**) | Simple dynamic background |
| Single Gaussian | $\mu_t, \sigma_t^2$ updated via $\rho = \alpha/\sigma^2$ | $\sigma$: standard deviation; $\rho$: adaptive rate | Slow-changing scenes |
| GMM Mixture | $P(X) = \sum w_i \eta_i$ | $K$: number of Gaussians (**3–5**); $w_i \in [0,1]$, $\sum w_i = 1$ | Waving trees, fountains |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D^2 = (X - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (X - \mu)$ | Dimensionless squared distance | Match test in GMM |
| KDE Probability | $P(I_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi h^2}} e^{-\frac{(I_t - I_{t-i})^2}{2h^2}}$ | $N$: history length (**100–200**); $h$: bandwidth | Multimodal backgrounds |
| Shadow Detection (HSV) | $\alpha \leq \frac{V_{\text{fg}}}{V_{\text{bg}}} \leq \beta \quad \land \quad \vert H_{\text{fg}} - H_{\text{bg}} \vert \leq \tau_H$ | $\alpha \in [0.4, 0.6]$, $\beta \approx 0.9$ | Suppress cast shadows |
| Morphological Opening | $A \circ B = (A \ominus B) \oplus B$ | Erosion then dilation with structuring element $B$ | Remove salt noise |
| Morphological Closing | $A \bullet B = (A \oplus B) \ominus B$ | Dilation then erosion | Fill holes in blobs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Background subtraction is the **backbone of CCTV surveillance, traffic monitoring, autonomous vehicles (Tesla's perception stack), sports analytics (player tracking), and robotics obstacle avoidance (service robots like Pepper and ASIMO).** The **Stauffer–Grimson GMM** is implemented in OpenCV's `cv2.createBackgroundSubtractorMOG2()` and remains the most cited reference in the field.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Running Average Equivalence to Exponential Smoothing

We start with the recursive running-average update:

$$
B_t = (1 - \alpha) B_{t-1} + \alpha I_t \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $B_{t-1}$ using the same equation at time $t-1$:

$$
B_{t-1} = (1 - \alpha) B_{t-2} + \alpha I_{t-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Insert this into Equation (1):

$$
B_t = (1 - \alpha) \left[ (1 - \alpha) B_{t-2} + \alpha I_{t-1} \right] + \alpha I_t
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the outer multiplication:

$$
B_t = (1 - \alpha)^2 B_{t-2} + \alpha(1 - \alpha) I_{t-1} + \alpha I_t
$$

**Step 4:** Continue unrolling the recursion up to time $t = 0$ (the very first frame, when $B_0 = I_0$):

$$
B_t = (1 - \alpha)^t B_0 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{t} (1 - \alpha)^{t-i} I_i
$$

**Step 5:** Replace $B_0 = I_0$ and group terms:

$$
B_t = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t} (1 - \alpha)^{t-i} I_i = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{t} (1 - \alpha)^{t-i} I_i
$$

**Interpretation:** Each historical frame $I_i$ contributes to the current background with weight $\alpha (1 - \alpha)^{t-i}$. This is a **discrete exponential moving average (EMA)**. The effective memory horizon is $\tau = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ frames.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A board question may ask, *"What is the effective time constant of a running-average background model?"* The answer is $\tau \approx \frac{1}{\alpha}$. For $\alpha = 0.01$, the model "remembers" roughly **100 frames** into the past.

### 3.2 Derivation: Single Gaussian Update Rule from Maximum Likelihood

Suppose the pixel value $X$ is drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The likelihood of observing $N$ samples is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \, e^{-\frac{(X_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}
$$

**Step 1:** Take the log-likelihood:

$$
\ln \mathcal{L} = -\frac{N}{2} \ln(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2} \ln(\sigma^2) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(X_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \ln \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(X_i - \mu)}{\sigma^2} = 0 \implies \mu^{*} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i
$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \ln \mathcal{L}}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(X_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^4} = 0 \implies \sigma^{*2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Convert the batch MLE into an online recursive form. For each new sample $X_t$, assign it a learning weight $\rho$:

$$
\mu_t = (1 - \rho) \mu_{t-1} + \rho X_t
$$

$$
\sigma_t^2 = (1 - \rho) \sigma_{t-1}^2 + \rho (X_t - \mu_t)^2
$$

These are **exactly the running-Gaussian update rules** used in OpenCV's `BackgroundSubtractorMOG` (the predecessor to MOG2).

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Numerical Background Subtraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style numerical:** Given a pixel $(x, y)$ whose intensity history over 5 frames is $\{I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5\} = \{100, 102, 98, 150, 105\}$ and a learning rate $\alpha = 0.2$, with initial $B_0 = 100$, compute the running average background at $t = 5$. Use threshold $T = 20$ and classify each frame.

**Step 1 — Initialize:** $B_0 = 100$.

**Step 2 — $t=1$:** 

$$
B_1 = (1 - 0.2) \cdot 100 + 0.2 \cdot 100 = 100.00
$$

**Step 3 — $t=2$:**

$$
B_2 = 0.8 \cdot 100 + 0.2 \cdot 102 = 100.40
$$

**Step 4 — $t=3$:**

$$
B_3 = 0.8 \cdot 100.40 + 0.2 \cdot 98 = 99.92
$$

**Step 5 — $t=4$:**

$$
B_4 = 0.8 \cdot 99.92 + 0.2 \cdot 150 = 109.94
$$

**Step 6 — $t=5$:**

$$
B_5 = 0.8 \cdot 109.94 + 0.2 \cdot 105 = 108.95
$$

**Step 7 — Foreground Classification:** The deviation at $t=4$ is $\vert 150 - 99.92 \vert = 50.08 > 20$ → **Foreground** (a moving object passed by). All other frames are background. This illustrates how a transient spike (e.g., a passing white car) is correctly flagged and then *gradually absorbed* back into the background as $\alpha$ dilutes its weight.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Method | Memory | Computation | Multimodal? | Adapts to Illumination? | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frame Differencing | Very Low | Very Low | No | Weak | Static camera, simple motion |
| Running Average | Low | Low | No | Moderate | Slow-changing scenes |
| Running Median | Medium | Medium | Weak | Moderate | Indoor surveillance |
| Single Gaussian | Low | Low | No | Good | Controlled lighting |
| **GMM (Stauffer–Grimson)** | Medium | High | **Yes** | **Excellent** | **Outdoor with swaying trees** |
| KDE (Elgammal) | High | Very High | Yes | Good | Research-grade, complex textures |
| Deep Learning (e.g., SegNet) | Very High | GPU only | Yes | Excellent | Modern autonomous driving |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Background Subtraction Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Video Input I xyt] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Color Space Conversion RGB to HSV]
    B --> B2[Gaussian Smoothing]
    B1 --> C[Background Model Bxyt]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Subtraction Operation]
    A --> D
    D --> E[Thresholding T]
    E --> F{Is Diff greater than T}
    F -->|Yes| G[Foreground Mask F 1]
    F -->|No| H[Background Mask F 0]
    G --> I[Shadow Detection Module]
    H --> J[Background Model Update]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output Foreground Blob Map]
    K --> L[Connected Component Labelling]
    L --> M[Tracking Module Next Stage]
```

### 4.2 GMM Internal Architecture (Per-Pixel State Machine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[New Pixel Value Xt] --> M{Match Test}
    M -->|Match Found| U1[Update Weight wi]
    U1 --> U2[Update Mean Mui]
    U2 --> U3[Update Variance Sigmai]
    U3 --> R[Recompute Ratio wi over Sigmai]
    R --> S[Re-sort Gaussians]
    M -->|No Match| N[Replace Weakest Gaussian]
    N --> U4[Initialize with Mui = Xt]
    U4 --> U5[Set high variance]
    U5 --> S
    S --> B{Top B Gaussians}
    B -->|Background| BG[Mark as Background]
    B -->|Remaining| FG[Mark as Foreground]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Pixel Classification (Stauffer–Grimson)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Pixel Xt at time t]) --> Init[Has model been initialized]
    Init -->|No| Boot[Bootstrap from first N frames]
    Init -->|Yes| Loop{K check Gaussian 1 to K}
    Loop -->|Yes| Dist{Distance less than 2.5 times Sigma}
    Dist -->|Yes| Hit[Update matched Gaussian]
    Hit --> Done([End classification])
    Dist -->|No| Loop
    Loop -->|No| Replace[Replace least-weight Gaussian]
    Replace --> Rank[Rank by w over Sigma]
    Rank --> Pick[Select top B as background]
    Pick --> Final{Pixel matches background Gaussian}
    Final -->|Yes| MarkBG[Mark Background]
    Final -->|No| MarkFG[Mark Foreground]
    MarkBG --> Done
    MarkFG --> Done
    Boot --> Done
```

### 4.4 Shadow Suppression Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Foreground Pixel Candidate] --> B[Extract HSV channels]
    B --> C[Compute brightness ratio alpha = Vfg over Vbg]
    B --> D[Compute hue difference dH]
    C --> E{alpha in range 0.4 to 0.9}
    D --> F{dH less than tauH}
    E -->|Yes| G[AND]
    E -->|No| H[Keep as Foreground]
    F -->|Yes| G
    F -->|No| H
    G --> I[Classify as Shadow]
    I --> J[Suppress from output]
    H --> K[Pass to Tracking]
    J --> K
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define background subtraction. List any three challenges faced in background modelling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Background subtraction is a computer vision technique that detects moving objects (foreground) by computing the difference between the current video frame and a reference background model, then applying a threshold.

$$
F(x, y, t) = 1 \text{ if } \vert I(x, y, t) - B(x, y, t) \vert > T
$$

**Three challenges (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Illumination changes** — gradual (sunset) or sudden (cloud cover, lights on/off).
2. **Shadows** — cast shadows alter pixel intensity and may be falsely detected as foreground.
3. **Dynamic backgrounds** — swaying trees, water ripples, flickering monitors create multimodal intensity distributions.

*(Alternative answers: bootstrapping problem, foreground aperture, camouflage, camera jitter.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the role of the learning rate $\alpha$ in the running average background model? What happens if $\alpha$ is too high or too low?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The learning rate $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ controls the trade-off between **adaptation speed** and **noise immunity** in the recursive update:

$$
B_t = (1 - \alpha) B_{t-1} + \alpha I_t
$$

- If $\alpha$ is **too high** (close to 1): $B_t \approx I_t$, so the model becomes equivalent to frame differencing; foreground objects pollute the background and "ghosting" occurs.
- If $\alpha$ is **too low** (close to 0): $B_t$ barely changes, leading to a stale background that cannot adapt to illumination drift or scene changes; also slow recovery from foreground-absorbed states.

**Typical engineering value:** $\alpha \in [0.001, 0.01]$ for outdoor surveillance, $\alpha \in [0.05, 0.1]$ for indoor dynamic scenes.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and **must** contain two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. KTU 2024 scheme mandates an **internal choice** between two full-length questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** based background subtraction method proposed by Stauffer and Grimson. Derive the match-test condition and weight-update rule.

**(b) [7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete background subtraction pipeline including shadow detection and morphological post-processing.

---

**Model Answer — (a) [7 marks]:**

**1. Core Idea (1 mark):** Each pixel $(x, y)$ is modelled as a **mixture of $K$ Gaussian distributions** $\mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \Sigma_i)$ with weights $w_i$ summing to 1. This handles **multimodal backgrounds** such as waving trees or flickering monitors, where a single Gaussian is insufficient.

**2. The Mixture Model (1 mark):**

$$
P(X_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i \cdot \eta(X_t, \mu_i, \Sigma_i), \quad \eta(X_t, \mu, \Sigma) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(X_t-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1}(X_t-\mu)\right)
$$

**3. Match Test (1.5 marks):** A pixel $X_t$ is said to **match** the $i$-th Gaussian if its Mahalanobis distance is below a threshold (typically $k = 2.5$):

$$
(X_t - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (X_t - \mu_i) < k^2
$$

**4. Update Rule (1.5 marks):** For the matched Gaussian $i$:

$$
w_i \leftarrow (1 - \alpha) w_i + \alpha
$$

$$
\mu_i \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \mu_i + \rho X_t
$$

$$
\sigma_i^2 \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \sigma_i^2 + \rho (X_t - \mu_i)^T (X_t - \mu_i)
$$

For unmatched Gaussians, only $w_i \leftarrow (1 - \alpha) w_i$ is applied; $\mu$ and $\sigma$ remain unchanged. If no Gaussian matches, the **least-weighted** Gaussian is replaced by a new one with $\mu = X_t$, large $\sigma$, and small $w$.

**5. Background Selection (1 mark):** Sort all $K$ Gaussians by $\frac{w_i}{\sigma_i}$ in descending order. The first $B$ distributions covering a fraction $T_{\text{bg}}$ of the total weight are chosen as background:

$$
B = \arg\min_b \left( \sum_{i=1}^{b} w_i > T_{\text{bg}} \right), \quad T_{\text{bg}} \approx 0.7
$$

**6. Classification (1 mark):** A pixel is **foreground** if it matches none of the background Gaussians; otherwise it is **background**.

---

**Model Answer — (b) [7 marks]:**

The pipeline is described by the **block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). The textual explanation:

1. **Frame Acquisition (0.5 mark):** Capture frame $I_t$ from the camera.
2. **Preprocessing (1 mark):** Apply Gaussian smoothing ($\sigma = 1$ to $2$) to suppress sensor noise. Optionally convert to HSV colour space to decouple intensity from chromaticity.
3. **Background Model Storage (0.5 mark):** Maintain $B(x, y, t)$ — either a single image (frame difference) or per-pixel statistics (GMM parameters).
4. **Subtraction + Threshold (1 mark):** Compute $|I_t - B_t|$ and threshold to produce a binary mask.
5. **Morphological Post-Processing (1.5 marks):** Apply **opening** (erosion $\rightarrow$ dilation) to remove salt-noise speckles, and **closing** (dilation $\rightarrow$ erosion) to fill small holes inside the foreground blobs. A typical $3 \times 3$ structuring element suffices.
6. **Shadow Detection (1.5 marks):** For each foreground pixel, evaluate the **HSV shadow test**:
   - Brightness ratio: $\alpha = \frac{V_{\text{fg}}}{V_{\text{bg}}}$, must satisfy $\alpha \in [\alpha_{\min}, \alpha_{\max}]$, e.g., $[0.4, 0.9]$.
   - Hue difference: $\vert H_{\text{fg}} - H_{\text{bg}} \vert \leq \tau_H$, e.g., $\tau_H = 0.5$.
   - Saturation difference: $\vert S_{\text{fg}} - S_{\text{bg}} \vert \leq \tau_S$.
   - Pixels satisfying all three are re-labelled as **shadow** and suppressed.
7. **Output (1 mark):** A clean, shadow-free binary foreground mask, ready for connected-component labelling and tracking.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Running Gaussian Average** method for background modelling. Derive the update equations for $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ from the maximum-likelihood principle.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the **Running Average**, **Single Gaussian**, and **GMM** methods using a comparative table. State the limitations of each.

---

**Model Answer — (a) [7 marks]:**

**1. Concept (1 mark):** Proposed by Wren et al. (1997), this method models each pixel intensity as a **single Gaussian distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, with parameters updated recursively over time. It assumes the background is **unimodal** and slowly varying.

**2. Likelihood Function (1 mark):** Assuming $N$ i.i.d. samples:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(X_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**3. MLE for $\mu$ (1 mark):** Taking the derivative of $\ln \mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $\mu$ and setting it to zero:

$$
\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i
$$

**4. MLE for $\sigma^2$ (1 mark):** Differentiating w.r.t. $\sigma^2$:

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (X_i - \hat{\mu})^2
$$

**5. Online Recursive Form (2 marks):** To make the estimator online and adaptive, introduce a learning rate $\rho$:

$$
\mu_t = (1 - \rho) \mu_{t-1} + \rho X_t
$$

$$
\sigma_t^2 = (1 - \rho) \sigma_{t-1}^2 + \rho (X_t - \mu_t)^2
$$

Often, $\rho = \alpha / \sigma_{t-1}$ is used for **adaptive** learning.

**6. Foreground Classification (1 mark):** A pixel is foreground if:

$$
\vert X_t - \mu_t \vert > k \cdot \sigma_t, \quad k = 2.5
$$

---

**Model Answer — (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Running Average | Single Gaussian | GMM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model Type | Deterministic (no distribution) | Parametric (1 Gaussian) | Parametric (K Gaussians) |
| Parameters Stored | 1 ($B$) per pixel | 2 ($\mu, \sigma$) per pixel | $3K$ ($w, \mu, \sigma$) per pixel |
| Multimodal Backgrounds | ❌ Fails | ❌ Fails | ✅ Handles via $K > 1$ |
| Shadows | Poor | Moderate | Good (with HSV test) |
| Slow Illumination Drift | Good | Good | Good |
| Sudden Illumination Change | Poor | Poor | Moderate (with reset logic) |
| Computational Cost | Very Low | Low | Moderate–High |
| Memory Footprint | Minimal | Low | $K \times$ larger |
| Best For | Static indoor | Controlled lighting | Outdoor dynamic |

**Limitations (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

- **Running Average:** Cannot represent multimodal backgrounds; one foreground object can permanently corrupt the model if $\alpha$ is large; slow recovery from "foreground absorption."
- **Single Gaussian:** Assumes unimodal distribution; fails for waving trees, water surfaces, or any periodic background motion; sensitive to outliers.
- **GMM:** Higher memory and computation; sensitive to initial parameters; requires careful choice of $K$, $\alpha$, and $T_{\text{bg}}$; may incorrectly classify slow foreground objects (e.g., a stopped car) as background after several frames.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "background model" with "background image":** Many students write $B(x,y)$ as a single static image. KTU examiners expect you to know that *modern* methods like GMM store **statistical parameters** (mean, variance, weights), not pixel values.
> 2. **Forgetting the Mahalanobis distance:** In the GMM match test, using the simple Euclidean distance $\vert X_t - \mu_i \vert$ instead of $(X_t - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (X_t - \mu_i)$ costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" behind shadow detection:** Simply stating "shadows are removed" loses marks. You must explain **which HSV properties** distinguish shadows (low brightness, preserved hue, low saturation change).
> 4. **No numerical example:** If the question permits, a small worked example (as in Section 3.3) earns **2 bonus marks** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\rho$:** $\alpha$ is the **weight learning rate**; $\rho = \alpha / \sigma^2$ is the **adaptive mean learning rate**. Mixing them up is a **favourite trick** of KTU paper-setters.
> 6. **Omitting post-processing:** A raw thresholded mask is *never* the final output. Always mention **morphological opening/closing** and **shadow suppression** to get full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

- ✅ **Definition:** Background subtraction isolates moving foreground by computing $\vert I_t - B_t \vert > T$.
- ✅ **Pipeline Order:** Acquire → Preprocess → Compare → Threshold → Morphology → Shadow Suppress → Track.
- ✅ **Running Average Update:** $B_t = (1-\alpha)B_{t-1} + \alpha I_t$; time constant $\tau = 1/\alpha$.
- ✅ **Single Gaussian:** One $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ per pixel; foreground if $\vert X - \mu \vert > 2.5\sigma$.
- ✅ **GMM:** $K$ Gaussians per pixel (typical $K = 3$–$5$); ranked by $w_i / \sigma_i$; top $B$ are background; $T_{\text{bg}} \approx 0.7$.
- ✅ **Match Test:** $(X_t - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (X_t - \mu_i) < 2.5^2$.
- ✅ **KDE:** Non-parametric; uses last $N$ samples with Gaussian kernel of bandwidth $h$.
- ✅ **Running Median:** Robust to outliers; useful when foreground is sparse.
- ✅ **Shadow Detection (HSV):** Brightness drop $0.4 \le V_{\text{fg}}/V_{\text{bg}} \le 0.9$, hue preserved, saturation close.
- ✅ **Morphology:** Opening = erosion $\rightarrow$ dilation (removes noise); Closing = dilation $\rightarrow$ erosion (fills holes).
- ✅ **Challenges:** Illumination changes, shadows, dynamic backgrounds, bootstrapping, foreground aperture, camouflage.
- ✅ **Modern Trends:** Deep learning (SegNet, U-Net) now outperforms GMM in benchmarks, but GMM is still asked in KTU 2024 syllabus.
- ✅ **OpenCV Implementations:** `cv2.createBackgroundSubtractorMOG2()`, `cv2.createBackgroundSubtractorKNN()`.
- ✅ **Key Citations:** Stauffer & Grimson (1999) — GMM; Wren et al. (1997) — Adaptive Background; Elgammal et al. (2000) — KDE.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain any background modelling method," **always** state (i) the model equation, (ii) the update rule, (iii) the foreground classification criterion, and (iv) one real-world application. This four-part structure maps to the KTU **Bloom's Taxonomy L3–L4** marking scheme and guarantees full credit.
